###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL Lite

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39878A26.7684B62A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de> <3981AD0A.97778F9E@zip.com.au> <8lsc92$c49$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <398238DC.1350C747@zip.com.au>`

```

Topic was : : OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood? I hate all this
cross-referencing.
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> extra features.   Any one for a OO cobol lite standard for GNU?

No takers to Ken's question ? Let's broaden it a little :-

From existing versions of COBOL '68, '74, '85, '85.5 and '97, what would
you like to see retained, what features from COBOL 200X do you want to
see added, those which should be dropped, others which haven't been
included, (even though you may mention 'additions' at this stage -
consider yourself SOL !). So let's have a shopping list from people that
care.

- Judson - you bemoan the Standards - where's your shopping list.
- Lief - do you want it frozen at COBOL '74 for portability
- Howard - Where's your shopping list BUT JUST remember, in your list,
there are things called PCs.

Any other takers, Wiiliam II, Ib, Cheesle, on and on......... ?

In response to Ken's question we can narrow down 'OO COBOL Lite' based
on your comments about 'Structured COBOL Lite'.

Jimmy
```

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>

- **References:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de> <3981AD0A.97778F9E@zip.com.au> <8lsc92$c49$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <398238DC.1350C747@zip.com.au> <39878A26.7684B62A@home.com>`

```
Jimmy has suggested something worth while.  It could be very productive,
and
IMHO should be directed to a higher level.

One suggestion would be to convert this
request to a general call and ask ComputerWorld publish it as well as
others.

I realize that this opens up a can of worms. However, the effort is worth
it if enough input can be gathered.  Perhaps the current standard proposals
location should be included.  It might catch the eyes of some people who
like doing research, and they  might provide a site to collect, sort, and
summarize the input.
If you don't ask, don't complain.

Warren Simmons
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:39878A26.7684B62A@home.com...

there

> >
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…

ago.  The following is the LENGTHY list of all the "enhancements" in the
current revision (well as of a couple weeks ago - before the latest J4
meeting).

keep from the current Standard - but I'll leave that work to others).

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:39878A26.7684B62A@home.com...
> - Judson - you bemoan the Standards - where's your shopping list.
> - Lief - do you want it frozen at COBOL '74 for portability
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any other takers, Wiiliam II, Ib, Cheesle, on and on......... ?

Well, that was no small topic. I can't figure how to provide such a
statement without sitting down for a couple of days and think. Generally
speaking I would say I am pretty satisfied with what is there, if I should
whish for something, some basic OO could be interesting, but then in the
context that I for instance would like to be able to access stuff like SOAP.

On the other side, who is the right person to define an OO light? What is
that? I think it is a dangerous topic....

Two features I DO know I have missed is character pointers and not to forget
local storage to Sections. Then again, to understand my wish for local
storage in Sections, one have to program my way. Yeah, I know you American
think I am crazy doing Sections like it should be sort of C functions, but
that's the way I am :-)

No further comments.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Lite

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39913B6E.AA124A3C@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de> <3981AD0A.97778F9E@zip.com.au> <8lsc92$c49$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <398238DC.1350C747@zip.com.au> <39878A26.7684B62A@home.com>`

```
2000-08-09 13:11:31 MEST

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> ...

> No takers to Ken's question ? Let's broaden it a little :-
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> on your comments about 'Structured COBOL Lite'.

In the old days when I worked in an Ada (83) compiler project,
I had the idea of "Ada-lite". Interesting enough, it did not
catch on, for whatever cause.

In the case of Ada, some subsets could have been defined
very easily. For instance, you cold take "tasking" away and
still have a genuine subset. For other things to be
excluded, the situation was not that simple. Generics, for
instance, leave their trace in the simplest "Hello world"
program, so there was no subsetting possible there.

Now there might be one reason why the idea did not catch on:
Ada was entirely new. No bunch of old programmers who were
brought up in Ada. No one to ask how and which things were
useful in everyday work.

This of course is different with Cobol where we have the bunch
of old programmers - some of them even, which is essential,
familiar with many plattforms. The expertise of defining a
useful hierarchy of language-subsets is there.

However, that is additional work. Look at the CD 1.8 - it
approaches in length the bible, and the time of completing
the draft has a tendency to move forward in time. All the
thinking about making the language free of contradictions
etc had to be repeated for every language subset. Who shall
do all that work?

Maybe that is (one of ) the reason(s) that there never will be
a subset to any new version of Cobol.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Lite

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00

## Thread: faa machine — 5 msgs

### faa machine

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5jaeossgf512eovi85l5i8lf3ltf555o6g@4ax.com>`

```
I have heard the faa machine is a mainframe and they are having
trouble upgrading it, and was wondering if anyone knew of some
articles or code from it (is it ok under freedom of info act, or is it
one of those top secret things)?
reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: faa machine

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m7tnn$1o19$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<5jaeossgf512eovi85l5i8lf3ltf555o6g@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore wrote in message <5jaeossgf512eovi85l5i8lf3ltf555o6g@4ax.com>...
>I have heard the faa machine is a mainframe and they are having
>trouble upgrading it, and was wondering if anyone knew of some
>articles or code from it (is it ok under freedom of info act, or is it
>one of those top secret things)?
>reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam

The FAA has many systems which use mainframes. The system
that has proven difficult to upgrade is the Air Traffic Control system.
The new system was/is called Advanced Automation System (AAS).

AAS was initiated in 1983. IBM FSC won the bid and started in
1988. The system was to be implemented on distributed computers
using both 390 and RS/6000 computers. The SLOC estimate was
2.4 million, of which 1.8 million was Ada and much of the rest C++.
(FSC was later sold to Loral.)

In 1994, the AAS project was restructured. More than half the
original project was cancelled. Most of what remained was the
workstation software for the air traffic controllers. (For $7.6B,
they should have pretty displays to show that all was not wasted!)

Some of the references, on the web, mention AAS (along with
many other government projects) as: What not to do!

Search using the keywords FAA and AAS. There are several
"hits" for both keywords where AAS does not refer to the project.

It is likely the source code is proprietary; hence not available under
the Freedom of Information Act.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

## Thread: open vendor question — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### open vendor question

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au>`

```

I have been working out how to implement a generic feature in Cobol
for handling various length strings without many code changes.

I understand that there is a "PIC X ANY" feature in linkage section
for the standard.

Can the major vendors indicate when they intend to support this
feature.  If so when?

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

PS:  The definition of a major vendor is up to you.  If you think you
are small then you probably are!
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

From one of my "usually reliable" sources at MERANT, (concerning Net Express)

"I'm allowed to give an <UN>official position... (well my view as I see
it)...

This feature in my opinion would require runtime and generator changes and
is not isolated to our checker technology so is not on the cards in the
short term. Any implementation would have be part of our strategy for
implementing the whole ISO200x standard."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
To use this feature (in a function or method - I am not certain if it is or
is not in the current draft for called subprograms) what you code is:

Linkage Section.
01 SomeParm Pic X Any Length.
Procedure Division Using SomeParm.
   ...

The length of SomeParm is determined by the "run-time" system.  It is *not*
required that the invoking program (or program using this reference) "fill
in" any field with the length of this particular SomeParm. It is also
perfectly legal for "SomeParm" to have different lengths each time the
function/method is invoked.

Does this help explain how it is used?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Tue, 1 Aug 2000 20:07:41 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>To use this feature (in a function or method - I am not certain if it is or
>is not in the current draft for called subprograms) what you code is:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Does this help explain how it is used?

So are you saying that at this point if you used FUNCTION
LENGTH(SomeParm) it would return the actual length?  I guess all I'm
saying is that, even right now, you can define a linkage a linkage 01
level as PIC X but you can still reference past just one byte by using
reference modification or whatever.  Of course you'd have to pass the
length as a second 01 level or have some other way of determining it,
so if PIC X ANY gets past that it would be great.

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mala2$c8a$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
Yes,
  I am saying that if you use Function LENGTH  - that you will get the "real"
length of the passed item.

No,
  You may not *LEGALLY* define any Linkage Section items as PIC X - and then
address any amount  of storage that you want.  Although this WORKS some
(most?) of the time, the results are explicitly undefined.  What is more,
there are (historical) examples of where using this "undefined" situation
causes problems.  Just ask any IBM CICS programmer who accidentally tried to
address beyond a 4K boundary with older IBM COBOL products.

Furthermore, with "conformance" checking that will be introduced with the
next Standard - this may even cause a compile-time error - if the lengths of
your passed and received items don't match.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** infocat@sprynet.com (Frank Swarbrick)
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398a0c05.22019712@nntp.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com> <8mala2$c8a$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Wed, 2 Aug 2000 21:27:41 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Yes,
>  I am saying that if you use Function LENGTH  - that you will get the "real"
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>your passed and received items don't match.

OK, here's my thing....   Take the following code,

ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  NUMTEST.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  MY-MESSAGE                           PIC X(10) VALUE '1234567A''.
01  MY-RESULT                               PIC X.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    CALL 'CHKNUMR' USING 
                                    BY MY-MESSAGE
                                    BY CONTENT LENGTH OF MY-MESSAGE
                                    BY REFERENCE MY-RESULT
     IF MY-RESULT = 'Y' 
         DISPLAY 'MY-MESSAGE HAS NUMERICS'
     ELSE
         DISPLAY 'MY-MESSAGE HAS NO NUMERICS'
     END-IF
     GOBACK.

ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. CHMNUMR.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
     01  SUB                                           PIC S9(9) COMP.

LINKAGE SECTION.
     01  SCAN-AREA                                 PIC X(80).
     01  SCAN-SIZE                                    PIC S9(9) COMP.
     01  SCAN-RESULT                            PIC X..
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING SCAN-AREA

SCAN-SIZE

SCAN-RESULT.
     MOVE 'N'  TO  SCAN-RESULT
     PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
                                 UNTIL SUB > SCAN-SIZE
                                       OR SCAN-RESULT = 'Y'
          IF SCAN-AREA (SUB:1) NUMERIC
              MOVE 'Y'  TO  SCAN-RESULT
          END-IF
     END-PERFORM.

     EXIT PROGRAM.
END-PROGRAM CHMNUMR.
END-PROGRAM. NUMTEST.

First of all, yes I realize you could simply check MY-MESSAGE for
NUMERIC.  This is only an example!  :-)

In any case, here I defined SCAN-AREA as PIC X(80).  But does it
really matter what size I define it as?  It's only a pointer to the
working-storage in the calling program.  As long as SCAN-SIZE is set
properly it doesn't matter what size SCAN-AREA is defined as.  I'm not
assember expert, but I'm fairly certain this is done all the time in
assember.

Now as for conformance checking, yeah I bet that would be caught at
compile time.  And certainly having a PIC X ANY would be beneficial,
there's no denying that.  But why pretend we can't do something now
when we really can?

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8md4v5$mi0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com> <8mala2$c8a$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <398a0c05.22019712@nntp.sprynet.com>`

```
Again, this example PROBABLY will work on MOST systems with MOST compilers -
but it still isn't supported.

Try accessing a 64K item in the linkage section of a subprogram - when you
have only defined it as 1K.  This may WELL cause you problems (in some
operating systems).

The point is that COBOL behavior is only defined to the extent that the
CALLing and CALLed program define the parameters the same (or smaller in the
CALLed program).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<65rkos0nq7mtrr55obue2i1sfrv9so12e3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com> <8mala2$c8a$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <398a0c05.22019712@nntp.sprynet.com> <8md4v5$mi0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Thu, 3 Aug 2000 20:07:31 -0500, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
wrote:

>Again, this example PROBABLY will work on MOST systems with MOST compilers -
>but it still isn't supported.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>CALLed program).
Making a small change in Frank program should take care of this

ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  NUMTEST.
<snipped>

ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. CHMNUMR.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
     01  SUB                                           PIC S9(9) COMP.

LINKAGE SECTION.
     01  SCAN-AREA.
         02 Scan-area-char                            PIC X
                        OCCURS 1 TO max-number-allowed-by-compiler
                        DEPENDING ON SCAN-SIZE.
     01  SCAN-SIZE                                    PIC S9(9) COMP.
     01  SCAN-RESULT                            PIC X..
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING SCAN-AREA
<snipped>

Then the called program has legally access to all the data it has been passed by the
caller.  



     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)



      I'm still chasing girls.  I don't remember what for, but I'm still chasing them.  - Joe E. Lewis
```

Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 1 Aug 2000 20:07:41 -0500, "William M. Klein"
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

Frank,

It all depends upon what you mean by 'actual'. Wont bother to post here,
but because of this topic I already had tried it in NetExpress. Given a
field of length pic x(80, stuff it with what you like, including x'00'
positioned somewhere - Function Length ALWAYS returns 80.

> I guess all I'm
> saying is that, even right now, you can define a linkage a linkage 01
> level as PIC X but you can still reference past just one byte by using
> reference modification or whatever.

Right on - that's what Thane said. Ref-mod is the trick.

> length as a second 01 level or have some other way of determining it,
> so if PIC X ANY gets past that it would be great.

	call xyz(or invoke MyClass "xyz') using myLenth MyText

Jimmy

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mbue9$a19$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com> <39891292.95FC253B@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:39891292.95FC253B@home.com...
> It all depends upon what you mean by 'actual'. Wont bother to post here,
> but because of this topic I already had tried it in NetExpress. Given a
> field of length pic x(80, stuff it with what you like, including x'00'
> positioned somewhere - Function Length ALWAYS returns 80.

Well, the way I interpret the ANY LENGHT issue, the function Length actually
should return the correct size.

If I was to compare ANY LENGTH to anything in other languages, it would be
like the open arrays you have in C and Pascal. That is, an array with an
undefined length at implementation time, so, at runtime, the receiving
function will dynamically define the length.

This is not to be mixed with the more commonly known ascii 0 terminated
strings, which basically is a block of memory, that somewhere has a
terminating ascii 0. Also known as char pointers. (in C: char *) (in pascal:
PChar);

Acucobol does for instance, have functionality to provide character pointers
if one uses good will (which we do, don't we :-) ). But, their presence is
not dealt with automatically by the runtime, which would have been a real
scoop.

What Cobol needs is a datatype PCHAR, and embedded knowledge in the runtimes
that handles these differently from conventional PIC X declarations, yet
allows standard operations on them.

What I would like to see is:

    77 DYNAMIC-STRING                    USAGE CHAR POINTER.
    77 STATIC-STRING                         PIC X(100).

as such, a:
    MOVE STATIC-STRING                TO DYNAMIC-STRING

should establish the needed memory for DYNAMIC-STRING automatically and copy
all the 100 elements from STATIC-STRING to DYNAMIC-STRING.

The other way around however, say that DYNAMIC-STRING contained: "Hello
world"X"00" and we did the following:

    MOVE DYNAMIC-STRING          TO STATIC-STRING

Which should move "Hello world" to STATIC-STRING not including the
terminating ascii 0.

Cheesle
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```


Cheesle wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> should return the correct size.

Just to clarify this one :-
-------------------------------------------------------------
Program-id. TestLength.                             
Working-Storage section.                            
01 MyText                       pic x(80).          
01 LengthCount                  pic 9(02).          
Procedure Division.                                 
Initialize MyText                                   
compute LengthCount = function length(MyText)       
move "Hello World" to MyText                        
compute LengthCount = function length(MyText)       
move x"00" to MyText(12:1)                          
compute LengthCount = function length(MyText)       
compute LengthCount = function length(MyText(1:12)).
----------------------------------------------------------------------

First three tests = 80 and the last = 12 - but you knew that already.

This thread or elsewhere - Bill was ADAMANT you will get correct length
- so can he please clarify. (He's usually right <G>).

Must confess, I was disappointed first time I used function length and
got the 'max' characters. If above coding sample is correct, then it's a
pity we didn't have :-

compute LengthCount = function MaxLength(MyText)    *> = 80
compute LengthCount = function ActualLength(MyText) *> = 12

Both serve a use as a short-cut to 'manipulating'       	 .

(BTW : Not standard but can a Micro Focus/Merant user explain to me
difference between function Length and function Length-AN. I don't know
how many times I've read the on-line help, comparing both, word by word
- could it be that Length-AN would give me what I was really after = 12
and not 80 ?)

-----------------------
> 
> If I was to compare ANY LENGTH to anything in other languages, it would be
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> terminating ascii 0.

I take your points above. Currently this is what I'm doing :-

Class - EditCustomer
--------------------
method-id "do-some-stuff"
*>------------------------

  move length of CUSTOMER-name to Mylength   *> Non-standard but I think
					     *> Bill said it wil be in
					     *> COBOL 200X - otherwise:-
  compute MyLength = function length(CUSTOMER-name)

  invoke os-MyDialog "set-up-field" using MyLenth
					  CUSTOMER-name

Class : MyDialog
*>----------------
method-id. "set-up-field".
*>-----------------------
Linkage seciton.
01 lnk-length		pic x(4) comp-5. *> could be 9(03).
01 lnk-Text.
   05  pic x occurs 1 to x depending on lnk-Length.
Procedure Division using lnk-length lnk-Text.
	etc.... do "funnies" to turn into displayable object

----------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mcgmc$err$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com> <39891292.95FC253B@home.com> <8mbue9$a19$1@news.cerf.net> <3989B55F.68E2457E@home.com>`

```
Bill is (was) adamant that you get the "correct" length *if* you use the ANY
LENGTH for a data item - and it is defined in Linkage Section (the only place
that ANY LENGTH is valid).

FYI,
  The difference between LENGTH and LENGTH-AN (or BYTE-LENGTH in the draft
Standard) ONLY shows up if you have DBCS (MOCS or National) data.

LENGTH returns the number of characters. Therefore, if you have
    05 Nat  Pic NN.

FUNCTION LENGTH (Nat) will return a value of "2" because Nat is two NATIONAL
characters long.

However, Function Byte-Length (NAT) or
                 Function Length-AN (Nat)

will return a value of *4* - if you are in a system that uses 2 bytes for
each national character.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open vendor question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398AA050.A9C1A204@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3986CAF4.1CC673EB@zip.com.au> <39875efa.33009776@nntp.sprynet.com> <8m7s7v$q0a$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3988d2a1.9303676@nntp.sprynet.com> <39891292.95FC253B@home.com> <8mbue9$a19$1@news.cerf.net> <3989B55F.68E2457E@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Just to clarify this one :-
> -------------------------------------------------------------
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> and not 80 ?)

The length should always be 80.  Low values is only special for C
programming,  hex 00 is just another byte.

The difference with pic x 'any' is only on linkage.  When a string is
passed to the calling routine the size is magically passed.

If I pass PIC x(12) to a routine using 'pic x any' then the function
length in the routine will (should) return 12.

If I pass PIC x(80) to the same routine then the function length in
the routine will (should) return 80.

This is my understanding of the feature and exactly what I need.

An extension to this is if I pass a value "using  'some text'."  I
would expect function length to return 9.

I strongly disagree with cobol following the C route of ending a
string with a Null.   There are some advantages but it is actually
against what I perceive as the general thread of the language, fix
length fields are easy in cobol hard in C.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

- **From:** "Stephen Dennis" <sdennis@svdltd.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

"Phil Vucovich" <pvucovich@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net...
> Does anyone have a good algorithm that would allow me to add a
> specified number of days to a date giving the new date.  It needs
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Why not throw mine out there?

The following gives broad Date support from 27255 BC to 30826 AD
with a proleptic gregorian calender. The 1601 Epoch comes from
Windows NT's Epoch for FILETIME. So, if your COBOL supports a
__int64, you can construct a FILETIME directly.


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. SVDDATE.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

      ******************************************************************
      * 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD                                              *
      ******************************************************************
       01  FDM-X   PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  FDM-Y   PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  FDM-DIV PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  FDM-MOD PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  FDM-TMP PIC S9(8) COMP.

      ******************************************************************
      * 1002-FLOOR-DIV                                                 *
      ******************************************************************
       01  FD-X    PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  FD-Y    PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  FD-DIV  PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  FD-TMP  PIC S9(8) COMP.

      ******************************************************************
      * 1003-ISLEAPYEAR                                                *
      ******************************************************************
       01  ILY-YEAR    PIC S9(5) COMP.
       01  ILY-LEAP    PIC X.
           88  ILY-IS-LEAP-YEAR    VALUE 'Y'.
           88  ILY-NOT-LEAP-YEAR   VALUE 'N'.

      ******************************************************************
      * 1004-ISVALIDDATE                                               *
      ******************************************************************
       01  IVD-YEAR            PIC S9(5) COMP.
       01  IVD-MONTH           PIC 99    COMP.
       01  IVD-DAY-OF-MONTH    PIC 99    COMP.
       01  IVD-VALID           PIC X.
           88  IVD-IS-VALID-DATE       VALUE 'Y'.
           88  IVD-IS-NOT-VALID-DATE   VALUE 'N'.
       01  IVD-DAYS-IN-MONTH-VALUES.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 31.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 29.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 31.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 30.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 31.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 30.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 31.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 31.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 30.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 31.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 30.
           05  FILLER          PIC 99 COMP VALUE 31.
       01  IVD-DAYS-IN-MONTH-TABLE REDEFINES IVD-DAYS-IN-MONTH-VALUES.
           05  IVD-DAYS-IN-MONTH   OCCURS 12 TIMES PIC 99 COMP.

      ******************************************************************
      * 1005-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR                                       *
      * 1006-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR1601                                   *
      ******************************************************************
       01  GTL-YEAR         PIC S9(5) COMP.
       01  GTL-MONTH        PIC 99    COMP.
       01  GTL-DAY-OF-MONTH PIC 99    COMP.
       01  GTL-LINEAR       PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  GTL-YEAR-LESS-1  PIC S9(5) COMP.
       01  GTL-TEMP-DAYS    PIC 9999  COMP.

      ******************************************************************
      * 1007-LINEAR-TO-GREGORIAN                                       *
      * 1008-LINEAR1601-TO-GREGORIAN                                   *
      ******************************************************************
       01  LTG-LINEAR        PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-YEAR          PIC S9(5) COMP.
       01  LTG-MONTH         PIC 99    COMP.
       01  LTG-DAY-OF-MONTH  PIC 99    COMP.
       01  LTG-DAY-OF-YEAR   PIC 999   COMP.
       01  LTG-DAY-OF-WEEK   PIC 9     COMP.
       01  LTG-D0            PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-D1            PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-D2            PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-D3            PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-D4            PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-N1            PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-N4            PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-N100          PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-N400          PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-JAN01         PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-MAR01         PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-CORRECTION    PIC 9     COMP.
       01  LTG-PRIOR-DAYS    PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-TEMP          PIC S9(8) COMP.
       01  LTG-1ST           PIC S9(8) COMP.

       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  VD-DATE.
           05  VD-YEAR         PIC S9(5) COMP.
           05  VD-MONTH        PIC 99    COMP.
           05  VD-DAY-OF-MONTH PIC 99    COMP.

       01  VD-VALID            PIC X.
           88  VD-IS-VALID     VALUE "Y".
           88  VD-NOT-VALID    VALUE "N".

       01  FTL-DATE.
           05  FTL-YEAR         PIC S9(5) COMP.
           05  FTL-MONTH        PIC 99    COMP.
           05  FTL-DAY-OF-MONTH PIC 99    COMP.

       01  FTL-LINEAR           PIC S9(8) COMP.

       01  LTF-LINEAR           PIC S9(8) COMP.

       01  LTF-DATE.
           05  LTF-YEAR         PIC S9(5) COMP.
           05  LTF-MONTH        PIC 99    COMP.
           05  LTF-DAY-OF-MONTH PIC 99    COMP.
           05  LTF-DAY-OF-YEAR  PIC 999   COMP.
           05  LTF-DAY-OF-WEEK  PIC 9     COMP.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       0000-START-HERE.
           EXIT PROGRAM.

       ENTRY "VERIFY-DATE" USING VD-DATE, VD-VALID.
           MOVE VD-YEAR to IVD-YEAR.
           MOVE VD-MONTH TO IVD-MONTH.
           MOVE VD-DAY-OF-MONTH to IVD-DAY-OF-MONTH.
           PERFORM 1004-ISVALIDDATE.
           MOVE IVD-VALID TO VD-VALID.
           EXIT PROGRAM.

       ENTRY "FIELDED-TO-LINEAR" USING FTL-DATE, FTL-LINEAR.
           MOVE FTL-YEAR TO GTL-YEAR.
           MOVE FTL-MONTH TO GTL-MONTH.
           MOVE FTL-DAY-OF-MONTH TO GTL-DAY-OF-MONTH.
           PERFORM 1006-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR1601.
           MOVE GTL-LINEAR TO FTL-LINEAR.
           EXIT PROGRAM.

       ENTRY "LINEAR-TO-FIELDED" USING LTF-LINEAR, LTF-DATE.
           MOVE LTF-LINEAR TO LTG-LINEAR.
           PERFORM 1008-LINEAR1601-TO-GREGORIAN.
           MOVE LTG-YEAR TO LTF-YEAR.
           MOVE LTG-MONTH TO LTF-MONTH.
           MOVE LTG-DAY-OF-MONTH TO LTF-DAY-OF-MONTH.
           MOVE LTG-DAY-OF-YEAR TO LTF-DAY-OF-YEAR.
           MOVE LTG-DAY-OF-WEEK TO LTF-DAY-OF-WEEK.
           EXIT PROGRAM.

      ******************************************************************
      * The following "Floor Division Modulus" routine calculates the  *
      * proper modulus of a negative number. For negative numbers, a   *
      * modulus is different than a remainder. The modulus is always   *
      * positive.                                                      *
      *                                                                *
      * For example, -1 floordivmod 2 gives a FDM-DIV of -1 with a     *
      * FDM-MOD of +1. In other words, -1/2 ==> -1 + 1/2               *
      *                                                                *
      * In contrast, a remainder approach gives a different answer:    *
      * -1/2 ==> 0 + -1/2                                              *
      ******************************************************************
       1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD.
           IF FDM-X >= 0
               DIVIDE FDM-Y INTO FDM-X GIVING FDM-DIV REMAINDER FDM-MOD
           ELSE
               ADD 1 TO FDM-X GIVING FDM-TMP
               SUBTRACT FDM-Y FROM FDM-TMP
               DIVIDE FDM-Y INTO FDM-TMP GIVING FDM-DIV
                   REMAINDER FDM-MOD
               ADD FDM-Y TO FDM-MOD
               SUBTRACT 1 FROM FDM-MOD
           END-IF.

      ******************************************************************
      * The following "Floor Division" performs the same division      *
      * operation as 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD above. See above for a more     *
      * detailed explanation.                                          *
      ******************************************************************
       1002-FLOOR-DIV.
           IF FD-X >= 0
               DIVIDE FD-Y INTO FD-X GIVING FD-DIV
           ELSE
               ADD 1 TO FD-X GIVING FD-TMP
               SUBTRACT FD-Y FROM FD-TMP
               DIVIDE FD-Y INTO FD-TMP GIVING FD-DIV
           END-IF.

      ******************************************************************
      * The following code determines whether a year is a leap year or *
      * not. It works for negative years.                              *
      ******************************************************************
       1003-ISLEAPYEAR.
           MOVE ILY-YEAR TO FDM-X.
           MOVE 4 to FDM-Y.
           PERFORM 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD.
           IF FDM-MOD <> 0
               MOVE "N" TO ILY-LEAP
           ELSE
               MOVE 400 TO FDM-Y
               PERFORM 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD
               IF (FDM-MOD = 100) OR (FDM-MOD = 200) OR (FDM-MOD = 300)
                   MOVE "N" TO ILY-LEAP
               ELSE
                   MOVE "Y" TO ILY-LEAP
               END-IF
           END-IF.

      ******************************************************************
      * The following code determines whether a given date is a valid  *
      * one or not. This test guarantees that the given date will be   *
      * convertible to a 'linear' day number without the overhead of   *
      * over actually calculating it.                                  *
      ******************************************************************
       1004-ISVALIDDATE.
           MOVE "N" TO IVD-VALID.
           IF (-27256 <= IVD-YEAR) AND (IVD-YEAR <= 30826)
           AND (1 <= IVD-MONTH) AND (IVD-MONTH <= 12)
           AND (1 <= IVD-DAY-OF-MONTH)
           AND (IVD-DAY-OF-MONTH < IVD-DAYS-IN-MONTH(IVD-MONTH))
               IF (IVD-MONTH = 2) AND (IVD-DAY-OF-MONTH = 29)
                   MOVE IVD-YEAR TO ILY-YEAR
                   PERFORM 1003-ISLEAPYEAR
                   IF (ILY-IS-LEAP-YEAR)
                       MOVE "Y" TO IVD-VALID
                   END-IF
               ELSE
                   MOVE "Y" TO IVD-VALID
               END-IF
           END-IF.

      ******************************************************************
      * The following date conversion routine takes a Gregorian        *
      * Calendar date and calculates a linear numbering of days from   *
      * the Epoch of 1 R.D. January 1                                  *
      *                                                                *
      * The date is assumed valid (see 1004-ISVALIDDATE)               *
      ******************************************************************
       1005-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR.
           SUBTRACT 1 FROM GTL-YEAR GIVING GTL-YEAR-LESS-1.
           MULTIPLY GTL-YEAR-LESS-1 BY 365 GIVING GTL-LINEAR.

           MOVE GTL-YEAR-LESS-1 TO FD-X.
           MOVE 4 TO FD-Y.
           PERFORM 1002-FLOOR-DIV.
           ADD FD-DIV TO GTL-LINEAR.

           MOVE 100 TO FD-Y.
           PERFORM 1002-FLOOR-DIV.
           SUBTRACT FD-DIV FROM GTL-LINEAR.

           MOVE 400 to FD-Y.
           PERFORM 1002-FLOOR-DIV.
           ADD FD-DIV TO GTL-LINEAR.

           MULTIPLY 367 BY GTL-MONTH GIVING GTL-TEMP-DAYS.
           SUBTRACT 362 FROM GTL-TEMP-DAYS GIVING FD-X.
           MOVE 12 TO FD-Y.
           PERFORM 1002-FLOOR-DIV.
           ADD FD-DIV TO GTL-LINEAR.

           ADD GTL-DAY-OF-MONTH TO GTL-LINEAR.

           IF GTL-MONTH > 2
               MOVE GTL-YEAR TO ILY-YEAR
               PERFORM 1003-ISLEAPYEAR
               IF ILY-IS-LEAP-YEAR
                   SUBTRACT 1 FROM GTL-LINEAR
               ELSE
                   SUBTRACT 2 FROM GTL-LINEAR
               END-IF
           END-IF.

      ******************************************************************
      * The above code calculates GTL-LINEAR with an Epoch of 1 R.D    *
      * We make an adjustment now to change the Epoch to 1601 January  *
      * 1st.                                                           *
      ******************************************************************
       1006-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR1601.
           PERFORM 1005-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR.
           SUBTRACT 584389 FROM GTL-LINEAR.

      ******************************************************************
      * The following date conversion routine takes a linear numbering *
      * of days from the Epoch of 1601 January 1st and calculates the  *
      * corresponding date in the Gregorian Calendar.                  *
      ******************************************************************
       1007-LINEAR-TO-GREGORIAN.
           SUBTRACT 1 FROM LTG-LINEAR GIVING LTG-D0.

           MOVE LTG-D0 TO FDM-X.
           MOVE 146097 TO FDM-Y.
           PERFORM 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD.
           MOVE FDM-DIV TO LTG-N400.
           MOVE FDM-MOD TO LTG-D1.

           MOVE LTG-D1 TO FDM-X.
           MOVE 36524 TO FDM-Y.
           PERFORM 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD.
           MOVE FDM-DIV TO LTG-N100.
           MOVE FDM-MOD TO LTG-D2.

           MOVE LTG-D2 TO FDM-X.
           MOVE 1461 TO FDM-Y.
           PERFORM 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD.
           MOVE FDM-DIV TO LTG-N4.
           MOVE FDM-MOD TO LTG-D3.

           MOVE LTG-D3 TO FDM-X.
           MOVE 365 TO FDM-Y.
           PERFORM 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD.
           MOVE FDM-DIV TO LTG-N1.
           MOVE FDM-MOD TO LTG-D4.

           ADD 1 TO LTG-D4.

           COMPUTE LTG-YEAR = 400*LTG-N400 + 100*LTG-N100
                            + 4*LTG-N4 + LTG-N1.

           IF (LTG-N100 <> 4) AND (LTG-N1 <> 4)
               ADD 1 TO LTG-YEAR
           END-IF.

           MOVE LTG-YEAR TO GTL-YEAR.
           MOVE 1 TO GTL-MONTH.
           MOVE 1 TO GTL-DAY-OF-MONTH.
           PERFORM 1005-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR.
           MOVE GTL-LINEAR TO LTG-JAN01.

           MOVE 3 TO GTL-MONTH.
           PERFORM 1005-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR.
           MOVE GTL-LINEAR TO LTG-MAR01.

           IF (LTG-LINEAR  < LTG-MAR01)
               MOVE 0 TO LTG-CORRECTION
           ELSE
               MOVE LTG-YEAR TO ILY-YEAR
               PERFORM 1003-ISLEAPYEAR
               IF ILY-IS-LEAP-YEAR
                   MOVE 1 TO LTG-CORRECTION
               ELSE
                   MOVE 2 TO LTG-CORRECTION
               END-IF
           END-IF.

           SUBTRACT LTG-JAN01 FROM LTG-LINEAR GIVING LTG-PRIOR-DAYS.
           ADD 1 TO LTG-PRIOR-DAYS GIVING LTG-DAY-OF-YEAR.
           COMPUTE LTG-TEMP =
               (12 * (LTG-PRIOR-DAYS + LTG-CORRECTION) + 373)/367.
           MOVE LTG-TEMP TO LTG-MONTH.

           MOVE LTG-MONTH TO GTL-MONTH.
           PERFORM 1005-GREGORIAN-TO-LINEAR.
           MOVE GTL-LINEAR TO LTG-1ST.

           COMPUTE LTG-DAY-OF-MONTH = LTG-LINEAR - LTG-1ST + 1.

           MOVE LTG-LINEAR TO FDM-X.
           MOVE 7 TO FDM-Y.
           PERFORM 1001-FLOOR-DIVMOD.
           MOVE FDM-MOD TO LTG-DAY-OF-WEEK.

      ******************************************************************
      * The above code uses LTG-LINEAR with an Epoch of 1 R.D.         *
      * We make an adjustment now so that we may accept LTG-LINEAR     *
      * with an Epoch of 1601 January 1st.                             *
      ******************************************************************
       1008-LINEAR1601-TO-GREGORIAN.
           ADD 584389 TO LTG-LINEAR.
           PERFORM 1007-LINEAR-TO-GREGORIAN.
       END PROGRAM JERM.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. DRIVER.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT OUTPUTFILE
               ASSIGN TO "OUTPUT"
               ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD OUTPUTFILE.
       01  DATE-RECORD.
           05  DR-LINEAR-DATE     PIC S9(8)   COMP.

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  A               PIC S9(8) COMP VALUE -1.

      *
      * Size of this structure is 8 bytes.
      *
       01  FIELDED-DATE.
           05  YEAR        PIC S9(5)   COMP.
           05  MONTH       PIC 99      COMP.
           05  DOM         PIC 99      COMP.
           05  DOY         PIC 999     COMP.
           05  DOW         PIC 9       COMP.

       01  BOOL            PIC X.
           88  IS-VALID    VALUE "Y".
           88  NOT-VALID   VALUE "N".

      *
      * This size of this item is 4 bytes.
      *
       01  LINEAR-DATE     PIC S9(8)   COMP.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       0000-START-HERE.
           CALL "JERM".
           MOVE 2000 TO YEAR.
           MOVE 2 TO MONTH.
           PERFORM VARYING A FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL A > 20
               MOVE A TO DOM
               CALL "VERIFY-DATE" USING FIELDED-DATE, BOOL
               IF NOT-VALID
                   DISPLAY "DATE NOT VALID."
               ELSE
                   CALL "FIELDED-TO-LINEAR" USING FIELDED-DATE,
                       LINEAR-DATE
                   DISPLAY YEAR " " MONTH " " DOM " " LINEAR-DATE
                       WITH NO ADVANCING

                   CALL "LINEAR-TO-FIELDED" USING LINEAR-DATE,
                       FIELDED-DATE
                   DISPLAY " " YEAR " " MONTH " " DOM " " DOY " " DOW
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM.
           OPEN OUTPUT OUTPUTFILE.
           MOVE LINEAR-DATE TO DATE-RECORD.
           WRITE DATE-RECORD.
           CLOSE OUTPUTFILE.
           GO TO 9999-STOP-RUN.

       9999-STOP-RUN.
           STOP RUN.
       END PROGRAM DRIVER.
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m5rnr$8ct$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net>`

```
Sounds like homework, but try this one (if your compiler supports the
used functions...):

Defines:
       01 FullDate.
          03 FD-TT                  pic 99 value 0.
          03 Filler                 pic x value '.'.
          03 FD-MM                  pic 99 value 0.
          03 Filler                 pic x value '.'.
          03 FD-JJ                  pic 9999 value 0.

       01 ReverseDate.
          03 RD-JJ                  pic 9999 value 0.
          03 RD-MM                  pic 99 value 0.
          03 RD-TT                  pic 99 value 0.

       01 IntReverseDate pic 9(8) value 0.
       01 ResultDate pic 9(8) value 0.
       01 IntegerDate pic 9(8) value 0.
       01 PlusTage     Pic S999 value 0.

       Add2Date Section.
         Move Fd-jj to Rd-jj
         Move fd-mm to rd-mm
         move fd-tt to rd-tt
         Move ReverseDate to IntReverseDate
         Move function Integer-of-date( IntReverseDate )
             to IntegerDate
         Add PlusTage to IntegerDate
         Move Function Date-Of-Integer( IntegerDate ) to ResultDate
         .

HTH - Stefan Meyer

#### ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3986CCB5.9BAFC238@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net>`

```


Phil Vucovich wrote:
> 
> Does anyone have a good algorithm that would allow me to add a specified
> number of days to a date giving the new date.  It needs to account for the
> different number of days in each month as well as leap years.  If anyone has
> any ideas, let me know, or at least point me in the right direction.

COBOL has functions which can convert dates between true Julian or
Lilian dates.  I can think of occasions where you might not want to use
these, but need to know more details to recommend an alternative.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3986EFEA.ADC92CDB@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net> <3986CCB5.9BAFC238@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> COBOL has functions which can convert dates between true Julian or
> Lilian dates.  I can think of occasions where you might not want to use
> these, but need to know more details to recommend an alternative.

What's a Lilian date?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m6uec$ug$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net> <3986CCB5.9BAFC238@brazee.net> <3986EFEA.ADC92CDB@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
"Calvin Crumrine" <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote in message
news:3986EFEA.ADC92CDB@dced.state.ak.us...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Short answer - it is a date expressed as an "integer" from a specific start
date.  Usually, it is from the date of the "Gregorian" conversion from the
Julian calendar (actually the date the conversion occurred in Roman Catholic
countries).

In Standard COBOL, it is the number of days since Dec 31, 1600.  (This means
that day 1 is a Monday - which fits in with ACCEPT FROM DAY-OF-WEEK).

Some compilers (such as IBM mainframe compiler) have a way of "selecting"
which date scheme you use.

I am certain that if you check www.deja.com, you can find the historical
references to the person after which "Lilian" is named.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398702CD.264EF2C1@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net> <3986CCB5.9BAFC238@brazee.net> <3986EFEA.ADC92CDB@dced.state.ak.us>`

```


Calvin Crumrine wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's a Lilian date?

A Lilian date is an integer date system in which day 1 is October 15,
1582, the first day of the Gregorian calendar. It is named for Luigi
Lilio, who provided most of the ideas for the Gregorian calendar reform,
including the rule for leap years.   

IBM wrote their language environment before COBOL had standardized on
true Julian and the LE provided languages with a 64 bit double precision
floating point number containing the number of seconds elapsed since
00:00:00 14 October 1582.

IBM also has a date which they incorrectly call Julian, which was
developed when they had an early case of a Y2K type problem.  It is a
seven digit integer in the form of YYYYDDD.  Because of this, they
called their version of Julian date "Integer date", which is the number
of days since March 15, 2000.

Astronomers use Julian date: 
A serial number used since 1582 in astronomical calculations, equal to
the number of days elapsed since January 1, 4713 BC.  This system was
invented in 1582 by Joseph Scaliger, who named it for his father, Julius
Caesar Scaliger.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m77u4$mdf$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net> <3986CCB5.9BAFC238@brazee.net> <3986EFEA.ADC92CDB@dced.state.ak.us> <398702CD.264EF2C1@brazee.net>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:398702CD.264EF2C1@brazee.net...
>

> IBM wrote their language environment before COBOL had standardized on
> true Julian and the LE provided languages with a 64 bit double precision
> floating point number containing the number of seconds elapsed since
> 00:00:00 14 October 1582.
>

Howard,
  You might well think that was true, but actually it isn't.  The COBOL
definition of an "integer" date WELL pre-dates the first release of LE.  On
the other hand, Luigi and Pope Gregory were well, WELL before that, so it is
(semi-) understandable why IBM didn't use the "correct for COBOL" date.
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

- **From:** "Johannes Jung" <jung004@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m9d1i$gtm$17$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net>`

```
Here are the algorithms:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
-------
use:
    01  s-datum pic s9(7) comp3 value 1000802.
    01  s-number-of-days pic s9(9).

 call "datd7n" using s-number-of-days s-datum.
 add 30 to s-number-of-dates.
 call "datn7d" using s-number-of-days s-datum.

Now you have the new date 1000901.

If you divide s-number-of-days by 7, then the remainder gives the day:
monday, tuesday and so on.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
--------
      IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.                          DATD7N.
      ******************************************************************
      ***
      ***  FUNKTION:    SINNV.DATUM(GREG.) --> NAT.ZAHL
      ***
      ***  AUTHOR:      J.JUNG  FEBRUAR83
      ***
      ****AENDERUNGEN***************************************************
      *** ..0283 ORIGINAL AUS FORTRAN: SPAETH HELMUTH, AUSGEW OP.RES...
      *** 011298 KOPIE AUS LIBJUN/JD.S
      ***
      ***
      ******************************************************************
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-S34.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-S34.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  J                 PIC S9(5) COMP-3.
       01  L                 PIC S9(5) COMP-3.
       01  L1                PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  L2                PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  L3                PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  S-DATUM           PIC S9(7).
       01  S-DATUM-RED       REDEFINES S-DATUM.
           05  S-JH          PIC S9(1).
           05  S-JAHR        PIC S9(2).
           05  S-MONAT       PIC S9(2).
           05  S-TAG         PIC S9(2).
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  L-JD              PIC S9(9).
       01  L-DATUM           PIC S9(7) COMP-3.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING L-JD L-DATUM.
       BEGINN-0001.
           MOVE L-DATUM                TO S-DATUM.
           COMPUTE J = (S-MONAT - 14) / 12.
           COMPUTE L = (1900 + S-JAHR) + J + 4800.
      *    2000 = 1...
           COMPUTE L = L + 100 * S-JH.
            COMPUTE L1 = (1461 * L) / 4.
            COMPUTE L2 = (367 * (S-MONAT - 2 - 12 * J)) / 12.
            COMPUTE L3 = (L + 100) / 100.
             COMPUTE L3 = (3 * L3) / 4.
      *    1970.01.01 = 01 --> SUB 2440587
      *    COMPUTE L-JD = S-TAG - 32075 + L1 - 2440587
            COMPUTE L-JD = S-TAG - 2472662 + L1
                         + L2
                         - L3.
       ENDE-00001.
           EXIT PROGRAM.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
--------------------------------
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.                          DATND7.
      ******************************************************************
      ***
      ***  FUNKTION:    NAT.ZAHL --> DATUM-GREG.
      ***
      ***  AUTHOR:      J.JUNG  FEBRUAR83
      ***
      ****AENDERUNGEN***************************************************
      *** ..0283 ORIGINAL AUS FORTRAN: SPAETH HELMUTH, AUSGEW OP.RES...
      *** 011298 KOPIE AUS LIBJUN/DJ.S
      ***
      ***
      ******************************************************************
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-S34.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-S34.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  L                 PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  L2                PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  N                 PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  JJRR              PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  MONAT-R           PIC S9(9) COMP-3.
       01  S-DATUM           PIC S9(7).
       01  S-DATUM-RED       REDEFINES S-DATUM.
           05  S-JH          PIC 9(1).
           05  S-JAHR        PIC 9(2).
           05  S-MONAT       PIC 9(2).
           05  S-TAG         PIC 9(2).
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  L-DJ              PIC S9(9).
       01  L-DATUM           PIC S9(7) COMP-3.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING L-DJ L-DATUM.
       BEGINN-0001.
      *    1970.01.01 = 01 --> ADD 2440587
      *    COMPUTE L = L-DJ + 68569 + 2440587.
            COMPUTE L = L-DJ + 2509156.
            COMPUTE N = 4 * L / 146097.
            COMPUTE L2 = (146097 * N + 3) / 4.
            COMPUTE L = L - L2.
            COMPUTE JJRR = 4000 * (L + 1) / 1461001.
            COMPUTE L2 = 1461 * JJRR / 4.
            COMPUTE L = L - L2 + 31.
            COMPUTE MONAT-R = 80 * L / 2447.
           COMPUTE L2 = 2447 * MONAT-R / 80.
           COMPUTE S-TAG = L - L2.
           COMPUTE L = MONAT-R / 11.
           COMPUTE S-MONAT = MONAT-R + 2 - 12 * L.
           COMPUTE JJRR = 100 * (N - 49) + L + JJRR.
            COMPUTE S-JAHR = JJRR - 1900.
       ENDE-00001.
           IF S-JAHR                   < 70
               MOVE 1                  TO S-JH
           ELSE
               MOVE 0                  TO S-JH.
           MOVE S-DATUM                TO L-DATUM.
       EXIT-00001.
           EXIT PROGRAM.
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Algorithm Question

- **From:** "Mike Sheehan" <msheehan@pks.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8megv2$p5k$1@kermit.esat.net>`
- **References:** `<zLsh5.157$fw5.121654@news.uswest.net>`

```
I could give you some algorithms - written from first principles that we
used for an exercise in our MSc Software Re-engineering program at the
University of Limerick, Ireland. But, you might prefer to use standard
functions etc. We had a variant that could allow for "working days" as well
as actual days. We initially wrote the code in Ada, then converted it into
several PLs including COBOL.

Mike

On Tue, 01 Aug 2000 22:50:14 +1000, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
enlightened us:

>"James J. Gavan" wrote:
>> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ken

According to The American Heritage Dictionary, LASER is indeed a noun.
Here's the definition:

laï¿½ser ( lï¿½ï¿½zï¿½r) n. 1. Any of several devices that convert incident
electromagnetic radiation of mixed frequencies to one or more discrete
frequencies of highly amplified and coherent ultraviolet, visible, or
infrared radiation. 2. A device whose output is in an invisible region
of the electromagnetic spectrum. [ l(ight) a(mplification by)
s(timulated) e(mission of) r(adiation)] 

And Ken, it is not an anachronism.  An anachronism is a representation
of someone as existing or something as happening in other than the
chronological, proper, or historical order.  Also, one that is out of
its proper or chronological order,

Did you possibly mean acronymic or acronymous ?

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

What do chickens think we taste like?






Boycott Mitsubishi Industries
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com> <8lukvk$lr4$1@news.igs.net> <3982E40B.4AD2D0FA@zip.com.au> <m6qcos0huuqjd51ttpdq4lrt7ps71rtt11@4ax.com> <kpyh5.31$PZ5.26850@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39872C2B.A5513F6@worldnet.att.net>`

William Lynch wrote:
> 
> That's why I code:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>         IF FIELD-A IS GREATER THAN SOME-OTHER-FIELD

I got in the habit of typing the words out back when we had a band
printer which didn't have a ">" (or was it "<", I can't remember).  I
never saw any reason to change the habit back to using a symbol,
possibly because the letters in "is greater than" are always in the same
place on all keyboards, and I can type faster than I should be thinking
anyway.

Hmm, some people call "?" an interrogation symbol, others a question
mark.  So we have the "an" vs "a" question here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a noun

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3988888E.8FF65BD@perfectcircle.com>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com> <8lukvk$lr4$1@news.igs.net> <3982E40B.4AD2D0FA@zip.com.au> <m6qcos0huuqjd51ttpdq4lrt7ps71rtt11@4ax.com> <kpyh5.31$PZ5.26850@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39872C2B.A5513F6@worldnet.att.net> <39872EE7.BC40DD3B@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> William Lynch wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Our standard does not allow "IS GREATER THAN", it requires ">".

This started as my preference to a symbol as I could then "see" the
statement and its meaning without having to read the words describing the
condition.  The important words on that line are the variables being
compared, and a textual relationship operator such as "IS GREATER THAN"
distracts the reader from quickly reading the variable names.

While some people would say this is a matter of style, we made it part of
our standard.  One of the goals of our standards is to make all code look
the same, regardless of who wrote the code.  The style of logic constructs,
paragraph names, variable names, etc. are all addressed in our standard.  We
have succeeded in making our programmers a team, with everyone thinking and
writing the same way.  When looking at multiple programs or changes to one
program, we can no longer tell who wrote what sections.  All the code uses
the same style.

We take to heart the idea that effort to write a program is 20% of it's
life, with 80% being used for maintenance.  In our case, it's more like 5%
vs 95%.

Some people might say our standard is too restrictive.  We don't think so.
If a programmer comes up with a style he/she wants to use, it is discussed
with the top few programmers, and if the idea is a good one, it is
incorporated into our standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a noun

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39889187.721E240C@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com> <8lukvk$lr4$1@news.igs.net> <3982E40B.4AD2D0FA@zip.com.au> <m6qcos0huuqjd51ttpdq4lrt7ps71rtt11@4ax.com> <kpyh5.31$PZ5.26850@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39872C2B.A5513F6@worldnet.att.net> <39872EE7.BC40DD3B@brazee.net> <3988888E.8FF65BD@perfectcircle.com>`

```
Fred Young wrote:
> 

> Our standard does not allow "IS GREATER THAN", it requires ">".
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> distracts the reader from quickly reading the variable names.

To me, it is much easier to "see" the English word Greater than to see a
symbol which looks very much like "<".  We can read it aloud as well. 
To me the verb is just as important than the nouns, and is more likely
to be the cause of an undiscovered error. (try misspelling the symbols
and the compiler will often let you know).

The following reasons apply to either standard equally well.  The
maintenance programmer will often assume your mistaken "<" was a
business rule.  
 
> While some people would say this is a matter of style, we made it part of
> our standard.  One of the goals of our standards is to make all code look
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> incorporated into our standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a noun

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ma57r$pv6$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com> <8lukvk$lr4$1@news.igs.net> <3982E40B.4AD2D0FA@zip.com.au> <m6qcos0huuqjd51ttpdq4lrt7ps71rtt11@4ax.com> <kpyh5.31$PZ5.26850@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39872C2B.A5513F6@worldnet.att.net> <39872EE7.BC40DD3B@brazee.net> <3988888E.8FF65BD@perfectcircle.com> <39889187.721E240C@brazee.net>`

```
I am not all that strong on ">" versus "greater" - but do "applaud" shops
that set SOME standard.

I do, however, have a STRONG preference for ">=" as opposed to "greater or
equal".  The reason is that ">=" is clearly a "single" operator" - while
"greater or equal" may WELL be read (incorrectly) as a combined abbreviated
conditional expression.  This caused J4 over a year to "sort out" when you
started coding "NOT greater or equal".
```

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)

```
In article <kpyh5.31$PZ5.26850@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote in message
>news:m6qcos0huuqjd51ttpdq4lrt7ps71rtt11@4ax.com...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ah, but in COBOL, do *you* know what the meaning of the word, "is" is?

I am uncertain as to what you are calling 'meaning' here but the last time
I looked IS was optional and did not have any effect on the compiled code.

01  FLDA PIC X(1).

... compiles to the same object code as 

01  FLDA PICTURE IS X(0001).

... and

IF FLDA IS EQUAL TO FLDB (imperative)

... compiles to the same object code as

IF FLDA = FLDB (imperative)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol as a noun

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** piasa_bird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m70cs$3tl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com> <398613D7.9317CE69@perfectcircle.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-n96fMdcXY4tL@tcpserver> <8m6khs$5gu$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <8m6khs$5gu$1@news.igs.net>,
  "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

Nothing at all wrong with "GO TO"!

Sometimes a "GO TO" is more useful than a perform verb.  It is just as
important that a program be functional as it is that the program is
easy to edit.  Sometimes a proliferation of performs just makes it
harder to make any changes.
 I learned Assembly while I was taking COBOL in school. Most programs
are never as simple as the ones we learned in school.  A top-down
approach is just as valid as a structured programming approach.
Sometimes the top-down method of programming is more streamlined and
maybe even faster.  At our shop we hardly ever perform a pre-read in an
initialization routine; In fact, I havent seen one yet.
I can see nothing wrong with using a "GO TO" to skip over a section of
code.
  COBOL is a language not a religion!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: searching files into a table

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398708BC.F8D664C2@home.com>`

```


Wulf Gollin-Ott wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can anyone help me?

Wulf,

You should be able to adapt the following to your needs. Then you could
fill a listbox with an ordered/sorted collection - check also File
common dialog. I don't use it but I think you will find an example of a
file tree in the module Dialog System. 

Jimmy, Calgary AB

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Class : Create Ascii - I'm using NetExpress to convert some old RM/COBOL
74 data files to temporary files, so that I can generate one
ascii-delimited from info in three data files. Having produced the 
ascii-delimited now I want to delete those temporary files :-

*>-------------------------------------------------------------- 
Method-id. "delete-files".                                       
*>-------------------------------------------------------------  
Working-storage section.                                         
                                                                 
*> Total length must be 50 chars :-                              
                                                                 
01 ws-TempName.                                                  
   05                       pic x(12) value "c:\ndt\temp\".      
   05 wst-ClientFacility    Pic x(07).                           
   05                       pic x(03) value "*.*".               
01 ws-WildCardName          pic x(50).                           
                                                                 
Local-storage section.                                           
01 ls-program                   object reference.                
Procedure Division.                                              
   invoke FileNames2 "new" returning ls-program                  
   move   ws-ClientFacility       to wst-ClientFacility          
   move   ws-TempName             to ws-WildcardName             
   invoke ls-program "delete-using-wildcard"                     
          using ws-WildCardName                                  
   invoke ls-program "finalize" returning ls-program             
                                                                 
Exit Method                                                      
End Method "delete-files".                                       
*>-------------------------------------------------------------  

*>------------------ filenam2.cbl --------------------------     
                                                                 
Class-id.       FileNames2                                       
                inherits from Base.                              
                                                                 
Class-control.  FileNames2           is class "filenam2"         
                                                                 
                CharacterArray       is class "chararry"         
                FileName             is class "filename"         
.                                                                
                                                                 
*> Coded as a separate class so that more methods can be added   
*> later                                                         
                                                                 
*>-------------------------------------------------------------- 
FACTORY.                                                         
*>-------------------------------------------------------------- 
Method-id. "new".                                                
*>-------------------------------------------------------------- 
Linkage Section.                                                 
01 lnk-self                     object reference.                
Procedure Division returning lnk-self.                           
  invoke super "new" returning lnk-self                          
End Method "new".                                                
*>-------------------------------------------------------------  
END FACTORY.                                                     
*>-------------------------------------------------------------  
OBJECT.                                                          
*>-------------------------------------------------------------- 
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                         
                                                                 
*>-------------------------------------------------------------- 
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.                                          
                                                                 
*>-------------------------------------------------------------  
Method-id. "delete-using-wildcard".                              
*>-------------------------------------------------------------  
Local-storage section.                                           
01 n                         pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-collection             object reference.                   
01 ls-filename               pic x(100).                         
01 ls-length                 pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-size                   pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-StatusCode             pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-string                 object reference.                   
                                                                 
Linkage section.                                                 
01 lnk-WildCardName          pic x(50).                          
Procedure Division using lnk-WildCardName.                       
                                                                 
 move length of lnk-WildCardName to ls-length                    
 invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"                         
        using     ls-length                                      
                  lnk-WildCardName                               
        returning ls-string                                      
 invoke FileName "expandwildcards" using     ls-string           
                                   returning ls-Collection       
 invoke ls-Collection "size" returning ls-size                   
                                                                 
 perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-size                 
    move spaces to ls-filename                                   
    invoke ls-Collection "at" using n returning ls-string        
    invoke ls-string "getValueZ" returning ls-filename           
    call "CBL_DELETE_FILE" using     ls-filename                 
                           returning ls-StatusCode               
 End-perform                                                     
                                                                 
 invoke ls-Collection "deepfinalize" returning ls-Collection     
                                                                 
End Method "delete-using-wildcard".                              
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "get-ascii-filenames".                                
*>-------------------------------------------------------------  
Local-storage section.                                           
01 n                         pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-filename               pic x(100).                         
01 ls-length                 pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-size                   pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-StatusCode             pic x(4) comp-5.                    
01 ls-string                 object reference.                   
                                                                 
Linkage section.                                                 
01 lnk-WildCardName          pic x(50).                          
01 lnk-collection            object reference.                   
                                                                 
Procedure Division using     lnk-WildCardName                    
                   returning lnk-collection.                     
                                                                 
 move length of lnk-WildCardName to ls-length                    
 invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"                         
        using     ls-length                                      
                  lnk-WildCardName                               
        returning ls-string                                      
 invoke FileName "expandwildcards" using     ls-string           
                                   returning lnk-Collection      
Exit Method                                                      
End Method "get-ascii-filenames".                                
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
                                                                 
End OBJECT.                                                      
End CLASS FileNames2.                                            
                                                                 
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: searching files into a table

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m6fj7$md2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39869970$1@news.teuto.net>`

```
Hi Wulf,

not a method but a function which may solve your problems:

X"91" function 69 - Scan Directory

X"91" function 69

Searches a directory for a given file specification.


call X"91" using result
                 function-code
                 parameter



Parameters:
result	Group item defined as:
    f-error	    pic x comp-x
    f-handle	    pic x(2) comp-x
    f-attrout	    pic x comp-x
    f-time	    pic x(2) comp-x
    f-date	    pic x(2) comp-x
    f-size	    pic x(4) comp-x
    f-fileout	    pic x(<n>)
function-code	pic x comp-x
parameter	Group item defined as:
    f-action	    pic x comp-x
    f-attrin	    pic x comp-x
    f-filein	    pic x(<m>)


you should refer to your online help for further details....

HTH - Stefan Meyer

with the
> filenames from a selected directory, eg c:\*.dat.

#### ↳ Re: searching files into a table

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m6fg8$ghe$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<39869970$1@news.teuto.net>`

```
Hi Wulf,

You can use the X"91" function 69 call to get the files that are in a
directory. The various parameters and options are documented in the online
help.

Regards
David.
