Ooh, I misunderstood about the 5-inch disks, maybe.. I thought he had LOST
them.

Well, WDS, if you still have your disks, I have a working 5-1/4 drive on my
old system (reads/writes 360K and 1.2M) and could put the files onto 3-1/2
(720 or 1.44). Send email if you wish to pursue.

See, I KNEW keeping that old system with the 5.25" floppy was smart!

(Also, I still do some *real* legacy work and my backups are on 5.25". My
clients are "supposed" to have their source code backed up, but just in
case....)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Out of touch - Need help

- **From:** "Tim Hillock" <timhillock@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HOqh5.239388$7o1.6418923@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<dnYg5.2262$Oy3.43713@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com> <3985f66f.11846141@news1.frb.gov> <A0qh5.1626$3X1.442368@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I still have a 5-inch on my machine as well.   I still have some old
programs on them.

I still have this drive, because  I haven't saved them on a 3 1/2.   Just
recently I started to back them up on a drive which I will then write to a
cd.   When I first got a PC most programs came on the 5-inch.  Don't forget,
after buying original programs, and then latter on you buy the upgrade
package, these look for the previous version.  My old WP 5.1 and Word 2
programs are on 5-inch.   My current computer is over 4 yrs old.  It's going
to be rough when I upgrade to a new one.   One thing for sure is that I'll
buy the full version OS whatever it is at that time.  I'm not going to try
to save a few bucks by reloading from my old Dos 5.0 and Win 3.0 disks (both
5'ers).

At one point you will have to decide to bite the bullet and get the full
package instead of getting the new upgrade package.   Depending on what it
is,  it could be a difference of a few hundred dollars.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Out of touch - Need help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3986D18D.BC388209@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<dnYg5.2262$Oy3.43713@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com> <3985f66f.11846141@news1.frb.gov> <A0qh5.1626$3X1.442368@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <HOqh5.239388$7o1.6418923@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Tim Hillock wrote:
> 
> I still have a 5-inch on my machine as well.   I still have some old
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> is,  it could be a difference of a few hundred dollars.

Sometimes, you can work with the vendor to get around this.  There are
shops which do not allow floppy drives at all.  But I have some software
which I have upgraded several times from its first purchase on
floppies.  When I needed to reinstall once after removing it entirely,
it simply asked for any earlier version - I put in the previous
version's upgrade CD, it checked, and then continued installation.

On the other hand, I remember once installing a version of DOS,
upgrading twice, then installing Windows 3.1, and finally replacing it
with Windows 95.

Nowadays, with internet upgrades, I am more worried.  I back up my
application, but if it doesn't have a SETUP file, that won't do me much
good when I move it to a new computer, or have to format and reinstall
my OS partition.  Most installation routines adjust the registry and
create icons to run the products.

It is obvious that Microsoft doesn't expect us to move applications (and
data) from an old machine to a new one.
```

IMO Microsoft expects us to buy new applications when we buy new PCs.

>See, I KNEW keeping that old system with the 5.25" floppy was smart!
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Anytime I get a new system without a 5.25 drive , I replace the 3.5 with a 
combo drive that has both formats.  It's only a few dollars more than a 
standard single drive and uses only one set of power and drive connectors.
Works like a charm.
The only down side is that I cannot switch the drive designation
without having to open the case.  Someday they might think of putting a 
slide switch on the drive front to alter the jumper settings that control
which is drive A and B.
```

> A couple of ideas may get you started.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

One thing though.  I once wanted to reinstall a large program, I can't
remember whether it was dBASE IV or Borland C++.  My diskettes were 5ï¿½",
so I went somewhere and copied each diskette to a 3ï¿½" floppy.

When I tried installing, it went OK for a few diskettes and then stopped
when it didn't find the file it was looking for on the correct
diskette.   The installation program was expecting a 3ï¿½" installation.  
So created an installation subdirectory, copied all of the files from
the floppies to there, and installed from there.   Good thing I had lots
of disk space.
```

## Thread: Display upon — 5 this month / 8 total

_Thread started [2000-07](2000-07.md#t-8fc8a5ecf6)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Display upon

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3986CE3E.297DF46B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net> <8m55kj$2veq$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <8m56he$8bi$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I believe that Howard uses a COBOL compiler without the EXTENSION of a
> "screen section".  That is not a part of ANSI/ISO COBOL (but will be in the
> next revision).

I use mainframes (currently IBM).  A co-worker mentioned how she had to
do a bit of debugging in the production environment.  She put in some
displays which puzzled some users who saw them mixed with some output. 
Admittedly it might be a good idea to have standards that ALL user
output be to standard output files and not displayed.

But even still, it might be nice for debugging purposes to have
different displays going to different locations.  We just have them
going to SYSOUX.

In the olden days, installations were adamant about DISPLAY UPON CONSOLE
causing pages of debugging data to go on the console typewriter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Display upon

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m6mm2$ij0$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net> <8m55kj$2veq$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com> <8m56he$8bi$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3986CE3E.297DF46B@brazee.net>`

```
In all (current and past) IBM COBOL compilers, you may specify DIFFERENT
locations in the DISPLAY UPON statement.  These are "associated" with
external media.  You might also want to look at the OUTDD compiler option
(available since VS COBOL II).
```

#### ↳ Re: Display upon

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m6kqh$5pv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net>`

```
Funnily enough, Howard, I am finding that I am going back to the
original usage of the DISPLAY.  As I GUI-ize, the console becomes redundant.
That allows me to use it exclusively for debug information, fatal errors,
etc., much like an operator's console.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net>...
>The history of the DISPLAY command makes it less useful than it might.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>set-name.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Display upon

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<COJh5.17993$RG6.1485775@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net> <8m6kqh$5pv$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Interesting.  When COBOL was first designed, there were only output
printers, and console printers.  Only IBM used their card oriented line
printers and JCL so that some control could be exercised. RRand used a RR
15 cps typewriter, and we called it a console for use as an operators
console.  Any messages not directed to the operator had to appear on a High
Speed Printer listing OR the typewriter page was divided into vertical
sections where output in each section had designated use.

Warren Simmons

news:8m6kqh$5pv$1@news.igs.net...

redundant.

> etc., much like an operator's console.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-2ef6945a89"></a>
## Thread: anyone using any variant of OOCOBOL? — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

_Thread started [2000-07](2000-07.md#t-2ef6945a89)._

#### ↳ Re: anyone using any variant of OOCOBOL?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m6jdr$4pa$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8m48db$drt$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
Yes, and I am converting legacy systems.

Mike Sheehan wrote in message <8m48db$drt$1@kermit.esat.net>...
>Hi,
>Are there any individuals or companies who have implemented OOCobol?
>
>- What implementation\compiler are you using?

Fujitsu.

>- How did you find the change in paradigm i.e. using OO features?

Not difficult.  The real change was in having enough machine resources
to build **really LARGE** systems, more a result of hardware evolution than
software.

>- How has it affected the way you code in COBOL?

It has allowed me to take a much more "bottom up" approach to
system design.

>- What are the advantages of using OOCOBOL rather than switching to
>something else?

The biggest advantage that I was looking for was the ability to get to
the imbedded part of the system without exiting back up, then back
down a menu tree.  Example -- old system: I am entering an order
for a contract, for a customer, and I discover that the product the
customer wants is not in the product master.  I need to exit back to
the main menu, and correct, then re-enter the contract update module.

New system: the product handling object contains *everything* for
product maintenance, including creating a new product master listing.
I am in the contract module, and I invoke the "check-product-present"
method.  On failure, I am in position to add, get a new listing, then
when I exit stack fashion, continue on where I left off.

As to the COBOL as opposed to other languages, the answers are the
same as they have always been.

>I'm writing a dissertation at present on OOCOBOL and transfering legacy
>systems...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Well, I never thought it would spark off such ineresting threads!! I have
certainly been taking notes ; )
In some of the threads there was mention of programmers using either the
`windows standard` use of keys for example ctrl+v for pasting and ctrl+c for
copy etc, and others quite rightly so, said they would program so that the
the number keypad is being used for rapid data input.Surely some of you guys
have thought of having the user choose what keys will do what from within
the program itself. I don`t mean by asking the user during the design stage,
I mean letting the user select the keys that they find are most comfortable
to use, while the program is running. It`s done with every game that is
designed for PC. If a user is not happy about a certian key configuration,
then let them change it. Problem solved!!

Colonel
psxzone@btinternet.com

(ps I don`t mean to stand on anyones toes - just thinking out loud)
```

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Well, the hook is there, but waiting for further code.  There are only about
three or four keys that I consider absolutely essential uniformity-wise
across
a system.  Those are:  the back up/abort  (I use the Escape to always mean
DO NOT PROCEED), the go ahead, I am done (the Enter key), the help key
(I am using windows standard F1), and the various cursor/tab control keys.
The rest are all fluff ... nice to have but not essential.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Storing user configurable keystrokes

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398D4E25.8DAF223F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <2q6i5.129$l36.81073@nnrp1.sbc.net> <Cm%i5.4160$ho5.54836@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Colonel wrote:
> 
> Well, I never thought it would spark off such ineresting threads!! I have
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> then let them change it. Problem solved!!

There is a down side:

What happens when the user reprograms the keys so they simply do not
have a combination?

What happens when they call support and are using non standard
keystrokes?  Now press F4 to clear the screen.

What happens when others 'help' people out?

What about storing those keystrokes,  how do you create a user
profile?  Do you have limited space?  Store in registry and I have
three machines.

These are considerations,  not to dampen your enthusiasm, more to show
why this is not normally down.

I think it is mandatory to allow modification of some keys,  there are
some base configurations that must be kept standard,  Ctrl-C and
Ctrl-V for paste and copy is one example of this.

At work someone has overridden the user template normal.dot in word
and disabled some keystrokes in it.  It is really annoying and since
we have two template libraries I cannot override it personally.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

```

time. If a user prefers a different config, it would be no big deal to
choose the config they desire. There could also be a `return all keys to
default` key which could be invoked should everything go completely wrong or
the user gets him/herself lost.

Colonel

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3988431d.1476923@news>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 02 Aug 2000 02:20:54 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>But, to prove GUIs can be done in COBOL look at latest article in
>cobolreport.com "Do You want a Revolution". 

Jimmy,

An interesting article, and while I don't necessarily agree with the
choice of UI tools <G>, the author made a number of good points.  

He was absolutely on target with respect to the difficulty of
designing consistent (and more-or-less Windows compliant) panels.
Just because you have a Windows UI tool doesn't mean you know how to
design a screen that looks like a "standard" Windows application.  

Given the number of things you can do in a GUI versus a character mode
UI, it takes a lot of care - and discipline - to get it right.  The
Microsoft Windows design guide covers a great deal of ground and some
of the behavior of a properly designed Windows application that we
take for granted is extremely subtle.

BTW, the mention of Object COBOL in the article puzzled me a little.
I thought that Merant dropped it a couple of years ago...

Kevin
Support@ScreenIO.com
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

> I thought that Merant dropped it a couple of years ago...

Micro Focus (before it became MERANT) did drop the product CALLED "Visual
Object COBOL".  However, they are a STRONG supporter of "Object COBOL" in
their Net Express product line.   I have looked at the article and it (fairly
clearly) is talking about "an object COBOL product" and not "the Visual
Object COBOL product".

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39888738.18912464@news>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net>`

```
On Wed, 02 Aug 2000 13:21:43 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>Why should anybody program using Microsoft's low level API's in the era
>of objects?  Isn't that what OO is all about, buy or create a generic
>panel and then forever just plug in the variables?
>
>(I am asking this from a degree of ignorance).

Howard,

A minor misunderstanding.  I'm definitely not suggesting anyone use
the Windows API for developing applications.  You'd be crazy to do
that at the application level.  OO or a third party tool is the only
way to go.

My comments (and those of the aforementioned article's author) related
more to the aesthetics and function of the GUI screens, not how they
were implemented.  

For example, just because you CAN relocate the scrollbars to the
top/left of your window instead of the standard bottom/right does not
mean that you SHOULD.  Ditto for, say, putting the menu on the bottom
instead of at the top of your window or for overriding the
Windows-assigned colors..  

Anyone developing a Windows application should take a real close look
at how other "standard" Windows apps behave before you get too far
along.  As I said before, many of the things we take for granted are
not that obvious until you really look at how standard Windows apps
look and feel.

NWPs (New Windows Programmers) do some pretty nonstandard (one might
say weird) stuff sometimes.  

Kevin

Support@ScreenIO.com
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39897e5c.4283227@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <39888738.18912464@news>`

```
Kevin is absolutely correct when he says that just because you can
incorporate your own personal design preferences into your GUI screens
doesn't mean that you SHOULD do it.

Although many of the "Windows default settings" don't necessarily
enhance data entry productivity, your design consideration should be:

1.  Who is using the system? and
2.  What is the purpose of the system?

General rule of thumb:

If the system is designed for a group of employees in which there is a
high degree of turnover, stick closely to the Windows defaults to help
minimize the learning curve.

If the system is designed for high volume data entry, turn the mouse
off and force keyboard navigation or field autotabbing, if possible.
Nothing kills productivity more than the end user typing, then moving
their hand to the mouse to move to the next field.

If the system is designed for minimal data entry and heavy browsing,
the mouse can be very useful, for example, an executive information
system.

As if things weren't complicated enough, now we have user interface
design considerations for web browser based user interface systems.
This really throws a monkey wrench into the works, because web browser
interfaces are being designed in every manner imaginable.

In addition, unless you use Javascript or a Java applet, forget about
the menu bar in your web browser interface.  Windows doesn't allow
child windows to support menu bars and pull down menus.

Sometimes I wonder whether we change the way we do things because it
is better or if we change the way we do things just because it is
different.
  

Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen) wrote:

>On Wed, 02 Aug 2000 13:21:43 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
>wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.screenio.com

Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net...
>
> Why should anybody program using Microsoft's low level API's in the era
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (I am asking this from a degree of ignorance).

In my case, I chose to learn "the API" way (in BASIC) for one very good
reason: all the "object" methods are proprietary to a specific compiler, and
I do not like being locked in to one vendor.

Another consideration was that I am interested in doing COBOL for Windows at
some point, and COBOL and BASIC are very similar in the verbs/methods used
to control flow, handle files, do arithmetic, etc, and I thought it might be
easier to stick with what I know in COBOL and BASIC, and need only learn
only one way - the API way - to handle the Windows user interface.

The Windows API is impressive in speed, functionality and quality.

Lastly, if you go "the API way" you don't have to ship all those support
DLL's and worry about compatibility and versioning and all that sh^h^hjazz.
If Windows is installed, your code runs, period.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00

- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
>news:398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net...

Well Michael, I do not often find myself in disagreement with you, but this
time, you hit about 6 buttons in a row<G>

>>
>> Why should anybody program using Microsoft's low level API's in the era
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>reason: all the "object" methods are proprietary to a specific compiler,
and
>I do not like being locked in to one vendor.
>

NON! NO! NO!  The exact opposite. The API is proprietary to one OS, MS
Windows,
and you are at the mercy of OS upgrades.  There are dozens of ways of
aleviating both
that and the problem of compiler dependency that rely on *not* using the
API. There is
no way to avoid it with API calls.

Examples:

Third party tools such as SP2.  That allows you to simply reconfigure to
switch OS.
A compiler change will present far more problems with standard Cobol thatn
the change
in the GUI.  To change the GUI, you change DLLs.

Introducing a level of abtraction.  If you code your own set of GUI basic
components using
OOP Cobol, you can write all your business code in your own OOP.  I have
about ten basic
objects in my own OOP screen I/O object.  All my source code is written in
that.  To convert
my code, I convert my screen DLL. I can test in an either/or fashion in two
windows with two
paths. It is simple to change platforms, or even to CGI. The same program
that runs on a
PC can run as a web server, no code change, just swap DLL's.

None of that is available with straight API code.

>Another consideration was that I am interested in doing COBOL for Windows
at
>some point, and COBOL and BASIC are very similar in the verbs/methods used
>to control flow, handle files, do arithmetic, etc, and I thought it might
be
>easier to stick with what I know in COBOL and BASIC, and need only learn
>only one way - the API way - to handle the Windows user interface.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

products like ScreenIO and Dialog System...In fact, you will find them
usually lacking when compared to native API programming...

Using the APIs directly is nothing more than calling entry points...There is
a learning curve, but once you use them it is pretty easily repeated in
other projects...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mcnp1$c24$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8mam4n$jpf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8mbut0$aaf$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

> Well, I would call that to exagerate. It is no question that the API is
> useful, but to develop an entire application in it, I think that would be
to
> pull it too far.
>
> Cheesle
>

I suppose I am confused as to why you say using APIs for an entire
application "would be to pull it too far"...

I try to modularize my applications...Usually with a module to handle the
main client area of a program...Then various individual modules to handle
any dialogs that might be required.

With a little imagination and effort, one can hide almost all of the API
processing from the business logic of an application...

I have developed a module that I call a "default dialog box procedure"...It
handles all the controls within a window/dialog, including binding the
contents of the controls to designated database fields in working
storage...This module handles 99 percent of the gui (all via APIs)...

My logic puts this module in charge...Every message passed from windows to
an individual dialog procedure is passed to this module...This module takes
care of the rest...It will in turn call the  associated routines for reading
a record, for updating a record, for listing the records, for editing the
records, etc...

The individual dialog procedure deals almost entirely with application code
and logic, not the user interface APIs...It is very much like visual
basic...I define an array for the controls including the address of the
database element associated with the control, the type of control...etc...

I can display a screen (I like using pages in a TabPro notebook control with
the database records listed on the left page and fields for the selected
database record on the right page) which will:

---list all records in a database

---allow the user to select and display the contents of a particular record

---ensure that each element is edited within defined guidelines

---display a descriptive message informing the user what the control is when
the mouse passes over it

---change all fields in error with a red background and display an
appropriate error message in the status bar

---enable/disable the Top, Bottom, Next, Previous, Refresh, OK, Cancel, Add,
and Delete buttons when appropriate (also enables and disables applicable
menu items)

---allow the user to update the currently selected record (only when there
are no editing errors)

---allow the user to delete the currently selected record

---allow the user to add a new record

---automatically rescale the entire application window/dialogs and all
controls within when it is maximized, resized, restored, etc


ALL this and more with only one (I.e. "1") (i.e. a single) API call in the
entire module...All COBOL, All non OO, but very much modular code...

Believe it or not...no evaluates, no message processing, etc...The API
processing is VERY MUCH separated from the business logic...

kenmullins
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<8mcq9r$dnk$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8mam4n$jpf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8mbut0$aaf$1@news.cerf.net> <8mcnp1$c24$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Ken,
  I may be missing something - but it sounds to me that what you are doing is
to BASICALLY just "duplicate" what ScreenIO, Sp2, - and even Acucorp *could*
do for you.

You are creating your own "API access modules" that do all the low-level
stuff for you.  This allows you to separate your business logic from your UI
logic. Sounds good to me.

HOWEVER,  it also sounds like you could do the exact same thing using one of
the 3rd party (well I guess Acucorp is a "direct product") that will do all
of this for you.

What am I missing?  Are you saying it is good to code at the API level or
not?  If so, how do you gain over those who use "pre-made" routines where you
have made your own?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mctg2$hkc$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8mam4n$jpf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8mbut0$aaf$1@news.cerf.net> <8mcnp1$c24$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8mcq9r$dnk$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think it is good to code at the API level, yes...This is where you have
the most flexibility in developing a Windows or OS/2 application...Of course
when I started coding for OS/2, there were no 3rd party products available
for the GUI...

None of the mentioned 3rd party UI products are all inclusive in a Windows
or OS/2 environment...There are lot's of functions that these products
cannot perform which will be needed in most larger applications
eventually...

How many applications can be developed with these products without also
having to also use APIs for something...TCP Sockets, Shared memory access,
COM port access, compression, mutexes, multi-threading, communicating
between separate windows modules, graphics, reading parameters from INI
files, etc...Eventually a windows programmer is going to HAVE to use some of
the Windows APIs directly...

I'm not saying people should not use these 3rd party UI products...But I am
saying that people seem to run in the other direction when API UI is
mentioned...Some people in this thread have even out right stated that a
programmer would be crazy to use windows APIs...

<<Kevin Hansen stated>> "I'm definitely not suggesting anyone use the
Windows API for developing applications. You'd be crazy to do that at the
application level. OO or a third party tool is the only way to go."

or

<<Chessle stated>> "It is no question that the API is useful, but to develop
an entire application in it, I think that would be to pull it too far."

It's not crazy to use APIs...a 3rd party UI tool is NOT the ONLY way to
go...It is one way to go, yes, but not the ONLY way to go...

I believe the native APIs are more efficient than a lot of the 3rd party
products available...NE's Dialog System seems extremely slow in comparison
to native API programming. I tried looking at GUIScreenIO and consistently
received abends. Never tried SP2...

Some people here have also implied that using APIs cannot be hidden from the
business portion of an application...NOT TRUE...The UI can be hidden just as
well using APIs as it can with any of the 3rd party UI products.

Like I said, the original poster asked a question, and I am simply
responding with how I do UIs...API programming should not be feared...The
more one understands about how the native APIs work, the better off they
will be when something goes wrong...

Just stating my opinion...

kenmullins

A mutex is a mechanism for providing "mutually exclusive" access to
resources. They are primarily intended to enable thread synchronization
across multiple processes. Mutexes differ from all other synchronization
objects (semaphores, events,...) because they are owned by a thread so they
recover gracefully if the owning thread dies.

- Karl

It is similar yes...With a windows mutex, you can in effect enqueue on some
shared resource...Once EnQueued upon, other tasks or threads must wait until
that resource is freed or DeQueued...

kenmullins

Mutexes are actually very generic,  they occur in all operating
systems that allow multiple threads to run at once.  Yes the enqueue
and dequeue are variants of them.  I do not know the specific syntax
of enqueue and dequeue, can you enqueue on an imaginary object?  If so
then this is exactly what it is.

```
Mutexes are used to control access to shared resources such as a com port or
data between multiple processes or applications...

A program can wait on a mutex to ensure that it has exclusive access to the
data...Used very frequently when you have several things going at the same
resource...

kenmullins

"Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote in
message news:8mcnp1$c24$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> I suppose I am confused as to why you say using APIs for an entire
> application "would be to pull it too far"...

To be short, I am impressed with what you have done. The reason I feel it is
to pull it too far is because using the API rather than the native support
(at least in Acucobol context) is way more extensive without really giving
me much extra.

Consider for instance doing a statusbar control using the API, I would have
to deal with:
    canvas
    item list
    events
    memory
    styles

While in Acucobol it all can be done with one statement. I am most positive
this is the case for a lot of other Cobol vendors too.

But, then again, as you said, making your own copy books would significantly
simplify it, but it is never the less extra work.

> ALL this and more with only one (I.e. "1") (i.e. a single) API call in the
> entire module...All COBOL, All non OO, but very much modular code...

As I said, it is impressive, but is it worth the work?

Cheesle
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8mam4n$jpf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <8mbut0$aaf$1@news.cerf.net> <8mcnp1$c24$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8meqbn$i4g$1@news.cerf.net>`

Cheesle wrote:
> 
> "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote in
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yes I think it probably is 'worth the work' depending on your approach.
I don't know specifics, but both using N/E and I believe both using M/F
Dialog Editor (not the MS version), Ken and I have arrived at fairly
parallel routes - although he obviously achieved his some time ago
(probably at Win 3.1 ?).

By no means does my current model handle every conceivable Windows
control, just those I'm immediately interested in - but with Class
MyDialog I pass parameters(messages) from invoking business-logic
classes and let MyDialog do the grunt work. (As Thane has observed,
probably similar to what both Bob and Kevin are doing with SP2 and
ScreenIO respectively). 

Any mistakes I have encountered, so far, were all in the 'business'
classes; my UI(MyDialog) at this time appears to be fairly solid. It can
easily be expanded to include say check-boxes or any other gizmo I want
to add. In my case I arrived at MyDialog going the arduous route of
following N/E demos - but very uncomfortable. Having got beyond
concentrating on 'how to do it' for each new task, the penny dropped, I
was doing the same thing over and over again - shortcut - MyDialog.

In theory it should be possible to fit into MyDialog - but with GUI
classes I have the ability to design my own controls using draw/paint
classes - which of course are in COBOLese. There is one correspondent
who doesn't use the Dialog Editor I refer to above - he does all his
windowing dynamically using GUI classes.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39898fe4.528269@news>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8mam4n$jpf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Wed, 2 Aug 2000 22:36:08 -0400, "Ken Mullins"
<**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote:

>Whole heartedly agree with the API way...There is really no need for
>products like ScreenIO and Dialog System...In fact, you will find them
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>other projects...

Ken (and you other API advocates),

You are correct that using the Windows API is "simply" a matter of
calling entry points, but you grossly oversimplify what's involved in
Windows programming.

If you're going to develop an application that does all of its UI at
the API level, you're going to have to get into writing Windows
procedures, handling messages (at various levels), managing (and
accessing) shared memory, and many other things that are definitely
not for the timid.  You need to deal with recursion, which is not
available in all COBOL compilers and is a can of worms itself. 

You also need to deal with differences in how the various versions of
Windows work - and, yes, they ARE different.  

Windows API programming is not for the entry-level programmer - in
COBOL or any other language.

A low-level UI is arguably the most complex part of an application -
why not concentrate your programming energy on the application logic
and take advantage of UI tools that make the UI an SEP?  

If you followed your premise to its logical conclusion, you would use
assembler instead of COBOL because it's more powerful - but no one
does that anymore because they'd be struggling with their assembler
long after the competition's product developed in COBOL is bringing in
$$$.  Assembler would be inappropriate for a business DP application,
and so is using the API for your UI.

You should use the appropriate tool for the job - when I need to drill
a hole for some non-precision application, I use my Milwaukee hole
shooter instead of boring it with my mill.  

Well, most of the time, anyway... <G>

Regards,
Kevin
Support@ScreenIO.com
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

```
"Kevin J. Hansen" <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote in message
news:39898fe4.528269@news...
> On Wed, 2 Aug 2000 22:36:08 -0400, "Ken Mullins"
> <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote:
>
 <snip>
>
> If you're going to develop an application that does all of its UI at
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just curious ...
  Are there ANY of the "current-release" COBOL compilers that run under
Windows 95 or later that do NOT support recurssion?  I know that Fujitsu,
IBM, and MERANT all do.  I am not positive about Acucobol, RM, or CA. (I
assume PerCOBOL does - but that is just an assumption.)

Certainly, I think that any compiler that supports "BY VALUE" and "RETURNING"
in their CALL statements *also* support recursion.  All these are not
related, I think they would ALL be necessary if you were to create a Windows
API driven application.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recursion (was: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3989d481.201677909@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8mam4n$jpf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <39898fe4.528269@news> <8mcg45$ded$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Thu, 3 Aug 2000 14:11:26 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:


>
>Just curious ...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>assume PerCOBOL does - but that is just an assumption.)

Through V5 Fujtisu DOES NOT support recursion outside Object
Orientation.  There is no way with "Standard" Fujitsu COBOL to create
the mainline necessary to call the Windows Create_window API to start
an application processing messages from a window.  Fujitsu does not
offer a Procedure-pointer or a way to pass the entrypoint for the
message processing program to the API.  One must use PowerCOBOL or a
third party screen handler.  Once the main window is established, the
full Windows API is available, but there Is no recursion for a
"regular" (Non OO) COBOL program.

(At least publicized - there may be an undocumented runtime option
that allows recursion).


>
>Certainly, I think that any compiler that supports "BY VALUE" and "RETURNING"
>in their CALL statements *also* support recursion.  All these are not
>related, I think they would ALL be necessary if you were to create a Windows
>API driven application.

Fujitsu just started supporting returning with v5.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mcd8b$r7l$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <398874C7.F107FFC3@brazee.net> <cS2i5.896$NI2.337780@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <8mam4n$jpf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <39898fe4.528269@news>`

```

> Ken (and you other API advocates),
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> available in all COBOL compilers and is a can of worms itself.

Well, I do remember mentioning that there is indeed a learning curve...But
once this has happened, windows API programming is just like most other
programming...You can cut and paste from a working module to accomplish the
same things in a new module...

If someone can learn how to do OO programming or interface with various 3rd
party products for handling the UI, then they can just as easily learn to do
native API programming...In fact I have found that a typical program only
actually needs to deal with a very very small percentage of the APIs that
are actually available...

I think I also mentioned that NE comes with some examples on how to get
started using APIs...

The API calls can be encapsulated into OO objects...OR...They can be
contained within subroutines or DLLs...Personally I use a "Common Routines"
module for most API functions...For example to enable a window I use

Call WinAPI crEnableWindow
   using by value hWndWindow.

or

Invoke crEnableWindow
   WindowHandle is hWndWindow.

The code for entry point 'crEnableWindow' is a call or perhaps many calls to
the operating system API(s). I have a common routines module for Windows and
one for OS/2...The actual user app code does not change...I also reduce the
complication of dealing with the API(s) by having routines in the common
routines module that performs one, several or even many actual API
calls...For example opening and initializing a COM port...This might require
5 different API calls...But from my app, I simplify that to a single call :

Call WinAPI crComOpen
   using by reference z'COM1'
         by reference z'19200,N,8,1'
returning wsPortHandle.

Many call(s) get made to the actual operating system APIs, but my app is
coded using a very simple call to the crComOpen entry point. Once these
common routines are developed (and it will take some time to develop) the UI
or interface to OS functions like reading and writing to com ports, etc is
greatly simplified.

Window procedures and callbacks again are nothing more than entry points
that Windows calls with associated parameters passed...These can be
encapsulated into separate modules as well...Recursion and messaging is a
fact of life in a windows environment...An understanding of how these things
work is a requirement that cannot be avoided no matter how you handle the
user interface...

I suppose in summary, someone who is inquiring about what direction to take
in their future development should explore ALL the various options that are
available to them...API programming is one of those options available and
works well...They should not be intimidated by vendors of UI products
telling them how complicated API programming is, when in reality, it is not
really that complicated, ONCE the learning curve is completed and messaging,
recursion and multiple entry points is understood...Programming in windows
or OS/2 is going to require a learning curve regardless of the UI method
employed...Be it API calls directly, OO invokes and property settings,
Activex, or ScreenIO or SP2...There is still a learning curve to be dealt
with...The original poster will need to decide which method he wants to
use...His decision needs to be based on fact finding and asking questions
and getting responses...

After all, what is a programmer going to do when he encounters something
that cannot be done by a 3rd party UI product? He is going to need to
understand how windows or OS/2 works and that there are APIs to do what the
3rd party product(s) cannot do...Does ScreenIO or SP2 deal with reading and
writing com ports, semaphores, mutexes, pipes, TCP sockets, file sharing,
networking, shared memory between modules, threads, displaying graphics,
automatic scaling of windows and dialogs for different screen resolutions or
maximized vs a normal window, owner drawn controls, compression and
expansion of files or data, etc? These are common windows or OS/2 functions
that will eventually be needed in a lot of business applications...May as
well get started with the learning curve now...

:-)

BTW, I am not opposed to using 3rd party products by any means...I use the
NE dialog editor, ListPro, TabPro, SocketTools, the Victor Graphic
libraries, etc on a daily basis to make my life easier...The nice thing is
all these 3rd party products work just like calling the normal windows
operating system APIs and they conform to the normal windows programming
methods (message loops, recursion, sync or async processing)...I am not
restricted because my windows program is based entirely on a specific 3rd
party UI product...There is NO single 3rd party product that can accomplish
what all of the various tools and operating system API's can accomplish...

kenmullins
```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:cS2i5.896
> Lastly, if you go "the API way" you don't have to ship all those support
> DLL's and worry about compatibility and versioning and all that
sh^h^hjazz.
> If Windows is installed, your code runs, period.

Unfortunately, it is not always so. There are a number of versions of the
API DLL's out there, and there might be features you use that are not
supported by all. This becomes particularly true if you are developing for
all Windows variants, not to forget 16 bits Windows. As an example, I can
tell you that to obtain Windows default printer, Win 3.x, Win95, Win NT and
Win 2000 together provides 4 -FOUR- different methods of getting the exact
same result...

Needless to say, new features are not present in older DLL's, hence, you
might need updated DLLs. Anyway, in general, I agree with you, the Windows
API is powerful.

Cheesle
```

Kevin J. Hansen wrote in message <3988431d.1476923@news>...
>
>He was absolutely on target with respect to the difficulty of
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I found the above rather amusing.  During the design phase, I have spent
dozens of hours making sure that the GUI does *NOT* look like a MS
standard product.  I write typical Cobol projects ... the people using them
run them 8 hours per day while talking on the phone and looking out the
window.  They use the hex pad and the function keys on full auto without
a glance at the screen or the keyboard.  MS "style" makes that impossible,
and slows them down to about 30% of their normal speed.

All my code has my own distinctive style, and can be run easily with
keyboard
only, or with keyboard and mouse, or with mouse and the odd bit of keyboard
for data entry.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39890F3F.17440E66@home.com>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <8mac6a$cte$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> Kevin J. Hansen wrote in message <3988431d.1476923@news>...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> for data entry.

Valid points Kevin quotes from original article. But I'm with Don on
being 'non-Microsoft'. People tend to slavishly follow the standard set
by Redmond University - and Billy G. loves it. I prefer to think of
Windoze as another tool for screening like Screen Section. (OK that's a
bit simplistic because you can do so much more in the Windows
environment).

I sat down and figured out what I wanted to do - seeing some examples
from a text "APIs How to...". Admittedly mine is a one-focus product -
if you are sat in the back of a van taking readings with an ultrasonic
instrument, I think we can safely assume you don't want to jump into
WordPerfect, a spreadsheet, database package, or God know what else -
concentrate on the task in hand.

So I designed and programmed. I grab the whole screen, probably the
approach most people will have taken with Screen Section. User came
across and liked it, and his comment, "Much better(easier) than those
silly systems I see downtown in the glass-concrete oil patch buildings,
people to-ing and fro-ing in Windows  between silly little dialog boxes
dancing up all over the place".

One of my pet-hates - that bloody right double-click bringing up a set
of goodies you didn't know about - sorry you are SOL with me. If I need
it, to pique your interest, I include another pushbutton <Other>.

Yes - and because my guy ALWAYS used the NumPad - I use the ENTER at the
end of field-entries rather than slavishly following the TAB approach.
(That caused no end of aggravation, having to get back to the SDK, - MS
acknowledges the problem by suggesting THREE solutions - this was
followed by questions to Micro Focus, before I could do it).

Jimmy
```

Don:

This is precisely what I was referring to in my previous post.

Although there can be real advantages in designing the user interface
to look and behave exactly like a typical Microsoft application, *for
certain types of users and certain types of applications*, you
obviously gave a high degree of consideration to who was using the
system and how they were using it.

You chose to design the user interface of your system based upon
ergonomic principals as opposed to forcing the user interface to look
and behave exactly like a typical Microsoft application.


"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>
>Kevin J. Hansen wrote in message <3988431d.1476923@news>...

Bob Wolfe, flexus

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3989C15B.DF7DDB47@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <8mac6a$cte$1@news.igs.net> <39898241.5280267@news.epix.net>`

```


Bob Wolfe wrote:
> 
> Don:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> and behave exactly like a typical Microsoft application.

So you want the ability to separate your business rules from your front
end.  You can change your front end to meet your customer's changing
needs.  This would indicate that you do NOT want your business rule
COBOL to make low level calls to the Windows API, but would rather point
to a high level interface which can be tweaked or replaced system wide.  

Now my capstone project was anti-OO, and I have never written OO Cobol,
but I am not so much a bigot to fail to recognize when OO makes sense. 
Don't write your windows - write to a high level windows interface.  Let
someone else update the interface when you go to a new API.
```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a high level interface which can be tweaked or replaced system wide. 

Howard:

Yes...and I think that separation of the business rules from the front
end has been accomplished by Ken.  Ken's modular approach does keep
the UI and business logic separate.

I also believe that what Bill and others have indicated is that some
of the vendors already provide a similar capability.  Although it's
likely that none of the vendors will provide access to every low level
Windows API function, most provide access to those which are most
commonly used.

That when combined with high level support for Active X controls does
provide the capability to access virtually every function through the
use of OCX controls.

Obviously in some cases it is better to write your own code instead of
utilizing an existing product, but the most significant downside to
that approach is the fact that the operating system UI will change.
And at some point it will change significantly, perhaps controlled by
a vendor other than Microsoft (perish the thought!!).

If and when that happens, it's faster, easier and less expensive for
the COBOL programmer to plug in a new UI module written by their
preferred vendor than to re-write every module which directly invokes
the low level UI functions.

>Now my capstone project was anti-OO, and I have never written OO Cobol,
>but I am not so much a bigot to fail to recognize when OO makes sense. 
>Don't write your windows - write to a high level windows interface.  Let
>someone else update the interface when you go to a new API.

That's exactly what I am suggesting!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3989989c.2760399@news>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <8mac6a$cte$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Wed, 2 Aug 2000 19:48:41 -0400, "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
wrote:

>I found the above rather amusing.  During the design phase, I have spent
>dozens of hours making sure that the GUI does *NOT* look like a MS
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>for data entry.

Donald,

Well, it depends...  I agree completely with those of you who are
developing applications for specific requirements; high-volume data
entry, special-purpose stuff used by, um, unsophisticated users - that
sort of thing.

That makes all the sense in the world.

BUT...  If you're developing an application that'll be used by people
familiar with Windows, it'll drive 'em crazy if yours works
differently.  One of the nice things about Windows is the
standardization of the UI.  Menu at the top, toolbar below, standard
behaviors of controls, standard assignment of keys (e.g., Ctrl+C for
Copy, Ctrl+V for paste, that sort of thing).

I like the fact that clicking on that "X" in the upper right corner of
the window will close any Windows application.  In the bad old days of
DOS, I remember running popular applications that used all kiinds of
different key combinations to close them down or open files.  Every
new package required learning a new UI.  It was a PAIN!

One of the things that drives me nuts is apps that grossly deviate
from the standard when it's not necessary.  I suppose this could
relate to the developers ego....  You need to stop and figure out
which cutesy button (in an unexpected location) does what you want.
If the app used the standard design, it would be a breeze.

If you're targeting a specialized market, go ahead and do your own
thing - but if you're after the mass market you'll probably be more
successful if your product looks like a "true" Windows application.  

The point of my original comments was that, if you're trying to
develop an application that nominally conforms to the standard, you
need to familiarize yourself with the standard.  As I said, much of
the Windows behavior that we take for granted is quite subtle, and you
need to take a close look at it to get it "right".

Kevin
Support@ScreenIO.com
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

```


"Kevin J. Hansen" wrote:
> 
> Well, it depends...  I agree completely with those of you who are
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> new package required learning a new UI.  It was a PAIN!

Yeah, the Windows interface is far from perfect, but then again the
English language is far from perfect.  But it wouldn't be a service to
try to communicate in Esperanto (or whatever favorite language I have),
if it requires my listener that much up front work.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI Screens?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mc9sp$g6c$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<WHIh5.7523$a61.505406@news-east.usenetserver.com> <39878515.176CB23@home.com> <3988431d.1476923@news> <8mac6a$cte$1@news.igs.net> <3989989c.2760399@news>`

```

Kevin J. Hansen wrote in message <3989989c.2760399@news>...

>Well, it depends...  I agree completely with those of you who are
>developing applications for specific requirements; high-volume data
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>That makes all the sense in the world.

I do not think it a matter of sophistocation, but of speed vs training.
There is
a world of difference between an app that is used for an hour or two a day
in conjunction with other windows applications, and an app that is used
all the time, like a cash register.  I want a cash register to act like a
cash
register, and an adding machine to work like an adding machine.  I do not
give a rats ass if they are emulated on a computer or not, having to use
two hands for five of a possible dozen keys is silly, standardization or
not.

>
>BUT...  If you're developing an application that'll be used by people
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree with you on this, but those are not common keys in bussiness
data entry.  If I have a screen that accepts numeric data only, I should be
able to run the entire thing on the numeric pad.  If I cannot, then it is
a design flaw.  There is absolutely nothing to stop you from using all
the weird keys in addition to that, but the basics remain.  95% of data
entry to many Cobol system is numeric only.  Speed on entry is more
important than adhering to an interface designed for a different purpose.

>I like the fact that clicking on that "X" in the upper right corner of
>the window will close any Windows application.  In the bad old days of
>DOS, I remember running popular applications that used all kiinds of
>different key combinations to close them down or open files.  Every
>new package required learning a new UI.  It was a PAIN!

As do I.  However, I have also standardized on the fact that the Escape
key performs the same function, rather than Alt F4.  Two hands for an exit
key is poor design.

>One of the things that drives me nuts is apps that grossly deviate
>from the standard when it's not necessary.  I suppose this could
>relate to the developers ego....  You need to stop and figure out
>which cutesy button (in an unexpected location) does what you want.
>If the app used the standard design, it would be a breeze.

Yes and no.  In this case, you are the unsophistocated user, and you
are saying that you like a system that you do not have to learn.  I am
saying
that I do not mind learning a system if it will save me two hours per day
for ten years.  GUI designs often address the learning curve at the expense
of efficiency ... probably a good thing for the unsophistocated market, but
poor for the sophistocated market.

>If you're targeting a specialized market, go ahead and do your own
>thing - but if you're after the mass market you'll probably be more
>successful if your product looks like a "true" Windows application.

My markets are *very* specialized, and so my comments are addressed
to that type of programming.  However, I would also note that Cobol tends
to be used specifically for those types of markets.  There are very few
word processors or spreadsheets written in Cobol.

>The point of my original comments was that, if you're trying to
>develop an application that nominally conforms to the standard, you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I totally agree.  The same is true for developing your own "look and feel".
It is a painstaking process, and needs to be uniform throughout the system.

Having said that, however, I would note a further point.  There are few
successful
companies that concentrate on making their products identical to that of
their
competitors.  The idea is to improve them.
```

I checked out that article, and while the most seemed sound, I was somewhat
surprised to see that they claimed to have developed realtime multiuser
applications using Microsoft Cobol 2.1. I happen to have been using this
compiler for several years, and one of the biggest caveats with it, was that
it did not have record locking, but file locking. I for one, would not call
that real multiuser app.

Cheesle

<a id="t-f616821ecb"></a>
## Thread: Deadlocks — 15 msgs

### Deadlocks

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m92sa$ki6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Environment: Merant COBOL V4.1 revision 6 build 10/10/2 G; 32797. Run
Time System OXENI/AA0/00000P on Siemens RM400 runnig on Unix.

In an very old application which I have to maintain (:-{) there are
sometimes deadlock situations.

first question block:
How long keeps a record locked when a file is opened with lock mode
automatic?

1. I'm sure until the next write (without keep record locked...) or the
next close statement on this file.

2. Until the next read on this file regardless if the read fails or is
successful?

3. Until the next start statement on this file regardless if the read
fails or is successful?

Or should I always use unlock before any read/start statements to be
sure the last locked record is unlocked?

Second question block:

In a deadlock situation
- we kill one program expecting the locked record will be unlocked.
- More than once the record still keeps locked and we have to kill the
second program as well.

Which is the correct way to kill (or close down) a COBOL program on
Unix so that the files are being closed by the runtime system so that
locked records will be unlocked? (Short time solution, I know...Code
review would be the best but...)

Any help would be gratefully appreciated.

TIA - Stefan Meyer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Deadlocks

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39886830.108398656@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<8m92sa$ki6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Note First - I'm not a Unix expert.

On Wed, 02 Aug 2000 12:06:37 GMT, Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
wrote:

>Environment: Merant COBOL V4.1 revision 6 build 10/10/2 G; 32797. Run
>Time System OXENI/AA0/00000P on Siemens RM400 runnig on Unix.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>successful?

It should be unlocked on the next read -- but I don't know if success
or failure matters.  I *suspect* that the record is released even if
the read fails.

>
>3. Until the next start statement on this file regardless if the read
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>sure the last locked record is unlocked?


That depends.  It depends on if you have "Lock mode is automatic with
lock on multiple records" or not.  With the lock on multiple records,
only a single record is locked at any one time.

>
>Second question block:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>review would be the best but...)

I don't know about this one.  I suspect that your lock check loop may
have an error (such as it doesn't re-attempt the read?).  You can
detect that a record is locked and still retrieve the record under
certain circumstances.  Are you detecting the locked condition?

Is it possible that one of the programs gets a different type of lock
mode such as Maunal or Exclusive?

>
>Any help would be gratefully appreciated.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> That depends.  It depends on if you have "Lock mode is automatic with
> lock on multiple records" or not.  With the lock on multiple records,
> only a single record is locked at any one time.

I'm just starting to deal with databases, but could you expand on this
please? Your statement that "With the lock on multiple records, only a
single record is locked at any one time." doesn't seem to make sense. (Or
could this be a typo-maybe you mean without?)

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Deadlocks

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mb7ir$9an$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8m92sa$ki6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39886830.108398656@207.126.101.100>`

```
Hi Thane,

> Note First - I'm not a Unix expert.

Me too, normaly I don't care about the used OS...but if a program has
to be killed you have to use the OS commands.

> >How long keeps a record locked when a file is opened with lock mode
> >automatic?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the read fails.

But that is the question. I also suspect that a read attempt will
unlock a previous locked record. But I'm not sure.

> >3. Until the next start statement on this file regardless if the read
> >fails or is successful?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> only a single record is locked at any one time.

Oops. Isn't it exactly the different way?.

> >Which is the correct way to kill (or close down) a COBOL program on
> >Unix so that the files are being closed by the runtime system so that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> certain circumstances.  Are you detecting the locked condition?

I found one piece of code which goes into an infinite loop if a record
is already locked and I don't know why.
Here's the code:
       VOA-130A.
      **GROESSEN FUER RECHTS VERARBEITEN
           READ ARSTAMM NEXT RECORD
           AT END
                GO TO VOA-99.
           IF STAT-2 = LOCKED
                MOVE 31 TO FEHLERNUMMER
                MOVE 24 TO FELDNUMMER
                PERFORM FEHLERMELDUNG
                MOVE ZAR-KEY TO ARSTAMM-KEY
                GO TO VOA-130A.

As you may see, if a record is already locked, the program goes back
and tries to read again.

The only thing I can imaging is that the READ NEXT makes trouble. But
when I read the docs everything should be ok.
Do I have to read the previous record without a lock and later perform
the read next command? I don't know.

>
> Is it possible that one of the programs gets a different type of lock
> mode such as Maunal or Exclusive?

We always use lock mode automatic or in some special cases exclusive
file locking.


TIA - Stefan Meyer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
On Thu, 03 Aug 2000 07:39:06 GMT, Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
wrote:
>We always use lock mode automatic or in some special cases exclusive
>file locking.
>

I corrected the typo on "multiple records".  In your read/next I don't
know why the key gets moved back in - it does nothing.

I would check to see if the file causing the loop is one of the ones
that other programs get exclusive lock on.

Thane,

> I corrected the typo on "multiple records".  In your read/next I don't

Oops. Didn't see that move. You're right. It should do nothing. I'll

No, not at any time. If a file will be opened exclusive and another

Stefan Meyer

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deadlocks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <meyerst@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00

```


> I would check to see if the file causing the loop is one of the ones
> that other programs get exclusive lock on.

I just made an interesting test:
1. Executed the first prog on a pc with Unix terminal emulation and
locked a record in file arstamm

2. Executed the trouble-making programm on a terminal.

3. Tried to read from arstamm the already locked record

4. Get message: 'Record locked'

5. Unlocked the record in the first program

6. Trouble-making program continued as expected

Result: There seems to be a difference when executing a program on a
Unix terminal or on a Unix terminal emulation.

But we're using both terminals and terminal emulations.

So what can I do to fix the problem?

Any more hints would be gratefully appreciated.

Stefan Meyer

Thane,

> >Result: There seems to be a difference when executing a program on a
> >Unix terminal or on a Unix terminal emulation.
> >
> Coincidence?  I'd just have a hard time buying this.  Try a mixed back
> of Terminal and Terminal emulations and see what happens.

The program hangs if it is executed within an emulation regardless the
second program which locks the record.

Good News:
Everytime the FehlerMeldung is performed a single beep is triggered
using call X'E5'. When I comment out the call X'E5' the program is
working as expected and continues reading as soon as the record is
unlocked.

Strange?!

BTW: In the code there're hundreds of beeps in. My predecessor seems to
like beeping on locks/errors/...

What kind of troubles did you have? And how did you solve them?

Bye - Stefan Meyer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

is not reliable in the DOS/Win environment.  It seems to be
dependant on CPU speed.   If using an old runtime on a new pc,
the beep turns into a click, then it turns into silence (unless
you are
a bat I suppose).

#### ↳ Re: Deadlocks

- **From:** "Anton" <a.rusbach@coss.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mefve$eag$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`
- **References:** `<8m92sa$ki6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Dear Stefan,

> 2. Until the next read on this file regardless if the read fails or is
> successful?

Yes.

> 3. Until the next start statement on this file regardless if the read
> fails or is successful?

Yes.

> Or should I always use unlock before any read/start statements to be
> sure the last locked record is unlocked?

No, one runtime can't unlock any record being used by another runtime.
If no update is required, just open the file for INPUT.

> Which is the correct way to kill (or close down) a COBOL program on
> Unix so that the files are being closed by the runtime system so that

ctrl-C should do the job. Can be that your cobol version is maintaining a
lockbit in every record, therfor it ain't unlocked using the unix 'kill'
command.

grtx,
Anton
```

Sorry, missunderstanding. My guess was: unlocking a record which was
locked by myself i.e. the running program and not to unlock ALL records
locked by 'who knows....'

>
> > Which is the correct way to kill (or close down) a COBOL program on

that
>
> ctrl-C should do the job. Can be that your cobol version is
maintaining a
> lockbit in every record, therfor it ain't unlocked using the
unix 'kill'
> command.

Ctrl-C is disabled in all progs.
But the runtime normally closes all files and therefor unlocks all
records when a program ends.
My questions should be: When I kill rts32 is there a chance that all
files will closed correctly and will they remain corrupt in some
circumstances?

Bye - Stefan Meyer

### help with writing to a sequential file without suppress all my spaces

- **From:** trinh <trinhNOtrSPAM@aol.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1ef5ecca.ca3212fb@usw-ex0104-025.remarq.com>`

```
hi, i have net express cobol and having problem with writing to
a file without all my spaces at end of my record suppressing.
my input and output  file is open as line sequential.  both of
my input and output is 90 bytes long.  all my inpute record is
90 bytes long .. some record is 80.. etc bytes with spaces file
in to make it 90.  my problem is when i write to my output
file .. i want the record to be exactly  90 bytes long like my
input file without suppress all my trailing spaces... any
sugestion.. please help.
thanks
trinh@aol.com

#### ↳ Re: help with writing to a sequential file without suppress all my spaces

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<015a8666.2f5e73cb@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<1ef5ecca.ca3212fb@usw-ex0104-025.remarq.com>`

```
Using Micro Focus (oops Merant) NetExpress, if the record has
trailing spaces, the spaces WILL be dropped with a carriage
return and line feed at the end of the data.

To avoid having the record truncated, you must have a non space
character at the end of the record.

When I ran into this problem, I opted to use the 'bit stream'
library routines that NetExpress offers. With the CBL_OPEN_FILE,
CBL_WRITE_FILE, etc. you can accomplish what you want.

To make sure I understand, using line sequential, having a 10
character record with x'30303020202020202020', the record being
written is x'3030300d0a' (e.g. 000CRLF).

Regards,
Rich


-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help with writing to a sequential file without suppress all my spaces

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ma5mg$riq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1ef5ecca.ca3212fb@usw-ex0104-025.remarq.com> <015a8666.2f5e73cb@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`

```
Rich,
  What you are describing is ONLY true for "line sequential" files.  RECORD
SEQUENTIAL files keep trailing spaces, trailing hex-zeroes, and even "CRLF"
if they occur in the middle of a record.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with writing to a sequential file without suppress all my spaces

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<23c017b6.c8351e7b@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<1ef5ecca.ca3212fb@usw-ex0104-025.remarq.com> <015a8666.2f5e73cb@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com> <8ma5mg$riq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

Duh... It's never to late to learn. Just spent a few minutes
testing and you are absolutely correct. I must have been too
narrow minded (or had a "brain fart") when I was writing the
routine to do what I needed and neglected to use record
sequential.

Was there ever a time when writing record sequential files
trailing spaces were dropped?

Regards,
Rich

#### ↳ Re: help with writing to a sequential file without suppress all my spaces

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-YmddqDzwJupG@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<1ef5ecca.ca3212fb@usw-ex0104-025.remarq.com>`

```
On Wed, 2 Aug 2000 16:51:12, trinh <trinhNOtrSPAM@aol.com.invalid> 
wrote:

> hi, i have net express cobol and having problem with writing to
> a file without all my spaces at end of my record suppressing.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

LINE SEQUENTIAL files always strip trailing spaces from
the record and insert a "line end" (which is a CR/LF on
OS/2, Windows and DOS, on UNIX systems it is just a LF)

If you want and need 90 byte records use RECORD SEQUENTIAL
not LINE SEQUENTIAL. Note that RECORD SEQUENTIAL will
not insert a "line end". To get a "line End" define the record length
as 92 and place 0x13 in 91 and 0x14 in 92 (I think those are the
correct hex codes for CR and LF - but you had best look them up
to be sure)
```

```

|If you want and need 90 byte records use RECORD SEQUENTIAL
|not LINE SEQUENTIAL. Note that RECORD SEQUENTIAL will
|not insert a "line end". To get a "line End" define the record length
|as 92 and place 0x13 in 91 and 0x14 in 92 

Make that 0x10 in 92

Phil

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help with writing to a sequential file without suppress all my spaces

- **From:** pgraham@dynasty.net
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PcAi5.4751$D11.5638@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<1ef5ecca.ca3212fb@usw-ex0104-025.remarq.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-YmddqDzwJupG@tcpserver> <hzii5.1419$D11.1382@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
pgraham@dynasty.net wrote:

|lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca wrote:


||If you want and need 90 byte records use RECORD SEQUENTIAL
||not LINE SEQUENTIAL. Note that RECORD SEQUENTIAL will
||not insert a "line end". To get a "line End" define the record length
||as 92 and place 0x13 in 91 and 0x14 in 92 

|Make that 0x10 in 92

Apologies!  I don't use the C-style hex notation very often.  Using it
must have caused a brain-fart.

For ASCII:  CRLF is hex 0D 0A,  decimal 13 10 or CTRL ^M^J

Therefore it should be 0x0A in 92

Phil
```

version of Microfocus.

## Thread: Left Justify Numeric Field- HOW? — 10 msgs

### Left Justify Numeric Field- HOW?

- **From:** Larry Venick <lvenick@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m9rt2$91c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
This is the layout for my output record which occurs 25 times:
          05 DREY-P-REC.
            10  DREYP-SEQ                PIC 99.
            10  DREYP-DE-1               PIC X(5).
            10  DREYP-DE-1-VAL           PIC X(30).
            10  DREYP-DE-2               PIC X(5).
            10  DREYP-DE-2-VAL           PIC X(30).
            10  F                        PIC X(8)     VALUE SPACES.

The VAL fields are used (on some records) to hold Prices/Amounts.
In Working Storage I have the following defined:

       01 AMT-HOLD                                  PIC 9(9)V99.
       01 DISP-FIELD                                PIC ZZ,ZZZ,Z99.99.
       01 DISP-FIELD-R      REDEFINES  DISP-FIELD   PIC X(13).


My routine is :

       PROCEDURE DIVISION:

           MOVE 20                         TO DREYP-SEQ
           MOVE 'DS228'                    TO DREYP-DE-1
           COMPUTE AMT-HOLD        = (AHDB-ORD-INC-AMT-FED +
                                         AHDB-CAP-GAINS-AMT-FED)
           MOVE AMT-HOLD                   TO DISP-FIELD
           MOVE DISP-FIELD-R               TO DREYP-DE-1-VAL.
           MOVE 'DS229'                    TO DREYP-DE-2
           COMPUTE AMT-HOLD        = (AHDB-ORD-INC-AMT-STATE +
                                         AHDB-CAP-GAINS-AMT-STATE)
           MOVE AMT-HOLD                   TO DISP-FIELD
           MOVE DISP-FIELD-R               TO DREYP-DE-2-VAL.
           WRITE DIST-REC                  FROM DREY-P-REC
           INITIALIZE                      DREY-P-REC.


What I'm getting is:
20DS228            425.33DS229             00.00



What I want to get is :
20DS228425.33            DS22900.00


Nothing seems to work.....Any help would be appreciated.
Thanks

Larry






Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

  http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

and look for

NOTE TO OTHER CLC readers:
