```
In article <8m9rt2$91c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  Larry Venick <lvenick@my-deja.com> wrote:

it is questionable what the results would be if you redefine an edited
field.  since an edited field is no longer numeric or alphabetic.

however one could redefine a strictly numeric field to an alpha numeric
field and inspect for leading spaces using tallying and use the counter
to determine the location of the first non-space letter.  i.e. ctr + 1
= first digit you want. Then use move field (ctr + 1:size - ctr) and
move it to a desired field.

Using referrential addressing is a nice thing if you can use it.

"Move field (7:2)" moves 2 digits starting from position 7 of a given
field.  All you have to do is determine what part of the field to move.


> This is the layout for my output record which occurs 25 times:
>           05 DREY-P-REC.
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Left Justify Numeric Field- HOW?

- **From:** jack <jacksleightNOjaSPAM@hotmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<150d730f.cda94602@usw-ex0102-016.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8m9rt2$91c$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8meou0$tgf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi Larry,

You might want to give this a try:

In W/S add ctr pic s9(2) comp.

In P/D, before the start of rtn, add move zeros to ctr.

Change compute amt-fld-hold etc. to compute disp-fld etc.
Replace move amt-fld-hold etc. and move disp-fld-r etc. with:

inspect disp-fld-r tallying ctr for leading spaces
move disp-fld-r(ctr + 1:) to dreyp-de-1

Do the same for fld-2.

Hope this helps, Jack.

P.S.

I was under the impression that JUSTIFY only works when the
sending field is shorter than the receiving field. Am I wrong?
If anybody knows, I'd appreciate a definitive answer.

Tahnx.



-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

## Thread: Sorting a file w/o SORT verb — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Sorting a file w/o SORT verb

- **From:** Eclipse <eclipse@advalvas.be>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39884021.D1F51B5F@advalvas.be>`

```
Hi,

i would like to sort by name a sequential file without the SORT verb.

sequ file entries :

01 ident pic 9 (4)
01 Name pic a (20)




My idea was to open that file, get every entry one by one and placing
them in an indexed file.

My problem is to place them in the right order. I wanted to START the
indexed file with the key = name got from the seq file . If it doesn't
exist, write the entry. If it already exists, do nothing

Where will he write the entry after done the START ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a file w/o SORT verb

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ok6i5.128$l36.79859@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<39884021.D1F51B5F@advalvas.be>`

```
Forget START. Neither you nor the ISAM routines care whether the
record already exists.

OPEN OUTPUT ISAM-FILE. *>Create empty ISAM file
CLOSE ISAM-FILE.
OPEN I-O ISAM-FILE.      *> Open for random writing
PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
   READ INPUT-FILE AT END EXIT PERFORM END-READ
   WRITE ISAM-REC FROM INPUT-REC      *> If the record already exists,
no foul.
   END-PERFORM.
CLOSE INPUT-FILE.
CLOSE ISAM-FILE.
OPEN INPUT-FILE.
PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
   READ ISAM-FILE NEXT AT END EXIT PERFORM END-READ    *> read ISAM
file sequentially
   WRITE OUTPUT-FILE FROM ISAM-REC
   END-PERFORM.
CLOSE ISAM-FILE.
CLOSE OUTPUT-FILE.


"Eclipse" <eclipse@advalvas.be> wrote in message
news:39884021.D1F51B5F@advalvas.be...
> Hi,
>
> i would like to sort by name a sequential file without the SORT
verb.
>
> sequ file entries :
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> My idea was to open that file, get every entry one by one and
placing
> them in an indexed file.
>
> My problem is to place them in the right order. I wanted to START
the
> indexed file with the key = name got from the seq file . If it
doesn't
> exist, write the entry. If it already exists, do nothing
>
> Where will he write the entry after done the START ?
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a file w/o SORT verb

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39888693.6592192@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<39884021.D1F51B5F@advalvas.be>`

```
On Wed, 02 Aug 2000 15:35:57 GMT, Eclipse wrote:

>i would like to sort by name a sequential file without the SORT verb.
[various snippages]

>My idea was to open that file, get every entry one by one and placing
>them in an indexed file.

>My problem is to place them in the right order. I wanted to START the
>indexed file with the key = name got from the seq file [...]

You can sort a file by writing its records to an indexed file, and
then re-reading the indexed file into wherever you want the records.
Define the indexed file's record key to be your desired sort key.

You don't need to worry about placing the records in order into the
indexed file.  The concept of how records are later retrieved from the
indexed file will take care of this for you.

You also don't need to worry about STARTing the file, where it is
after you finish, or any of that.  First, create a new indexed file,
write all of your records into the indexed file, and close the indexed
file.  Then, open the indexed file input in the sequential access
mode, and read the records in sequential order.

That said, there are some things to consider.

  �  On most systems, this is a *very* slow, inefficient method
     of sorting a file.  It probably will also impose a large
     burden on the system with respect to the performance of any
     other programs that are running.

  �  This method assumes that all of your records contain unique
     sort keys.  If not, then you will need to add an increasing
     sequential number to your record key, so that each record will
     have a unique key.

  �  In most implementations, you will be limited to "sorting" the
     file in increasing sequence only.
```

<a id="t-cff533909f"></a>
## Thread: COBOL3 question — 2 msgs

### Re: COBOL3 question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mab95$f74$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<881493183D27D311ADE0006008A0BD8C0299A44B@esd-exchange1.esd.wa.gov>`

```
1) COBOL questions are often answered more quickly and accurately in the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup - which I am adding to this list.  (And then again,
CICS question may need to go to the CICS list-server).

2) "COBOL3" is a CICS translator option - it has MINIMAL relationship to any
IBM product called (correctly or incorrectly "COBOL3"). It is ABSOLUTELY the
correct term to use - when talking about a translator option.

3) The response that pointed to APAR PN70904 *does* explain some of the
enhanced features (from the newer IBM COBOL compilers) that may be used in
CICS programs - when this translator option is in effect.

4) I suggest that you look at your CICS Programming Guide and/or look at

 http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DFHJAP33/1%2e2%2e4

This talks (briefly) about the COBOL2, COBOL3, ANSI85, and OOCOBOL translator
options - and points to other references - if you need them.

5) As far as the compilers themselves go:

  IBM SAA AD/Cycle COBOL/370 - is already out of service
  VS COBOL II - before release 4.0 - is already out of service
  VS COBOL II, R4 - will go out of service at the end of March 2001
  IBM COBOL for MVS & VM
     and
  IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
   (either with MLE extensions)
      are both supported and have no announced drop dates.
  This support (or non-support) for each of these compilers apply to both
BATCH and CICS (and IMS/DC and DB2 and whatever).

"OBJECT CODE" compiled with earlier compilers will continue to be supported
after March 2001 - but only if linked and run with the LE run-time/link-edit
libraries.

NOTE: Some differences exist if you are talking about COBOL and CICS under
VSE - but I don't think that is your specific concern.

6) Co-existence of various "flavors" of COBOL compiled programs in the same
CICS "unit of work" is a non-trivial topic.  It is discussed in the current
COBOL migration guides. The easy answer (from your original question) is that
VS COBOL II and "COBOL for this-and-that" programs MAY <normally> co-exist
within the same CICS unit-of-work.

  ***
I hope this helps
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL3 question

- **From:** "Roland Schiradin" <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<881493183D27D311ADE0006008A0BD8C0299A44B@esd-exchange1.esd.wa.gov> <8mab95$f74$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Roland

#### ↳ Re: ERROR Message

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ma6h2$i7f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3988981C.82DBEA8D@seax.demon.co.uk>`

```
You will need to give us a CLUE as to what COBOL product you are using (and
preferably on what operating system).

There are no "generic" error messages.  (Even file-status codes - which are
generic - may show up in different ways depending upon the COBOL system in
use)

John,
  If you aren't actually a COBOL programmer, if you post the exact and
COMPLETE message that you get, we may be able to figure this out for you.
```

<a id="t-7126ac4206"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 — 3 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2000-07](2000-07.md#t-7126ac4206)._

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL 4.2

- **From:** "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9RLh5.12629$Mt.186314@nnrp1.ptd.net>`
- **References:** `<uVwqZIk#$GA.401@cpmsnbbsa08>`

```
Fujitsu 4.2 (and ealier versions) had problems with
un-installs.  Even if you properly run the un-install
(through the control panel) you'll still have some things
left in the registry.

Fujitsu has a "filefinder" program they can email you
(if you are on a support contract), but it really won't help,
as the problem is not files, it is the registry.

I have been there myself, and the only way to fix this
problem is to re-install Windows.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL 4.2

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7%Uh5.87$NI2.57472@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<uVwqZIk#$GA.401@cpmsnbbsa08> <9RLh5.12629$Mt.186314@nnrp1.ptd.net>`

```
don ferrario <don@ferrario.com> wrote in message
news:9RLh5.12629$Mt.186314@nnrp1.ptd.net...
> Fujitsu 4.2 (and ealier versions) had problems with
> un-installs.  Even if you properly run the un-install
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If the registry entries are where they belong
(HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Fujitsu\yada\yada) you can always remove those
keys using regedit instead of re-installing Windows.

Heck, that worked good for me removing leftover registry keys from 'demo'
versions of both [ProductA] and [ProductB]  whose un-installs didn't.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL 4.2

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39885626.103779336@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<uVwqZIk#$GA.401@cpmsnbbsa08> <9RLh5.12629$Mt.186314@nnrp1.ptd.net>`

```
On Wed, 02 Aug 2000 02:39:01 GMT, "don ferrario" <don@ferrario.com>
wrote:

>I have been there myself, and the only way to fix this
>problem is to re-install Windows.
>

No.  Version 5 as it installs will "Clean up" the registry from the
version 4.x problems.

Also, I have manually cleaned my machine many times (going between 4,
3, 4.2 and 5 when in beta).  Just run REGEDIT and search for FSC and
Fujitsu and delete all the keys you find (being careful).  Not for the
timid, but it does save an Windows Re-install.

Also, reinstalling without reformatting will duplicate any registry
problems you have.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

<a id="t-8d23ac77a5"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL for MVS & VM - dropped from marketing — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM COBOL for MVS & VM - dropped from marketing

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mam47$14h$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just in case anyone interested in such has missed it (as I did - when I made
a mistake in a post to IBM-MAIN),

On June 6, 2000, IBM announced the removal from marketing - effective Sept 6,
2000 - of:

  IBM COBOL for MVS & VM

See the announcement at:
     http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_900-124

For those who don't speak "IBM-ese" as a native language <G>, removal from
marketing is NOT the same thing as dropping from support.  However, it is
usually the first step on the path to such a statement.

For those who can't keep track of the "real" names of IBM's mainframe COBOLs
the product that will "remain" after this product is dropped is

 "IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM" (product number 5648-A25)
                                                  or   (product number
5648-MLE with MLE)
```

---

## Thread: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor — 45 msgs

### "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

- **From:** slipstr201@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.java,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
==============================
 Open Letter to Dept of Labor to Protect workers rights
 ======================================
 Alexis Herman
 Secretary of Labor
 U.S DOL
 200 constitution Ave, NW,  Room S-1032
 Washington, DC, 20210

 Sub: Loss of US jobs to fraudulent Immigrant workers, Immediate action
requested by the Department of Labor to comprehensively investigate and
prosecute companies engaged in illegal H1-B trade.

 In a congressional testimony to judiciary subcommittee on Immigration
chaired by Congressmen Lamar  Smith, INS Deputy executive commissioner
Bill Yates testified on the H1-B fraud schemes prevalent in the
industry.

 Bill Yates testified that INS service centers couldn�t authenticate the
claims made by H1-B applicants regarding their education or experience.
He also testified that over 45 % of all the credentials were outright
fraudulent.

 Many US companies are engaged in the illegal trade of bringing in
unqualified H1-B immigrants and training  them in marketable job skills.
H1-B Immigrants are then placed with US companies as contract workers.
Many of the unqualified H1-B Immigrants are underpaid and abused. Many
major US daily�s newspapers ran several articles on this issue.

 Some H1-B dependent companies are also outsourcing H1-B immigrants to
non-technical jobs as contract  workers. No Immigrant will ever complain
to authorities over the abuse on the fear of being fired from the job.
 H-1B dependent companies are also Illegally engaged in obtaining fixed
priced, time and material contract at a  lower cost using underpaid
Immigrants. This hurts competition and bottom line of companies hiring
American
 workers.

 Congressmen Lamar Smith himself wrote a letter to the acting INS
director to address the issue, INS is  struggling with the law which
does got provide a clear direction on what should be the qualifications
of an H1-B  applicant.
 Bill Yates in this testimony conceded that its difficult for INS to
verity the credentials as they originate from foreign sources and that
the format or form of submission by foreign business and schools is not
 standardized.  INS relies on American consulate personnel as the means
of verification.

 Even American consulates are handicapped due to the prevalence of
corruption and lack of standardized information in overseas countries.
American Consulate in Chennai -India relies on a computerized database
 of employees of major software and technology firms in India to verify
and authenticate experience.

 Fraudulent companies are getting around these using payoffs to HR
officials at major overseas companies to  enter the names of H1-B
applicants into the database.

 H1-B fraud hurts American workers, especially African Americans, other
minorities and new college graduates.

 We request that Department of Labor launch a comprehensive
Investigation of companies engaged in  defrauding American workers using
the H1-B program. Lack of any administrative action and monitoring is
 also encouraging companies to illegally profit from the program.

 We also request Department of Labor to set guidelines and qualification
standards for Immigrant workers and
 raise the issue with INS.


 Sincerely,



 An American Worker


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.java,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kByi5.678$_%2.138548@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
About thirty responses came to mind for this rant, ranging from
'Nitwit,' to 'Idiot.'

The US could use 1.8 million new technical workers per year for the
foreseeable future.

US colleges are graduating about 400,000 per year.

Where do the balance come from?

If you think we only need to train a hamburger-flippers to be
electrical engineers, or take inner-city youth, give them a hard-hat,
and tell them to build a bridge, you have about as much grip on
reality as you do of constructing a cogent argument.

If your complaint is with the "fraud" inherent in the H1-B program,
how about eliminating all government oversight and permissions for
immigration of technical workers?
```

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Easy.  The companies which need that balance, has three choices:

1.  Hire foreigners to do the balance, in this country.
2.  Move overseas.
3.  Stop competing.

Case #1 keeps a lot of Americans working.  This is the case which he
wants to make illegal (for the sake of American workers).

```

"Isofarro" <micd@isofarro.freeservewithnochips.co.uk
>
> There has to be some reason why US colleges can only graduate
400,000
> people a year - be it by student choice or ability. Surely focusing
on
> the root problem instead of the symptoms would be a better tack?

Any attempt to focus on the 'root' problem of any social ill is a
criminal waste of resources and always fails.

We don't have enough engineers to meet demand because not enough
capable people WANT to be engineers. There are three solutions to the
shortfall:

1. Give babies chocolate-covered, calculator-shaped, pacifiers so they
grow up with a good feeling toward things technical (starting now, we
will see the results in a quarter century);
2. Require a certain percentage of college students to study
engineering/science (starting now, we'll see results in four to six
years);
3. Import the requisite skills (results by next week).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398D574A.26C6EB4B@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <kByi5.678$_%2.138548@nnrp3.sbc.net> <398AC5A9.5EE70FA3@brazee.net> <fq4ooskls55p6nuuo2bthmnr81f615f64g@4ax.com> <X71j5.46$f_.60519@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
2000-08-06 14:16:18 MEST

Jerry P wrote:

> ...

> There are three solutions to the
> shortfall:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> will see the results in a quarter century);

Maybe you capture a few talents that way which would go into
other professions otherwise.

> 2. Require a certain percentage of college students to study
> engineering/science (starting now, we'll see results in four to six
> years);

I have teached students which were actually not fit
for the subject (physics, in this case). Forcing them
into a subject you do not archive anything. You do not
do a favour to them or to society.

> 3. Import the requisite skills (results by next week).

Not so here in Germany. Here it is "results maybe within a year".
Just follow the news about the "Green Card" discussion in
Germany (which ist not what Americans call a "Green Card",
I do not know who coined this word here.)

I would add a

4. Do not stop engineers from working efficiently and
   creatively.

Engineer employed by companies who are ever expanding
bureaucracys do not contribute much. Engineers who are
not given the right tools do not contribute much. Engineers
who may not even buy a desktop-lamp on behalf of their
employer do not contribute much. Engineers who need a lot
of their time for keeping up-to-date with the organization
scheme of their company rather than aquiring new skills
do not contribute much.

Small companies who treat engineers like this go bankrupt
and thus return the engineers as valuable workforke back
to the job market. Large companies like this can persist
for a very long time.

Unfortunately, there can be no law against this misemployment.
As long as such companies exist, they offer quite safe
employment, and many people want that. You can get these
engineers out of their present employment by offering them
quite competitive salaries. Maybe. Some of them.

But some of them have also learned the art of bureaucracy
thoroughly. I would advise against employment in such cases
except you know what you are doing.

Excuse where my English obscures the meaning. I restrict
this posting to news:comp.lang.cobol only.

Herwig
```

#### ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.javascript,comp.lang.java,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


slipstr201@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> ==============================
>  Open Letter to Dept of Labor to Protect workers rights
>  ======================================

This letter is designed to protect the jobs of us American workers at
the expense of people who need jobs worse than we do.  The stated
concerns about whether or not those other workers are qualified or not
applies quite nicely to us as well.  The only question is whether we
should fight to keep our advantages by having our government eliminating
competition, or whether we should compete.

In the short term, barriers will make us richer, and maybe we will
retire before the jobs move elsewhere.  (I am a fogey and should be able
to retire with my skills in demand).  But we won't be doing any favors
to the next generation by non competition.

A world company which is not allowed to hire workers here, will hire
workers elsewhere.
```

enlightened us:

>
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>workers elsewhere.

Add to that the fact that quotas were raised last year to meet the
demands of companies needing skilled workers.  I don't believe there
is rampant H1-B fraud going on.  If a worker is brought in on an H1-B
and the company finds they can't do the work, they would be treated
just like an American worker in the same situation.  Adios.  Don't let
the door hit you in the ass on your way out.

The writer of the original post is either someone who couldn't cut it
(like someone already mentioned) or is a member of some Aryan Nation,
Neo Nazi all foreigners are scum organization.  I have no use for such
a person.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jack <jacksleightNOjaSPAM@hotmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c199d6.c0d27c2b@usw-ex0105-034.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net> <17B78713257F2A5F.770D62893F11255D.C9580080C8A65466@lp.airnews.net> <9kemosk652dgkseki6q90vr0jfplvpb98t@4ax.com> <GDgj5.2302$Ib.467728@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I'm somewhat conflicted on this subject. While I agree with
the "rugged individualist", "succeed on your own merits", "com-
etition is the American way" view, I have to ask:

Is it right that an H-1B applicant lies about his/her
education/experience and gets the job because it can't be
checked? And the American applicant doesn't. Assuming their
skills are equivalent.

Take the case where an H-1B takes a job in the US with the idea
of earning enough money to return to his native land and retire
(~ 50K). Don't laugh, a NYT article profiled a public school
teacher in India living quite well on her $1200/yr salary!

The point of this story is that it's difficult to compete on
this basis. I have no problem with an H-1B comming here to stay
and competing in the job market.

Now, while admittedly there is a great difference in degree, a
US company (with some scruples) would have a tough time
competing with a company in a country that allows slave labor.
My point is, I guess, that somehow the playing field has to be
level or it will be levelled.

To reiterate my opening point, politically I'm a "laissez faire
capitalist" (don't know if I spelled that right), but I wonder
about these kinds of situations and how (or if) capitalism,
through our political system should deal with them?

Thanx for listening, Jack.


-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398EBC3B.6475FAB7@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net> <17B78713257F2A5F.770D62893F11255D.C9580080C8A65466@lp.airnews.net> <9kemosk652dgkseki6q90vr0jfplvpb98t@4ax.com> <GDgj5.2302$Ib.467728@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <01c199d6.c0d27c2b@usw-ex0105-034.remarq.com>`

```


jack wrote:

> I'm somewhat conflicted on this subject. While I agree with
> the "rugged individualist", "succeed on your own merits", "com-
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> skills are equivalent.

American applicants often lie about these.  How many employers get
transcripts from prospective employee's colleges?   It is wrong, but it
is not limited to H-1B applicants.


> Take the case where an H-1B takes a job in the US with the idea
> of earning enough money to return to his native land and retire
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and competing in the job market.

I don't see any real difference between these two.  Or are you saying
you must NEED the money to get the job.  In that case, poor countries
should get all the jobs, and you shouldn't let rich kids (or rich
countries) have jobs.


> Now, while admittedly there is a great difference in degree, a
> US company (with some scruples) would have a tough time
> competing with a company in a country that allows slave labor.
> My point is, I guess, that somehow the playing field has to be
> level or it will be levelled.

Some countries allow slave labor (or prison labor).  I suspect ALL
countries allow this to some extent, including the U.S.A.  But labor
allowed to enter the U.S.A. with H1-B visas does not qualify.

If the protesters showed that companies such as Nike used this labor to
build their shoes, I would support them.  But when they show only that
Nike built the factories where people needed the jobs badly enough to be
happy to apply for low paying jobs, I applaud Nike.  Let the poor have
jobs, get enough to eat, get educations, and become comfortable.
Looking at the world, most industries work this way (mining appears to
be an exception).  Support what evidence shows has worked - question
what you think should work, but which evidence doesn't support.


> To reiterate my opening point, politically I'm a "laissez faire
> capitalist" (don't know if I spelled that right), but I wonder
> about these kinds of situations and how (or if) capitalism,
> through our political system should deal with them?

Best thing to do is to look at what has worked, and what has failed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398D4892.41F60EF4@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net> <rjemos0ikv791g3vr839bprg4up8hv6dum@4ax.com>`

```
One of the key reasons overseas projects fail is simply what we
call here 'the tyranny of distance'. If you are a long way from
your customers you tend not to have such a good idea what they
want, you cannot solve problems that require technical and
business compromise easily. This is all much more of a problem
also if there are time zones involved. Try and organise a two
hour phone conference involving someone in Wellington NZ,
Melbourne Australia, Lansing MI and Glasgow, Scotland. Who is not
going to sleep tonight?

Then you have all the cultural issues. Different cultures, even
if they nominally speak the same language, have different ways to
communicate things. For example the word 'interesting' in the UK
means 'stupid' as in "that's an interesting idea". Americans
think if you are not full of confidence you must be no good.
Australians think if you are up front saying how good you are
then you are pretentious and probably no good (FIGJAM = xxxx I'm
Good Just Ask Me). Etc.

Employers will always want to loosen immigration rules because it
keeps salaries down. If you cut the supply of labor the price
will go up and the demand will fall. To talk about an inbalance
between supply and demand does not make sense unless you have a
particular (low) price in mind. 

>From my perspective as a non green card person and a citizen of
another country, getting an H1B visa is not very appealing
because you become the serf of the employer. If you lose your job
you are out, sonny. Therefore H1B people are more likely to
accept low wages, unpaid overtime, poor conditions etc. A number
of friends have taken H1B jobs and not had good experiences. 

I also felt from a three month secondment in the US, to ensure
that software written there would work elsewhere, that life for
Americans is tougher than for Australians and NZers. The actual
standard of living after taking into account the cost of living
and the incredible competition for everything is lower than
Australia/NZ. Not just economic cost of living but also crime,
murder rates, social tension, divorce, life expectancy, takeover
of the political system by special interests with money,
pollution, etc. In so many ways the standard of living was worse
than elsewhere, although I found Americans assumed this was not
so. In fact they could not imagine that it was worse. 

However it is an incredibly exciting place to spent a while.
Anywhere else seems very dull by comparison.

Tim Josling
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iGmj5.591$1o3.258617@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net> <rjemos0ikv791g3vr839bprg4up8hv6dum@4ax.com> <398D4892.41F60EF4@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:398D4892.41F60EF4@melbpc.org.au...
> Employers will always want to loosen immigration rules because it
> keeps salaries down. If you cut the supply of labor the price
> will go up and the demand will fall.


Um, the way your phrased that, it suggests demand falls because the supply
of labor is cut. Hopefully that's just a grammatic glitch and not what you
were taught in school.

Demand is independent of supply; however, demand is very much dependent on
price.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "H1-B" Fraud Open letter to Dept of Labor

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398F13DA.3089470E@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net> <rjemos0ikv791g3vr839bprg4up8hv6dum@4ax.com> <398D4892.41F60EF4@melbpc.org.au> <iGmj5.591$1o3.258617@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`

```
Demand and supply curves are more or less independent in
classical theory. But if supply falls, the price goes up and the
effectiuve demand (demand at the current price) will fall. So
supply affects effective demand via the price. 

If the price of gas goes up the amount sold falls - am I missing
something here?

Tim Josling

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ TIME als RANDOM

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Waterreus" <sjorssw@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<965671854.643538@news.l3.wish.net>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net> <rjemos0ikv791g3vr839bprg4up8hv6dum@4ax.com> <398EB7E5.971FDA90@brazee.net>`

```
            Hallo COBOL-jongens en meisjes, dames en heren.

Mijn compiler begrijpt me niet d.w.z., de RANDOM-functie is haar volledig
onbekend.
Zodoende heb ik getracht de voorziening TIME te gebruiken.
Dit is op jammerlijke wijze mislukt.
De vertraging was afhankelijk van de door TIME geleverde hondertsten van een
seconde.
Dat was geen fijn plan: er ontstond een soortement loop: steeds dezelfde
cyclus 'random'
getallen.
Mijn vraag is  simpel : Kan de standaard COBOL-functie TIME uberhaubt
gebruikt worden om een RANDOM-generator te simuleren ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: TIME als RANDOM

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398F3717.476431FE@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<8md026$krt$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398AC47A.6E92B044@brazee.net> <rjemos0ikv791g3vr839bprg4up8hv6dum@4ax.com> <398EB7E5.971FDA90@brazee.net> <965671854.643538@news.l3.wish.net>`

```
2000-08-07 23:59:59 MESZ

Waterreus wrote:
> 
>             Hallo COBOL-jongens en meisjes, dames en heren.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> gebruikt worden om een RANDOM-generator te simuleren ?

--------------------------------------------------------------------

This is really fun. I try to translate this language which I am
ignorant of but which sounds remotely like a German dialekt.

German first:

--------------------------------------------------------------------

              Hallo Cobol Jungen und Mï¿½dchen, Damen und Herren,

Mein Compiler versteht mich nicht, das heiï¿½t, die RANDOM-Funktion
ist vollstï¿½ndig unbekannt.

Zuerst habe ich versucht, _....._ TIME zu gebrauchen.

Dies ist auf jï¿½mmerliche Weise miï¿½glï¿½ckt.

Anfï¿½nglich habe ich von der TIME immer die hundertstel Sekunde
gebraucht.

Das war kein feiner Plan - es entstand eine SortierungsSchleife:
stets ist derselbe Zyklus 'Random' entstanden.

Meine Frage ist einfach: Kann die Cobol-Standard-Funktion
ï¿½berhaupt als ZufallsZahlenGenerator gebraucht werden?

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Now I put that to English, with some further polishing:

--------------------------------------------------------------------

            Hi Cobol boys and girls, ladies and gentlemen,

My compiler doesn't understand me, that means, the RANDOM function
is unknown.

First I tried to use TIME.

This failed miserably.

I was using the hundreth of a second.

That was not a good idea - a sorting loop came into being, with
the same cycle all the time.

My question is simple: Is ist possible to use the Cobol
standard function TIME as a randomgenerator at all?

--------------------------------------------------------------------

This makes sense. Now I can answer.

Missusing a TIME function for a random generator is possible -
in principle, in any programming language. It depends on
details of the algorithms calling this self-made random
function.

PGP, for instance, uses the times of keystrokes of the user
in order to generate a new random pair of private/public key.

But one has to know what one does. If one needs a huge amount
of random numbers fast and thusly calls TIME many times
within a short time interval, the return values of this calls
are certainly correlated - and are anything else than random.
(This again applies to any programming language) I am
not surprised that your program failed what you expected
it to do.

Look into any textbook about the subject. What you want is a
"linear congruent method"-generator. In certain cases -
cryptography for example - DO NOT rely on a random generator
which any programming language provides! Write your own
random generator.

That, again, is possible in any programming language.
```

using?  I am wondering if there is a WAY to "turn on" the RANDOM function.

with using COBOL's "built-in" facility for this.

```
FYI,
  I did receive an "official" translation from a native Dutch speaker as
follows:

"Bill,

Translation and comments between text.
Complete translation also at the end.

> > Mijn compiler begrijpt me niet d.w.z., de RANDOM-functie is
> > haar volledig onbekend.
    My compiler does not understand me, I mean the RANDOM-function
    is completely unknown to it.
> > Zodoende heb ik getracht de voorziening TIME te gebruiken.
    That's why I tried to use the option TIME.
> > Dit is op jammerlijke wijze mislukt.
    This failed disastrously.
> > De vertraging was afhankelijk van de door TIME geleverde
> > hondertsten van een seconde.
    The delay was dependent on the hundredths of seconds delivered by TIME.
> > Dat was geen fijn plan: er ontstond een soortement loop:
    This was not a nice plan: some sort of loop came out:
> > steeds dezelfde cyclus 'random' getallen.
    over and over again the same cycle of numbers.
> > Mijn vraag is  simpel : Kan de standaard COBOL-functie TIME
    My question is simply this: Can the standard COBOL-function TIME
> > uberhaupt gebruikt worden om een RANDOM-generator te simuleren ?
    �berhaupt [germanism, HK] be used to simulate a RANDOM-generator ?

<IN ONE PIECE>
Hello COBOL-boys and girls, ladies and gentlemen.

My compiler does not understand me, I mean the RANDOM-function
is completely unknown to it.
That's why I tried to use the option TIME.

This failed disastrously.
The delay was dependent on the hundredths
of seconds delivered by TIME.

This was not a nice plan: some sort of loop came out:
over and over again the same cycle of numbers.

My question is simply this:
Can the standard COBOL-function TIME
be used to simulate a RANDOM-generator?
</IN ONE PIECE>
```

#### ↳ Re: Convert COBOL to JAVA

- **From:** Bill <wfsent@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39894D9D.A91B2A87@bellatlantic.net>`
- **References:** `<8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Many clients in non ibm 'shops' prefer to buy unix c/java solutions. I know of
one company that is rewriting their entire COBOL application,  (2+ million
lines) in C++. AS a system written in C++ is 'preferred' to one written in
COBOL. Who cares about reliability/functionality etc etc. It just 'sounds
better' to say I'm investing in a JAVA solution than a COBOL solution. So a
COBOL/JAVA converter sounds attractive.

Also there appears to be more Java / C programmers than COBOL programmers.

Bill

> Jeff

#### ↳ Re: Convert COBOL to JAVA

What if a company is currently using CICS for online and wants to convert to
a web-based solution.  Many of the skills and techniques are similar if JAVA
is used to replace COBOL.  CGI programming is in a sense
pseudo-conversational.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Convert COBOL to JAVA

- **From:** deskware_mattdean@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mc31p$t6l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com> <8mbr5i$sol$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Yes, the web is in a sense pseudo-conversational.  CobolScript is a
logical extension of these skill sets for anyone wanting to do interenet
programming.  CobolScript is a server side scripting
language based on COBOL.  Here are some examples:

CGI: http://www.cobolscript.com/samples.htm
WML: http://www.cobolscript.com/wml.htm
HDML: http://www.cobolscript.com/hdml.htm

Database connectivity: http://www.cobolscript.com/profwin.htm
http://www.cobolscript.com/profunix.htm.


Matthew Dean
dean@deskware.com

#### ↳ Re: Convert COBOL to JAVA

- **From:** "Mike Sheehan" <msheehan@pks.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mek62$r8j$1@kermit.esat.net>`
- **References:** `<8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Perhaps they have a "java only here policy" - seeing COBOL as legacy code...
But as many have said there is plenty of scope for mixing java with COBOL.
Startups have successfully gone the Java route e.g. a scottish bank; but
conversions are a different kettle of fish.

Mike

news:8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Convert COBOL to JAVA

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398AEC2D.861181EE@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com> <8mek62$r8j$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
Mike Sheehan wrote:
> 
> Perhaps they have a "java only here policy" - seeing COBOL as legacy code...
> But as many have said there is plenty of scope for mixing java with COBOL.
> Startups have successfully gone the Java route e.g. a scottish bank; but
> conversions are a different kettle of fish.

My shop mixed languages.  They allow us to pick a language more suited
for particular tasks.  Theoretically, this would allow us to expand our
skill set as well.

Except what happens is that we are needed at our current tasks, so we
don't get to expand our skill sets.  Then we change jobs, leaving a task
without skilled workers to do that task.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Convert COBOL to JAVA

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398EBDED.8C308654@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com> <8mep7l$trs$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
piasa_bird@my-deja.com wrote:

> In article <8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com>,
>   "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:
>
> You ever think that maybe a JAVA programmer makes more money???

So why does a java programmer make more money?  (I don't know whether to
spell it "java" or "Java", but it should not be "JAVA", as it is not an
acronym).

Supply and demand decides how much money a skill earns.  But there are a
lot more schools creating Java programmers than Cobol programmers.  The
supply needs to keep growing to match this, or else there will soon be a
correction.
```

#### ↳ Re: Convert COBOL to JAVA

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SvLi5.78974$i5.1223339@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
"Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:8mat3101497@enews4.newsguy.com...
> Could anyone explain to me why this might need to be done.  A colleaque at
> work is leaving the company to work for another, converting COBOL
> application to JAVA.  Now, I don't know anymore specifics.  It sounds
bogus
> to me.

it's most frequently a strategic choice. particularly in the case of Java,
as it's not so much the language that is the compelling factor (though it is
a very good clean language suited for many tasks), but that you can consider
Java (with all the supporting classes, J2EE et al) to provide a machine and
operating system independent PLATFORM upon which you can host your
applications.
```

---

## Thread: Free flowchart software without limitations. — 3 msgs

### Free flowchart software without limitations.

- **From:** "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mccch$9em$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
hi
Anybody know where to get a free Flowchart making software for win 9x ?
I have actually tried unlicensed versions of Smart draw, wizflow, paraben,
Rff etc..BUT
they have all got some or other kind of 'restriction' in them which let me
down for
my exams..for e.g.. the limitations are the Printed Output  consists of
message at the
footing which I don't want...another only let you draw certain (less) number
of boxes on
one page..
please let me know if some one knows.
Thank you

Regards
M S

ps: Is there any possible way to get rid of the 'auto' footing message on
the printed page ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Free flowchart software without limitations.

- **From:** "Colonel" <psxzone@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<8mccch$9em$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
If your`e using any of MicroSoft Office products, they supply various
`AutoShapes` one of which is flowcharting symbols, in particular for
flowcharting I use Powerpoint. I use Office 2000 so I`m not sure on the
previous versions. I do all my coursework documentation with Office,
including flowcharts, run charts, JSP charts etc.

I actually find it easier to use than SmartDraw.

Colonel
psxzone@btinternet.com

<a id="t-423380a865"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol over ISDN — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol over ISDN

- **From:** "Jock Strap" <jock@strap.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mce99$hld$1@phys-ma.sol.co.uk>`

```
We have an application written in MF COBOL which works fine locally, but
which is giving us severe problems running over ISDN.

The problem occurs if data is being entered into a text field (using a
normal Accept) and the line drops - usually due to the user answering the
phone.

The line is not picked up again, and the result is system hangs, error 43's,
file corruptions and deeply dissatisfied customers.

We are using 3Com office connect 531 routers on an NT4 box.

Has anyone any ideas???

Gordon Wallace mailto:grwallace@sol.co.uk
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol over ISDN

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KUji5.253567$7o1.6762651@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<8mce99$hld$1@phys-ma.sol.co.uk>`

```
1) Change your file handling so that is has transaction control (SELECT ...
WITH ROLLBACK, COMMIT, ROLLBACK)

2 Eliminate physical file locks and implement logical file locks by adding a
timestamp to every record and testing against the timestamp when updating

3) Close files before a DISPLAY and reopen them after the ACCEPT

4) Look into the 'ON EXCEPTION' clause for ACCEPT statements. It may allow
you to catch a lost connection or timeout condition and allow you to cleanly
terminate the application.

4) Implement a client/server architecture

6) Use a different file system like BTRIEVE which may offer better recovery
options. Micro Focus provides a file handler for BTRIEVE


"Jock Strap" <jock@strap.com> wrote in message
news:8mce99$hld$1@phys-ma.sol.co.uk...
> We have an application written in MF COBOL which works fine locally, but
> which is giving us severe problems running over ISDN.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The line is not picked up again, and the result is system hangs, error
43's,
> file corruptions and deeply dissatisfied customers.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

## Thread: Success stories for Cobol — 1 this month / 77 total

_Thread started [2000-06](2000-06.md#t-96a5a3cd70)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Success stories for Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** pizzamaxsw@aol.com (Pizzamaxsw)
- **Date:** 2000-08-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000803150717.15553.00000726@ng-fz1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<hkk85.33$b31.14077@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I learn to programming in COBOL since more than 20 years ago, with the IBM/360
and then IBM/370 and may be most of the people don't now  what kind of thing is
that 360 or 370; since that I had been programming in COBOL passing by diferent
kind of computers; processor speed (Imagine working with flippy and floppy
diskettes at less than 4MHZ). So, after 20 years I had tried  RPG, Basic,
Dbase, Clipper, Foxpro, Oracle, Informix, C, CP/M, Dos, Windows, Unix (Aix,
HP-UX, DG-UX.....) and guess what????? COBOL is always present as a programming
language for new development, upgrades, migrations.
After 20 years I still programming in COBOL and I discover new Tools for 
programming.
And after the Y2K nothing happens and so with COBOL. Think about it the only
basic thing you have to now is SPEAK ENGLISH, little of logic and then
enjoy.....
Vinicio Aizpurua
Independent Analist/Programmer
Miami, FL, USA
aizpuruav@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems with Cobol + C

Hello Marco, this is going back a long time (you've got some pretty old
product there), and unfortunately I can't try this, but I think the error
may be caused by picking up the wrong linker. There's a LINK.EXE in the
COBOL EXEDLL directory and the MSVC BIN directory. Check your PATH and
whichever of these is being picked up (i.e. is first in the PATH), re-jig
things to pick up the other one instead. If you're not sure which one it's
finding, copy the LINK.EXE to the current directory.

Let me know if this works.

Regards,


Dan Clarke.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: calling c-function or c-program from netexpress-cobol

- **From:** Danial Clarke <danial.clarke@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398ECC29.3982F098@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<8mernr$vre$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398B1ECA.FEEC8B40@home.com>`

```
James is right that an example would be helpful, but the short answer is
there are several ways to do this.

The most likely reason you're getting an RT-173 is that you're calling a C
function within a DLL but you haven't told the COBOL program which DLL
they're in.

For example, say we have a COBOL main program 'cbl1.cbl' like this:

     01 a pic x(10).
     procedure division.
     move "1234567890" to a
     display "a = " a
     call "csub1" using a
     display "a = " a
     stop run.

and this is calling a C function 'csub1', defined in the file 'csub.c':

#include <stdio.h>

void
csub1(char *a)
{
    strcpy(a, "ABCDEFGHIJ");
}

1. The simplest thing would be to link them into one exe, e.g:

    cobol cbl1.cbl obj;
    cl /c csub.c
    cbllink cbl1.obj csub.obj

- this will give us 'cbl1.exe' which will contain the COBOL and C routines.

2. If you want the C routines separately you need to give the COBOL some way
of knowing where the entry point 'csub1' is. If you just have one C routine
you can link it as a DLL of the same name, then COBOL will find it; however
it's more likely you've got a load of routines linked into one DLL. In order
that the COBOL program can find these you can load the DLL first, as follows:

    add the definition:

        01 mypointer usage procedure-pointer.

    to the working-storage section, then add the line:

        set mypointer to entry "csub.dll

    in the procedure division. This will load the DLL, and any entry points
in this DLL (e.g. 'csub1') will now be available. It's probably a good idea
to put in a test immediately after this line; if the pointer comes back bad
then it didn't find (or failed to load) the DLL.

3. Anther way of telling the COBOL program where to find the entry points is
to create an import library. For example:

    cl /c csub.obj
    cbllink /d/k csub.obj

    - this will create 'csub.dll', but it will also create 'csub.lib'.

    cobol cbl1.cbl obj;
    cbllink cbl1.obj csub.lib

    - this creates cbl1.exe, and the import library 'csub.lib' tells the exe
where to find the C entry point.

Hope this helps,


Dan.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: calling c-function or c-program from netexpress-cobol

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398EE172.A9AFBF18@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<8mernr$vre$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <398B1ECA.FEEC8B40@home.com> <398ECC29.3982F098@merant.com>`

```


Danial Clarke wrote:
> 
> James is right that an example would be helpful, but the short answer is
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> }

The above will copy 11 bytes to the string, possibly causing a 114 error
or data corruption. Use 'memcpy(a, "ABCDEFGHIJ", 10);' and then you
don't have to worry about dealing with the trailing nul byte even if the
COBOL data item was big enough.

Cheers, Kev.
```

```


Zoran Bujandric wrote:

> Hi Johan!
> Are you possibly suggesting us to give up Clarion and programming, and
> start playing Internet e-mail "chain letters" or what?

He's suggesting that people who read comp.lang.clarion, comp.lang.clipper,
comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects, comp.lang.clos, comp.lang.clu,
comp.lang.cobol, comp.lang.delphi, comp.lang.delphi.databases, or
comp.lang.clarion are likely chumps.  He's under the impression that he
has a better chance of finding fools here than finding people who have the
know how and inclination to make him and his kind pay.

I doubt if he will find big enough fools here, but it was easier to use a
spamming program to catch all newsgroups than to be selective.

Maybe all newsgroup FAQs should include instructions on how we can do our
part to make such spamming less comfortable.
```

## Thread: MOTHER FINDS $71000 IN SONS CLOSET — 1 msgs

### Re: MOTHER FINDS $71000 IN SONS CLOSET

- **From:** "Ken Russom" <kenr@cddn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.clarion,comp.lang.clipper,comp.lang.clipper.visual-objects,comp.lang.clos,comp.lang.clu,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.delphi,comp.lang.delphi.databases,comp.lang.dylan,comp.lang.eiffel
- **Message-ID:** `<sop4prku7s177@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8mfodb$i5d219@news.cablenet.ne.jp>`

```
Not meaning to be rude, I'm having a hard time getting this to fit my abc
templates.  Oh yeah, this isn't code it's, well, ur, just what is this 'meat
in a square can'?

<pcrqre@apexmail.com> wrote in message
news:8mfodb$i5d219@news.cablenet.ne.jp...
> Subject:  Parent of 15 year old finds $71,000 in closet!
> Date: 01 Aug  2000 12:59:08 -0400
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You most likely have just seen this story recently featured on a major
nightly news program (USA).
>
> His mother was cleaning and putting laundry away when she came across a
large brown paper bag
> that was suspiciously buried beneath some clothes and a skateboard in the
back of her 15-year-old
> son's closet. Nothing could have prepared her for the shock she got when
she opened the bag and
> found it was full of cash. Five dollar bills, twenties, fifties and
hundreds - all neatly rubber-banded in
> labeled piles.
>
> "My first thought was that he had robbed a bank", says the 41-year-old
woman, "There was over
> $71,000 dollars in that bag - that's more than my husband earns in a
year".
>
> The woman immediately called her husband at the car-dealership where he
worked to tell him what
> she'd discovered. He came home right away and they drove together to the
boy's school and
> picked him up. Little did they suspect that where the money came from was
more shocking than
> actually finding it in the closet.
>
> As it turns out, the boy had been sending out via E-mail on the Internet a
type of 'chain-letter' to
> E-mail addresses that he obtained off of the Internet. Everyday after
school for the past 2 months,
> he had been doing this right on his computer in his bedroom.
>
> "I just got the E-mail one day and I figured what the heck, I put my name
on it like the instructions
> said and I started sending it out", says the clever 15-year-old.
>
> The E-mail letter listed 3 addresses and contained instructions to send
one $5 dollar bill to the
> person at the top of the list, then delete that address and move the other
2 addresses up, and finally
> to add your name to the bottom of the list. The letter goes on to state
that you would receive
> several thousand dollars in five dollar bills within 2 weeks if you sent
out the letter with your name
> at the bottom of the 3-address list "I get junk E-mail all the time, and I
really didn't think it was
> gonna work", the boy continues.
>
> Within the first few days of sending out the E-mail, the Post Office Box
that his parents had gotten
> him for his video-game magazine subscriptions began to fill up with not
magazines, but envelopes
> containing $5 dollar bills.
>
> "About a week later I rode [my bike] down to the post office and my box
had 1 magazine and
> about 300 envelopes stuffed in it. There was also a yellow slip that said
I had to go up to the [post
> office] counter- I thought I was in trouble or something (laughs)". He
goes on, "I went up to the
> counter and they had a whole box of more mail for me. I had to ride back
home and empty out my
> backpack 'cause I couldn't carry it all".
>
> Over the next few weeks, the boy continued sending out the E-mail. "The
money just kept coming
> in and I just kept sorting it and stashing it in the closet, I barely had
time for my homework". He
> had also been riding his bike to several of the area's banks and
exchanging the $5 bills for twenties,
> fifties and hundreds. "I didn't want the banks to get suspicious so I kept
riding to different banks
> with like five thousand at a time in my backpack. I would usually tell the
lady at the bank counter
> that my dad had sent me in [to exchange the money] and he was outside
waiting for me. One time
> the lady gave me a really strange look and told me that she wouldn't be
able to do it for me and my
> dad would have to come in and do it, but I just rode to the next bank down
the street (laughs).
>
> " Surprisingly, the boy didn't have any reason to be afraid. The reporting
news team examined and
> investigated the so-called 'chain-letter' the boy was sending out and
found that it wasn't a
> chain-letter at all. In fact, it was completely legal according to US
Postal and Lottery Laws, Title
> 18, Section 1302 and 1341, or Title 18, Section 3005 in the US code, also
in the code of federal
> regulations, Volume 16, Sections 255 and 436, which state a product or
service must be
> exchanged for money received.
>
> Every five dollar bill that he received contained a little note that read,
"Please add me to your
> mailing list". This simple note made the letter legal because he was
exchanging a service (adding the
> purchaser's name to his mailing list) for a five dollar fee.
>
> Here is the letter that the 15-year-old was sending out by E-mail, you can
do the exact same thing
> he was doing, simply by following the instructions in this letter.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Send the person at the top of the list a $5 bill wrapped in 2 pieces of
paper (to securely hide it),
> along with a note that says: "Please add me to your mailing list".
>
> Then delete that name, move the other 2 up and put your name at the
bottom.
>
> Now start sending this ENTIRE e-mail back out to people.
>
> When 20 people receive it, those 20 people will move your name up to the
middle position and
> they will each send out 20. That totals 400 people that will receive this
letter with your name in the
> middle.
>
> Then, those 400 people will move your name up to the top and they will
each send out 20 E-mails.
> That totals 8,000 people that will receive this E-mail with your name at
the top and they will each
> send you a $5 bill.
>
> 8,000 people each sending you a $5 bill = $40,000 cash. That's if everyone
responds to this
> E-mail, but not everyone will, so you can expect more realistically to
receive about $10,000 in cash
> ($5 bills) in your mailbox.
>
> This will work for anyone, anywhere in the world in any country, but send
only a US CASH $5 bill.
>
>
> The more E-mails you send out, the more cash you will receive. If each
person sends out 100
> E-mails, there will be 1,000,000 people that receive this letter when your
name reaches the top. If
> only 1% of those people respond, you will still get $50,000 cash.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> THERE'S NOTHING MORE TO DO. When your name reaches the top in a few days,
you will
> start receiving $5 bills from other people just like yourself, who are
willing to invest a $5 bill to
> receive $10,000 cash.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Text File To Windows CSV format — 3 msgs

### Text File To Windows CSV format

- **From:** Assad Saeed <assad101@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mki6u$nps$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi all COBOL Lover!

I need your help. I have some application designed in COBOL running
unders SCO UNIX environment. Other way, I have MS Access 2000 database
application running under WIn NT.

Is is possile to get the data out from cobol into windows MS Access
i.e. CSV file or flat file...

I guess there will be some middleware apps to help resolve this
issue... Since I don't have any experience of COBOL / unix, I
delebratly need your help, All I need is simple text file in windows,
the rest I can handle

Thank you very much for your precious time in reading my mail...

Assad Saeed
Asst. Manager (MIS)
Adamjee Insurance Co. Ltd.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Text File To Windows CSV format

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik-spm@siber.org>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<398DEDC4.ADCCE63F@siber.org>`
- **References:** `<8mki6u$nps$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
DataFile Readers (specifically, Data2Flat) from Siber Systems 
http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/ can do it.

The new version that reads more Cobol data types
will soon be out. It imporves reading of several
Cobol datafile types.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov

Assad Saeed wrote:
> 
> Hi all COBOL Lover!
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Text File To Windows CSV format

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mkuhq$auk$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8mki6u$nps$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I do not know your compiler, so OLE may not be available, but that
would be the easiest way to do it.  Certainly you could write a Cobol
program to export it to a CSV file, though you would need the file
layouts ... you need those for any approach though.

<a id="t-e7f2160451"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu dll Path Question — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu dll Path Question

- **From:** steveei@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mjudo$ann$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
About a year ago, I used Fujitsu COBOL 3.0 to compile a callable COBOL
program, originally running on our IBM mainframe, as a dll.  The COBOL
program has no I/O except through the LINKAGE SECTION.  The resultant
dll is called from VB6.  That dll, and all the Fujitsu 3.0 run-time
dll's, are kept in the same folder with the VB6-produced .exe when
running production, and in the same folder as the VB6 .vbp when doing
development work.

This past week I modified the COBOL source and recompiled the dll.
The new dll works fine on all tested PC's when running the VB6 .exe.
And, on my PC, it's fine from the .vbp.  However, other developers are
getting "file not found" VB errors when calling my dll.  They can get
past these errors by putting the full path name in the Declare, such
as "\\Server1\Test\MyID\MyApp\new.dll", rather than just "new.dll".
However, we didn't need the full path name previously.  How can I
insure that Windows 95 developer PC's automatically look for the dll in
the folder where the program is being executed from?

By the way, I tried adding:
     CurDir = App.Path
to the VB code but that didn't help.  Registering the dll with regsvr32
also did not help.

Thanks for any ideas.

Steve Eisenberg   Wynnewood, Pennsylvania   U.S.A.

Before you buy.

<a id="t-80e3645a75"></a>
## Thread: To Read an Excel-Sheet from Cobol program — 10 msgs

### To Read an Excel-Sheet from Cobol program

- **From:** achimneher@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mmg1l$21v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I want to read an Excel-Sheet from an Cobol Program (ACUCOBOL) to store
the calculated data in different cobol files.

One possibility is to store the excel-sheet as a csv-file and to
interpret it in the cobol program.

But i would like to read the xls-file directly.

Does anybody knows a cobol-product, which can directly read excel files
and gives the possibility to interpret this in a cobol program.

Thank you very much


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: To Read an Excel-Sheet from Cobol program

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mp47n$fi1$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8mmg1l$21v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<achimneher@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8mmg1l$21v$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I want to read an Excel-Sheet from an Cobol Program (ACUCOBOL) to store
> the calculated data in different cobol files.

If you can expect Excel to be installed on the host computer that you want
this task accomplished on, you can use Excel via OLE Automation in Acucobol
5.0 to do this. This is pretty neat, you will then be able to control Excel
from within Acucobol. If you choose to, the user will not see Excel when you
do this. Using Excel this way, you can access each particular cell of the
spreadsheet as you like.

Here is a Word example using OLE Automation from Acucobol to print a preview
of a xls file:

      * Create "Word.Application" object
             create @Application of @Word
                 handle in word-app.

      * Make Word invisible
             modify word-app @Visible = 0.

      * Open file
             modify word-app @Documents::Open("demo.doc",0,1)
                 giving word-doc.

      * Print Preview
             modify word-doc @PrintPreview().
             modify word-app @Visible = 1.

      * Destroy the objects
           destroy word-doc.
           destroy word-app.

If your host computer does not have Excel, you will need to check out if
there are any 3rd party companies that offers an xls "engine" either as a
DLL or as an ActiveX. I am certain there are, yet I don't know of any.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To Read an Excel-Sheet from Cobol program

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ne8sv$8d5$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<8mmg1l$21v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8mp47n$fi1$1@news.cerf.net> <8nc2n5$82l$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
"Simon R Hart" <s.hart@mdfs.co.uk> wrote in message news:8nc2n5>
>spreadsheet as you like.

> Is that Cobol? Looks like a new language to me.

Good question, and I understand the question. One may claim it is not Cobol
in the sense that it is not a valid part of any Cobol standard. However, as
of the current Cobol standards no such thing as OLE Automation is covered,
hence to support it, one had to add extensions I guess.

So, yes it is Cobol, although proprietary to Acucobol.

On the other side, I have seen support for OLE Automation in a couple of
languages, and I can assure you, this one is not one of the worst.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To Read an Excel-Sheet from Cobol program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** srhartone@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nglm8$jn7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8mmg1l$21v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8mp47n$fi1$1@news.cerf.net> <8nc2n5$82l$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <8ne8sv$8d5$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
In article <8ne8sv$8d5$1@news.cerf.net>,
  "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
wrote:
> "Simon R Hart" <s.hart@mdfs.co.uk> wrote in
message news:8nc2n5>
> >spreadsheet as you like.
>
> > Is that Cobol? Looks like a new language to
me.
>
> Good question, and I understand the question.
One may claim it is not Cobol
> in the sense that it is not a valid part of any
Cobol standard. However, as
> of the current Cobol standards no such thing as
OLE Automation is covered,
> hence to support it, one had to add extensions
I guess.
>
> So, yes it is Cobol, although proprietary to
Acucobol.
>
> On the other side, I have seen support for OLE
Automation in a couple of
> languages, and I can assure you, this one is
not one of the worst.
>
> Cheesle
>
>

OLE Automation is a technology not specific to
any lanaguage at all.

A language should not have "extra" syntax just so
that it supports the latest technology.
If this were the case in 5 years time we would
end up with a language thicker than the bible!

Instead, as OLE Automation is OO based it should
only be called from OO based "standard" syntax.
For example:

If I wanted to create an instance of my Word
program then I would:

   invoke wordapp "new" returning wordServer

Then get the current document:

   invoke wordServer "getActiveDocument"
returning  theDocument

Then print it:

   invoke theDocument "PrintOut" using by value 0

This is the same as calling a file handler to get
a record:

   invoke Filehandler "new" returning filehand

   invoke filehand "getRecord" returning filehand-
Rec

   invoke filehand "print" using filehand-rec


Simon


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: mbp Visual Cobol Batch File — 11 msgs

### mbp Visual Cobol Batch File

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IcJj5.13193$SM4.1263203@nntp2.onemain.com>`

```
My apologies for the lack of knowledge and experience I'll exhibit in asking
these questions.  I'm primarly a Win/Web programmer that has been asked to
assist in dissolving a complication in upgrading a mbr/vcobol to 32-bit.

Our requirement is that we have a routine that will execute a batch file,
wait for its completion, and continue the program flow.  Not a spectacular
requirement by any means, but one that has presented problems.

Using the "native" CHAINR command and calling the winapi
CreateProcess/WaitForSingleObject routines has yielded the same result: The
calling and called program deadlocks.  If you kill either process, the other
continues as intended.

CreateProcess is called in a pedestrian manner that has worked well for me
in other places.  CHAINR has been called with various arguments.  Some of
the flailing attempts have been:

CMD.EXE /c d:\bob.bat
CMD.EXE /c START /wait CMD.EXE /c d:\bob.bat

Are we doing something wrong, or does mbr have a specific difficulty with NT
4.0?  Also, what is the best way to do this?
```

#### ↳ Re: mbp Visual Cobol Batch File

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39918b66.52494267@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<IcJj5.13193$SM4.1263203@nntp2.onemain.com>`

```
Try COMMAND.COM where you have CMD.EXE

<no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net> wrote:

>these questions.  I'm primarly a Win/Web programmer that has been asked to
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

```

Or better yet you can determine the name and location of the command
interpreter on Windows 9x, NT or 2000 using:

DISPLAY "COMSPEC" UPON ENVIRONMENT-NAME
ACCEPT MY-COMSPEC FROM ENVIRONMENT-VALUE

-Karl

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-12T00:00:00

```
Indeed, this is being done, but the problem seems to lie in a messaging
deadlock between the Command Line Interpreter (CMD.EXE or COMMAND.COM) and
the calling process.  I've implemented an approach that uses
MsgWaitForMultipleObjects and PeekMessage, but have yet to test it on NT.

```
COMMAND.COM behave similarly.  MS-Tech indicates that COMMAND.COM on an NT
basically passes the command off to CMD.EXE for processing.

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:39918b66.52494267@207.126.101.100...
> Try COMMAND.COM where you have CMD.EXE
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >My apologies for the lack of knowledge and experience I'll exhibit in
asking
> >these questions.  I'm primarly a Win/Web programmer that has been asked
to
> >assist in dissolving a complication in upgrading a mbr/vcobol to 32-bit.
> >
> >Our requirement is that we have a routine that will execute a batch file,
> >wait for its completion, and continue the program flow.  Not a
spectacular
> >requirement by any means, but one that has presented problems.
> >
> >Using the "native" CHAINR command and calling the winapi
> >CreateProcess/WaitForSingleObject routines has yielded the same result:
The
> >calling and called program deadlocks.  If you kill either process, the
other
> >continues as intended.
> >
> >CreateProcess is called in a pedestrian manner that has worked well for
me
> >in other places.  CHAINR has been called with various arguments.  Some of
> >the flailing attempts have been:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Are we doing something wrong, or does mbr have a specific difficulty with
NT
> >4.0?  Also, what is the best way to do this?
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

> COMMAND.COM behave similarly.  MS-Tech indicates that COMMAND.COM on an NT

these differences, check out AUTOEXEC.BAT.

I won't swear to it, but "2.4.17" is from ABOUT 1989 - so I do think that it
is a "little out of date".  One thing about the MF products is that (with the
correct directives) you can usually "emulate" earlier products with a current
product.  Is there a reason that you want this particular release?
```

<a id="t-d67cd1192f"></a>
## Thread: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive? — 48 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@mch6.siemens.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de>`

```
2000-08-08 18:10:00 MEST

I just happen to read in http://www.ucc.ie/xml/ :

   C.4   Which parts of an XML document are case-sensitive?

   All of it, both markup and text. This is significantly
   different from HTML and most other SGML document types.
   It was introduced to allow markup in non-Latin-alphabet
   languages and to obviate problems with case-folding in
   scripts which are caseless.

Now the analoguous to OO-Cobol. "Problems", the text says,
and problems we discussed recently - think of the
round-trip conversion of 'ï¿½' to 'SS' in Cobol Names.

Whatif OO-Cobol-names were to become case-sensitive? What
would that do to existing applications?

We could have that with a directive like

   >> CASE SENSITIVITY IS ON

etc, - then, existing applications would be unaffected
entirely.

Compilers on machines such as the Telefunken TR4
(which have 6 bits characters) could ignore this
directive or reject it. (But then again, I do not
think that a TR 4 could host a living cobol-compiler.)

Just a thought. If it is a stupid one, tell me why.

Herwig
```

#### ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de>`

```
Case sensitivity is one of the biggest mistakes ever foisted on the programming
community. The folks who brought us UNIX and C made their stuff case-sensitive
because they were too lazy to write their code in a user-friendly way. 

Just think of the number of errors resulting from "clever" programmers using
variables with the same names except for some uppercase some lowercase letter
in some position.

No thanks. Give me UNICODE and give me insensitivty to case (at least in my
compilers).

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@mch6.siemens.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39903558.2B631FFA@mch6.siemens.de>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com>`

```
2000-08-08 18:20:22 MEST

S Comstock wrote:
>
> ...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> in some position.

Oh no. Programmers doing this intentionally
ought to be shot - but that cannot be a cause
for dismissing this language feature.

But then, your statement favours some sort of
switching case-sensitivity on and leave it off
by default.

Just my present opinion. I believe J4 has discussed
this already, and by some good reason, case-sensitivity
in names has not been proposed.

Herwig
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
