- **Message-ID:** `<LBck5.50$7y1.34714@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com> <39903558.2B631FFA@mch6.siemens.de> <bB_j5.56$W03.26718@nnrp1.sbc.net> <399085FB.124BE12@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```
Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
wrote in message news:399085FB.124BE12@Josella-Simone-Playton.de...
>
> Then you ought to like the obfuscating C contest. C is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Exuse me, but I was the winner (?) of the 1998 "International Obfuscated
Basic Code Contest."

My winning (?) entry:

$IF 0
  Obfuscation Contest Entry
  Requires: PB 3.2 or PB 3.5 for DOS
  Rated: PG-13 (D)
$ENDIF

$LIB ALL OFF

DEFINT A-Z
DIM V AS BYTE PTR
READ N: REDIM Z(N-1) AS DWORD: DIM ABSOLUTE B(N*4-1) AS BYTE AT VARSEG
(Z(0))
COLOR 7,1:CLS:V=pbvScrnBuff+1800
FOR I=0 TO N-1: READ Z(I):FOR J=0 TO 3:@V=B(I*4+J): INCR V:DELAY .05:INCR
V:NEXT,
END
DATA 9,1936943435,544820512,544437057,1293971017,662266721
DATA 1917198451,544501359,1684957527,538998639,10


I could port it to Microsoft Qbasic if anyone is interested, with one
caveat: because QBasic does not support unsigned integers, bytes, absolute
arrays or pointers, I'd have to code in a kludge and might end up with code
which is somewhat obfuscated.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39915B98.3DB1D4F1@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com> <39903558.2B631FFA@mch6.siemens.de> <bB_j5.56$W03.26718@nnrp1.sbc.net> <399085FB.124BE12@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```
Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote:

> > I once ran into a FORTRAN program in which all variable were a
> > differing number of dollar-signs. It had statements like:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming language.

Cobol can match the example above.  But it doesn't matter how obfuscated
the code can be once it passes a certain threshold.  There certainly are
COBOL programs which have been written at a level which makes it cheaper
to throw away the code and start over rather than do maintenance.

It is possible to write bad code in any language.  Some languages make
it easier and more natural to write readable and maintainable code.  But
fools can be so ingenious!

Eschew obfuscation!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cGXj5.255$EP4.118390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com>`

```
S Comstock <scomstock@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com...
> Case sensitivity is one of the biggest mistakes ever foisted on the
programming
> community. The folks who brought us UNIX and C made their stuff
case-sensitive
> because they were too lazy to write their code in a user-friendly way.
>[snipped]
> No thanks. Give me UNICODE and give me insensitivty to case (at least in
my
> compilers).
>

No, it wasn't laziness, it was a combination of genetics and revenge.

Unix and C were brought to us by a virtually all-male group who were sick
and tired of women constantly complaining that men were not sensitve. Well,
they showed 'em.

Defense rests.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39907404.B683414A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com> <cGXj5.255$EP4.118390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
2000-08-08 22:55:22 MEST

Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> ...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

I have the dim impression that Unix&C community does not mix
too well with the Cobol community, given the statements in
this thread. How come? There are renown Cobol-Compilers
for Unix, and their users do not suffer in any way - well.
in the majority of cases.

What would be a better marriage than the language which
is the workhorse of financial computing with the
operating system that does not share the clumsiness
of many mainframe operating systems and that does not
share the non-reliability of Windows?

Oops, somebody put please the grammar right ...

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oD3k5.213$7d5.74349@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com> <cGXj5.255$EP4.118390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39907404.B683414A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```

"Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton"
>
> What would be a better marriage than the language which
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

10,000,000,000 lines of code, 10,000,000 programs, and 500,000
programmers would have to be scrapped, rewritten, or retrained to run
on an operating system used by a vanishingly small percentage of
computers.

As you say, COBOL works on Unix; so it's not COBOL that's holding back
the marriage. COBOL is, after all, (mostly) portable and could be
moved to a Unix box. Wonder why no one's thought of that? Or maybe
they have?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Marriage of Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3991F0C8.A1CB2200@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com> <cGXj5.255$EP4.118390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39907404.B683414A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```
I can detail a number of idiocies in the IBM mainframe operating system
OS390 and do not have similar familiarity with UNIX and C.  However,
from the reading I have done and the brief playing with Linux, my
impression is that Unix and C are every bit as arcane, user surly and
clumsy as OS390 and assembler.  It is just that the manifestations are
different.  Each environment has functions that work easily and are
straight forward that the other environment has to go through
contortions to duplicate.   The people who came up with JCL condition
codes and the negative way they work should be forced to share the same
cell in hell with the people came up with a case sensitive command
language.  My guess is that the old Burroughs high-end operating systems
have both OS390 and UNIX beat in terms of user friendliness but
Burroughs wasn't able to deliver a product that was considered reliable
by the marketplace.

>From a COBOL programmer's point of view, a skeptical approach to all
operating systems with a willingness to learn and adapt is the best way
to go.    

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca

Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote:
> 
> 2000-08-08 22:55:22 MEST
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> GruberStrasse 10 A / D-85655 GrossHelfenDorf / Bayern / EU
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Marriage of Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@mch6.siemens.de>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3992AC75.F5054F77@mch6.siemens.de>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <20000808121330.12193.00000051@ng-df1.aol.com> <cGXj5.255$EP4.118390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <39907404.B683414A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <3991F0C8.A1CB2200@istar.ca>`

```
2000-08-10 14:55:41 MESZ

Clark F. Morris, Jr. wrote:
>
> I can detail a number ...
>

One of the facts of live is that, allthough there may be
a willingness to learn, the major part of learning is done
in the younger days. Whether you were brought up with emacs,
vi, EDT or whatever, with 40 the competent programmer has
archived some virtuosity in the working-environment - not of
his choice but where fate has put him. The ease of use is an
entirely subjective concept. With advancing age, a slowly
solififying concept.

It is extremly unwise to force somebody to work with unfamiliar
working environment. Rather, the art of platform-hopping must
be exercised in order to archive ones best with the most
suitable plattform - and for every activity and every plattform,
there is another preference.

Of course, in every project, there is a final production
platform where integration takes place. But for most other
activites, there is no universal way of doing things suited
for everybody.

There may be objective viewpoints for selecting a preferable
working environment. But this can be a criterion for private
interests and private activities only, because only there one
has a free choice. In the programming shop, one oftenly has
to use what one gets - and one sadly will fall back behind of
what one could archive under better conditions.

Those who incidentaly may work with their prefered working
environment often do not see that others are working under
less fortunate conditions.
```

better than 2, if they ever get low level compilers.

>But this all has nothing to do with Cobol nor with any other
>language. The Unix-World happens to be closer to my skin, thats all.

>> From a COBOL programmer's point of view, a skeptical approach to

>> the best way to go.
>
>Certainly so.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mqimj$ja4$1@news.igs.net>`

James J. Gavan wrote in message <39906EDD.E67D38DF@home.com>...
>
>
>donald tees wrote:
>>
>> Case sensitivety in language is the work of the devil and from the >
spawn of satan.
>> ;<)
>
>As I recall, you don't believe in the Guy 'upstairs'. So how come a
>reference to the gent down below ?

THE IDEA OF COBOL CONTEXT SENSITIVE TO CASE HAS CONVERTED ME.
i think we should make the first letter after the "." a capital, to
emphasize the period. Then we can get rid of all this new fanged end shit.
The term "capitol offence" was designed specifically to deal with this type
of thinking.
```

understanding. My spelling is not good, but much better since I began using

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8n4foo$ddv$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <8mpp48$5lf$1@news.igs.net> <39906EDD.E67D38DF@home.com> <8mqimj$ja4$1@news.igs.net> <IQKk5.13548$gW5.861316@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8n10h4$bv2$1@news.igs.net> <14Zk5.7429$4T.434961@bgtnsc07-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8n3ka8$sg5$1@news.igs.net> <aggl5.33245$RG6.2593147@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Yes, I know what you mean.  I have been following NG's now for about
fifteen years.  It is astonishing how writing skills improve, and how much
you can learn by just reading an echo every day for a while.  I have gotten
into the habit of lurking in the professional echoes for topics that
interest
me.  It does not take long to learn the lingo.

I've been reading all the discussions on the up/down/every which way, and it
strikes me that it is akin to how many angels can dance on the head of a
pin.

I am up to my eyeballs in code.  Samantha came walking in at three today,
and said "what the hell are you working on a Saturday for?".   I said
"Saturday ???   Shit, I meant to go to the market.  I thought it was
Friday".

I am re-working my design.  Re-structuring the classes.  A real problem
that I have is that from a code perspective, the classes should be organized
in one manner, but from a system standpoint, they should designed from
a completely different direction. You almost need a two dimensioned
inheritance tree ....

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3992F968.72218E52@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <3992B5F8.65DC04C1@zip.com.au>`

```
An interesting side note is that the case insensitivity of COBOL (and
IBM operating systems) works because they are based on the English
alphabet where there is a one for one relationship between upper and
lower case.  This isn't true for all languages, something I found out in
a session on internationalization.  In addition, Unicode has multiple
ways of representing the same printed character for some of the accented
vowels in other languages.  This is due to historical reasons but it is
going to make life interesting.  While I too fervently believe that case
sensitivity is a bad thing in a computing language or command language,
there are interesting complications.  I think that I read Unix case
sensitivity was done to allow more short commands that were readable but
my memory was never perfect.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc., cfmtech@istar.ca  

Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Herwig Huener wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39930A55.BE7E839B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <3992B5F8.65DC04C1@zip.com.au> <3992F968.72218E52@istar.ca>`

```
I always wished that they had thought through sorting needs before they
developed character codes.  I suppose now we can brute force it - provided
we have some standards.

What should be the standards for sorting international addresses with mixed
case, accented letters, etc.?  Do such standards exist?  We should be able
to pass a parm saying that a sort field is dictionary?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mv8jp$k22$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <3992B5F8.65DC04C1@zip.com.au> <3992F968.72218E52@istar.ca> <39930A55.BE7E839B@brazee.net>`

```
Yes there are ISO Standards for both "sorting" and "matching/folding" of
upper-/lower-case letters.  MOSTLY, the draft Standard is simply referring to
them (both for FUNCTION UPPER-CASE/LOWER-CASE *and* for equivalence in
user-defined words).

UNFORTUNATELY, there are a "few" cases that cause particular problems.  Easy
example is what to do with the German "�".  Right now there is a "raging"
debate in a German group about what to do if you wanted to "capitalize" the
sentence,

"Er bem�hte sich in besonderem Ma�e, die Masse  des Atoms zu bestimmen."

and whether it "upper-cases" to

"ER BEM�HTE SICH IN BESONDEREM MA�E, DIE MASSE DES ATOMS ZU BESTIMMEN."

  and NOT

 "ER BEM�HTE SICH IN BESONDEREM MASSE, DIE MASSE DES ATOMS ZU BESTIMMEN."

This is a case where a single lower-case letter either has NO upper-case
equivalent - or where the equivalent is two characters (that are also the
upper-case equivalent of two separate lower-case letters).

  ****

There are also examples (such as a Turkish "dot-less i") where two different
lower-case letters have the SAME upper-case letter.

Bottom-Line:
  This is work going on between the COBOL internationalize group and the
group dealing with "character sets" to TRY and figure out what COBOL should
do about this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<39932973.9693589E@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <3992B5F8.65DC04C1@zip.com.au> <3992F968.72218E52@istar.ca> <39930A55.BE7E839B@brazee.net> <8mv8jp$k22$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Yes, we can sort to any shop standard we want to create.  That is not the same as
having a "dictionary" standard.  We can sort by our coding sequence or other
recognized coding sequence or we have to create our own sequence.  What we want
is dictionary sequence (if there is an adequately defined standard here, I
suspect Coï¿½rdinate, coordinate, & coï¿½rdinate may sort as ties)- and we want it to
be sorted the same way in a different environment.


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Yes there are ISO Standards for both "sorting" and "matching/folding" of
> upper-/lower-case letters.  MOSTLY, the draft Standard is simply referring to
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main
- **Message-ID:** `<8mvcgr$kth$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <3992B5F8.65DC04C1@zip.com.au> <3992F968.72218E52@istar.ca> <39930A55.BE7E839B@brazee.net> <8mv8jp$k22$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <39932973.9693589E@brazee.net>`

```
The sort Standards are being developed for "dictionary sorts".  These are
Country/Language "dependent"  (For example, French/Canadian has a different
sort sequence than French/France - I think.)

These sorting standards ARE being developed by "experts" in the field.  In
the draft Standard, you will be able to switch to whichever sort-system you
want EITHER at compile-time or run-time.

Howard - or anyone else,
  If you want me to give you a URL for looking at what is being done in this
area, please email me - or post it in the NG.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Sort sequences was Re: A daring thought: OO-Cobol names case-sensitive?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39935E44.7C55209@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3990306B.D4F79AC3@mch6.siemens.de> <3992B5F8.65DC04C1@zip.com.au> <3992F968.72218E52@istar.ca> <39930E67.F753588F@brazee.net>`

```
This is even more complicated than it may seem.  There is no collating
sequence that can properly put mixed case English into sequence let
alone most of the foreign languages.  The designers of Unicode - the 16
bit character encoding of all of the world's living alphabets plus most
symbols used (the plus sign, the have a nice day or happy face - hex
263A, etc.) have specifically stated that no collating sequence is to be
assumed for the character set.  Indeed reading the Unicode Standard is a
mind boggling exercise.  For historical reasons many of the characters
in European alphabets can be represented either by 1 character or a
character plus a non-spacing modifying character.  As this becomes more
widespread the semantics of comparison become more complicated to say
the least. Ordering rules differ between languages and even within
American English major encyclopedias differ in the application of the
rules.  We are leaving behind the days of all upper case letters.  Those
who have had to deal with other than the English language and English
alphabet have probably known these complications for years.  I can see
promises by the major sort vendors that their nationally sensitive sorts
using double byte character sets (or even an upper lower case standard
European 8 bit character set such as ISO 8859-1) will take only 20 times
the CPU cycles more than 1 that just uses either the standard ASCII or
EBCDIC.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca

> The additional point is that case insensitivity means that if I make a
> MIstake in my capitalization somewhere it does not break the compile.

Furthermore, compilers shouldn't be spelling sensitive.  If I misspell

mistakes.

## Thread: batch/cics interface — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### batch/cics interface

- **From:** nole_daddy@mindspring.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3990505a.12395028@nntp.mindspring.com>`

```
I have been asked to create a batch application that will send input
and receive output from a CICS region.   I think I know how to start a
transaction but  not how to modify the cics app to send output back to
batch rather than to a terminal.  Can someone point me in a direction
where I can find documentation on this ?   BTW - I'm running under MVS
OS/390  and  CICS  4.1

Thanks

Chris Skinner
systems consultant Consultec inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: batch/cics interface

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39902c85.2506323@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<3990505a.12395028@nntp.mindspring.com>`

```
On Tue, 08 Aug 2000 18:34:59 GMT, nole_daddy@mindspring.com wrote:

>I have been asked to create a batch application that will send input
>and receive output from a CICS region.   I think I know how to start a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>systems consultant Consultec inc.


My shop is using this assembler subroutine we found on the internet:
http://www.xephon.com/cgi-bin/xephon2/tdisplay?/c145a01.txt
The subroutine simplifies the call to CICS objects from batch via
EXCI.  It includes support for doing the equivilent of a "CICS LINK
PROGRAM(...)  COMMAREA(...)" from batch.  The COMMAREA is how you pass
data back and forth.

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: batch/cics interface

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<urt1ps8u7ji87hhp3df7fnnskue9ocnvfd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3990505a.12395028@nntp.mindspring.com>`

```
Chris,

your best bet is the EXCI - External CICS Interface.  This allows a (COBOL) batch program
to run a CICS application program by using 

	EXEC CICS LINK PROGRAM('CICSPGM')
                       COMMAREA(My-commarea)
                       NETNAME('PRODCICS')
                       SYNCONRETURN
       END-EXEC

Look at SC33-1944 CICS External Interfaces Guide



On Tue, 08 Aug 2000 18:34:59 GMT, nole_daddy@mindspring.com wrote:

>I have been asked to create a batch application that will send input
>and receive output from a CICS region.   I think I know how to start a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>systems consultant Consultec inc.

#### ↳ Re: batch/cics interface

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2000-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000811201118.17652.00000484@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```

>From: nole_daddy@mindspring.com
>Date: 8/8/00 2:34 PM Eastern Daylight Time

I just got done going thru this exact same thing and have a document on what to
do and how to do it.  I can send it to you when I get to work as I have it
stored there.  Gives copybooks and notates the documentation references.  Also
give directions for testing using Xpediter.

You will want to be sure that your CICS region is set up to use the EXCI (or
DPL calls depending on what you're going to do - same difference) and your
security is in place.  We have RACF and I had the CICS transaction made public
because there is no screen involved and so on.  All covered in the document
except for the region set up as I have no idea what the techies did (something
about setting up an IRC - no - that's not Internet Realtime Chat :D).

It is really slick once it is up and running.

In the mean time get the IBM book - SC33-1833-xx (as long as the xx is > 0) -
External CICS Interface.  Chapters 2 and 10 are the most informative but go
thru the whole thing anyway.  The sample program is ok but I do wonder at times
who writes their code :)

Eileen
>
>I have been asked to create a batch application that will send input
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>systems consultant Consultec inc.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: batch/cics interface

- **From:** "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NCHl5.1103$_E5.815108@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<3990505a.12395028@nntp.mindspring.com> <20000811201118.17652.00000484@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
No one as yet addressed the 'modify the CICS app to send output back to
batch rather than
Terminal' part.

Be aware that the EXCI (EXternal Cics Interface) is ONLY initiated from
OUTSIDE CICS.
ALSO, it is a SUBSET of DPL:  you can NOT have any Terminal I-O and most of
the
ASSIGN ... are NOT available.  There is NO way within CICS to communicate
with Batch
UNLESS Batch is the requestor.  Now, many sites have Console Log exits that
exmaine
all the CICS Write To Operator / Log for specific strings and then can 'kick
off' an MVS job
for you, AND that job can contain a program that is an EXCI CLIENT.

Also read the IBM Manual:  CICS/ESA V4R1 (or later) External CICS Interface
(Document Number SC33-1181-01) AND  Intercommunication Guide  (search the
book on 'EXCI')


P.S., the example code given in IBM manuals is written to be very brief so
you can paste it
into the editor and create a working program very quickly.

Your Systems person(s) will need to ensure that you have an EXCI connection
(VTAM, etc)
as well as probably modifying the startup, because the default does not
allow EXCI.

Please be sure that you batch code checks the return code AND sub-codes.
When IRC is disable OR CICS is down, you get the same erorr.

Good Luck

YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000811201118.17652.00000484@ng-fx1.aol.com...
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: batch/cics interface

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)

```
>Subject: Re: batch/cics interface
>From: "mangogwr" mangogwr@bellsouth.net 
>Date: 8/14/00 12:01 AM Eastern Daylight Time

>No one as yet addressed the 'modify the CICS app to send output back to
>batch rather than
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

All you have said is correct.  Batch must request CICS, CICS cannot request
batch except as described above.

If the original poster will please be more specific on his request of sending
output to batch rather than terminal perhaps we can come up with something
else. In any case my offer of the document still stands.

If you wish to 'display' the screen on say paper then you can use several
different methods for that, one of which requires the printer be defined as a
terminal and you would SEND to that terminal instead of a display terminal, or
you can write a report to a printer via SPOOL commands.

The situation I'm using is the product is scanned, the data is FTP'd to the
mainframe which then has a job that takes the input and formats records which
then does the DPL to CICS to update the VSAM file that is controled by CICS.
(People here will remember my register 15 problem).  

What specifically is your situation (to original poster).  Also try CICS-L for
more information.

Eileen
```

On Sun, 13 Aug 2000 21:01:47 -0700, "mangogwr" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>No one as yet addressed the 'modify the CICS app to send output back to
>batch rather than
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>OUTSIDE CICS.

Well, he wrote that he had to create a batch application that sends input to a CICS
transaction and receives its output - That makes batch the requestor for me <s>

>ALSO, it is a SUBSET of DPL:  you can NOT have any Terminal I-O and most of
>the
>ASSIGN ... are NOT available.

EXCI is a subset of DPL?  Which DPL function is available, that is not part of EXCI?
SYNCONRETURN used to be required, but no longer is.  EXCI and DPL are subsets of the full
CICS API, and that is what you possibly meant

>  There is NO way within CICS to communicate with Batch UNLESS Batch is the requestor.  

Hm - I am not quite sure.  Look a the following scenario:

CICS submits a batch job XYZ, which, actually, is an LU6.2 application

get the idea?  Now CICS can be the requestor, and can communicate with batch the usual
way.  I agree, it is somewhat like taking a 38 ton truck to get a pack of cigarettes, but
it can be done....

Yes, you're correct Volker, that is what I meant.  Sorry, I put in three 50
hour weeks
PLUS, I am replacing my outdoor sprinkler system evenings and weekends.

The 'EXEC SPOOL' is the way you write to the 'IntRdr', or 'card reader' that
CICS
uses.  Be aware however, BEFORE you start coding for this, many sites do nOT
allow this type of activity.

For one thing, they don't want the operators to 'freak' when your job goes
down
and no one knows 'who submitted that Job?'.

## Thread: Flowcharting — 17 msgs

### Flowcharting

- **From:** "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mprsn$ktj$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi
This is a student's question.
Can somebody please let me know, are there any websites
relating how to draw FLOWCHARTS for Programs. ie. I just
need some examples to refer because at the moment
I only use some of the
SQUARE BOXES for process line...
DIAMOND for decision....etc..(the basic ones)

I cant workout how to make a flowchart for the follwing
paragraph in a program..


SORTING-PROCESS.
     SORT   WORK-FILE
     ON ASCENDING CUST-CODE-KEY
     USING  CUSTOMER-FILE
     GIVING SORTED-FILE.


Can someone please give some hints on the above paragraph
for flowcharting.

Thankyou

Best regards
M S
```

#### ↳ Re: Flowcharting

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DAE70E45DD04E7DA.DA50115ECFDEDEFE.C54F27F848E31B39@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<8mprsn$ktj$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Tue, 8 Aug 2000 21:45:48 +0100, "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
enlightened us:

>Hi
>This is a student's question.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There are several ways you could show the SORT in your flowchart.  One
way is to use the defined symbol for SORT which is two triangles
attached at the base.  Another is to use the symbol for a predefined
process, which is a rectangle with interior verticle lines on each
side.

Hope that helps.

Regards,

  
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I have six locks on my door all in a row. When I go out, I lock every
other one. I figure no matter how long somebody stands there picking the
locks, they are always locking three.






Boycott Mitsubishi Industries
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Flowcharting

- **From:** lcccpiasabird@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ms04c$sk6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8mprsn$ktj$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <DAE70E45DD04E7DA.DA50115ECFDEDEFE.C54F27F848E31B39@lp.airnews.net>`

```
In article
<DAE70E45DD04E7DA.DA50115ECFDEDEFE.C54F27F848E31B39@lp.airnews.net>,
  SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:
> On Tue, 8 Aug 2000 21:45:48 +0100, "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
> enlightened us:
>

First of all I would point out the obvious that the flow chart is done
before the program.  Then you take the flowchart and design the
program.

Not every line of code actually converts to a symbol or direction on a
flowchart.  Sort is just a process or a box.  Sort is built in process
in the compiler we dont need to know everything about it.  If you want
to make a separate process for sort where it looks at all possible
option that could be a separate process. This process could include the
files in and out and whether ascending or descending etc.

Your book may give you the complete list of options for all possible
sorts.  The compiler just looks at all of your parameters and executes
a bit of code that it has pre-programmed for sorting.

On a mainfram often the sort is done outside of the COBOL program by a
utility program.  The concept is the same you tell it the file going in
the fields to sort on and what type of sort for each field.  Then you
tell it about the output.

The actually sorting process is far more difficult from ordering a deck
of cards; especially when the deck is one million records; try laying
that out on the table!


> >Hi
> >This is a student's question.
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> other one. I figure no matter how long somebody stands there picking
the
> locks, they are always locking three.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Warren Simmons wrote:

> Flow chart the program on the computer to create documentation for the
> person that follows.

Use a drawing program, or using a computer program to generate the flow chart?

I use flow charts to document job flow (run the accounting feed before the bank
reconciliation), but find that well written COBOL is easier to read than flow
charts.  And generated flow charts don't know what is important, listing
everything.  This certainly is useless.
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

By golly, I know a site like that myself. Back in the 60's our company
selected a computer on the basis of the software among other things.  CSC
was the vendor of the software, and this was one of my assignments.  See
that the COBOL spec would meet our needs.  I spent two weeks with this
group, and they used a Polaroid camera just to keep track of they
developments on the black board.  They also had a "universal" assembler
system they used to work between platforms.  I remember one name only, Owen
Mock.  Long time ago.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flowcharting

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8n6du8$fd4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8mprsn$ktj$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <DAE70E45DD04E7DA.DA50115ECFDEDEFE.C54F27F848E31B39@lp.airnews.net> <8ms04c$sk6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <399320A8.350673EA@larich.com> <sp8e0khjn4t79@corp.supernews.com> <8n3klc$spb$1@news.igs.net> <bggl5.33246$RG6.2593371@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8n4fv1$de6$1@news.igs.net> <9mql5.34407$RG6.2682578@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
    mAYBE THINGS DO NOT (<-- See what happens when you change from COBOL to
human? Note the start in Column 8) change as much as we think.  I just
thought of a flowchart that I used in the last year.  I was down in
Pennsylvania at a customer site, reviewing the data flow between a system
that I wrote and the in house SAP system.  They had a whiteboard with an
RS-232-C interface ... one of the neatest toys I have seen in a long time.
After you sketched on it with a marker, you pressed a button and it
downloaded into your PC as a PC-paint bit map. They had it hooked up to
their network, and I just plugged my PC into a hub. Full colour, four by
four foot whiteboard.  Hate to think what it cost.

We designed a really simple interface.  They would deposit my updates as
flat files in a specific directory on the net, and I would poll that
directory at specific intervals.  When I found a file, I would do  batch
update, and move the file to either an "OKAY" directory, or to an "ERROR"
directory.  I would also leave transactions that I originated in a fourth
directory, and would do my backup to a fifth.  We sketched all that out as
flowcharts on the board, and each grabbed our copy.  Then we headed back to
our respective locales (they to Europe, me to Canada, the US guys to
Chicago, etc.) and we did the rest by Email.

"Universal Assemblers" used to be common, though I only used one once(I
evolved it out of the need for migration).  I think most of them were really
just particularly good macro assemblers that somebody decided to use the
macro facility to write code for a different platform.  That is what
happened to me.  I used to write all my assembler using an extensive macro
library.  I expanded the macro library to include several OS's, then finally
to several CPU's before I retired the code.  I have re-written most of the
code in Cobol now ... it seems really wasteful to write something like a
device driver in Cobol, but it is a lot easier to maintain and keep current.
Pretty cheap, too, when I can devote an entire computer to an I/O subsystem,
and hook it into a world sized net for a thousand bucks worth of parts.

…[43 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi M S.
Being a student myself I know what you mean.

Personally I take each COBOL `verb` ( in your example the SORT verb ) and
translate it to the respective flowchart symbol. So being that the SORT verb
uses INPUT and OUTPUT files, I would use the respective INPUT/OUTPUT symbol.

Hope this helps. ( If you can understand it )

Colonel
psxzone@btinternet.com

#### ↳ Re: Flowcharting

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3992c354.947156@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<8mprsn$ktj$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Tue, 8 Aug 2000 21:45:48 +0100, M S wrote:

>This is a student's question.
>Can somebody please let me know, are there any websites
>relating how to draw FLOWCHARTS for Programs. 

Use your favorite search engine.  I entered "flowchart symbols" into
http://www.snap.com/ , and it came up with several web pages
containing information on flowchart symbols and their application.

[snippage]
>I cant workout how to make a flowchart for the follwing
>paragraph in a program..
[...]
>     SORT   WORK-FILE
>     ON ASCENDING CUST-CODE-KEY
[...]
>Can someone please give some hints on the above paragraph
>for flowcharting.

I have seen SORTs diagrammed using any of the PROCESS (rectangle),
PREDEFINED PROCESS (rectangle with double vertical lines), or
AUXILIARY PROCESS (square) symbols.

You might see this diagrammed as:
       |
       |
       V
---------------
|| Sort      ||
|| WORK-FILE ||
||       ... ||
---------------
       |
       |
```

## Thread: COBOL and Website CGI Scripting — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### COBOL and Website CGI Scripting

- **From:** horsupport@aol.com (Horsupport)
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000807232007.27599.00001107@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
Any way to use COBOL to write CGI scripts in developing websites?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Website CGI Scripting

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlfvos8mtnsks6jcsekld40s1a5uh66nuo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20000807232007.27599.00001107@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
On 08 Aug 2000 03:20:07 GMT, horsupport@aol.com (Horsupport) wrote:

>Any way to use COBOL to write CGI scripts in developing websites?

Sure, 

Create an html file similar to this:
 <html>
 <head>
 <title>Trying a CGI program</title>
 </head>
 <body>
 <h1 align="center">Trying a CGI Program</h1>
 <p>Fill in the appropriate data, then press the "SUBMIT" button
 </p>
 <form method="get" action="/cgi-bin/mycgi.exe">
 <p><strong>Your name</strong> <input name="name" size=30></p>
 <p><strong>Age</strong> <select name="age">
 <option value=0>Less than 20
 <option value=1>between 20 and 40
 <option value=2>between 40 and 60
 <option value=3>older than 60
 </select>
 </p>
 <p><strong>Sex </strong>
 <input type=radio name=sex value=0>female   
 <input type=radio name=sex value=1>male      
 <input type=radio name=sex value=2>not enough
 </p>
 <input type="submit" value="Send request">
 <input type="reset"  value="Clear form">      
 </form>
 </body>
 </html>


The CGI Program is in VA COBOL, as this is the only COBOL I use on my Windows/NT system:


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. "MYCGI".
       DATA DIVISION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      * ----------------------------------------------------------
       01  URL-data.
           02 URL-data-char PIC X 
                            OCCURS 1 TO 999 TIMES 
                            DEPENDING ON URL-data-length.
       01  Environment-var.
           02 Environment-var-name PIC X(12) VALUE Z'QUERY_STRING'.
           02                      PIC X     VALUE LOW-VALUE.
       01  URL-data-length         PIC 9(4)  BINARY VALUE 999.
       01  F1                      PIC X(20).
       01  F2                      PIC X(20).
       01  F3                      PIC X(20).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           PERFORM Send-header
           PERFORM Get-url-data
           IF Url-data-length > 0 
              INSPECT URL-data REPLACING ALL '+' BY ' '
              UNSTRING URL-DATA DELIMITED BY '&' 
                       INTO F1
                            F2
                            F3
              END-UNSTRING              
              PERFORM Send-html
           ELSE
              DISPLAY 'Error retrieving URL DATA'
           END-IF
           PERFORM Send-trailer 
           GOBACK
           .

       SEND-HEADER.
           DISPLAY 'Content-type: text/html'
           DISPLAY SPACES
      * Note, if using DOMINO WEB SERVER or APACHE WEB SERVER, 
      *       use DISPLAY X'0A0D' instead!
           DISPLAY '<html>'
           DISPLAY '<header>'
           DISPLAY '<title>'
           DISPLAY 'My first COBOL CGI Program'
           DISPLAY '</title>'
           DISPLAY '</header>'
           DISPLAY '<body>'
           .

       Get-url-data.
           MOVE 999 TO Url-data-length
           CALL 'GetEnvironmentVariableA' 
                 Using BY REFERENCE Environment-var
                                    URL-data
                           BY VALUE LENGTH OF URL-data
                 RETURNING URL-data-length
           IF URL-data-length NOT = 0
           ELSE
              DISPLAY 'ERROR GETTING URL'
              PERFORM Send-trailer
              STOP RUN
           END-IF
           .

       Send-html.
           DISPLAY '<h1>My first attempt at a CGI program</h1>
           DISPLAY '<P>'
           DISPLAY '<br>Parm1=' F1
           DISPLAY '<br>Parm2=' F2
           DISPALY '<br>Parm3=' F3
           DISPLAY '</P>'
           .
 
      Send-trailer. 
           DISPLAY '</body>'
           DISPLAY '</html>'
           .
 
 

     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)



      Civilization is a movement, not a condition; it is a voyage, not a harbor. -- Toynbee
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Website CGI Scripting

- **From:** Matthew Dean <dean@deskware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3990530F.84EDF306@deskware.com>`
- **References:** `<20000807232007.27599.00001107@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```

We produce a server-side scripting language called "CobolScript".  It
has been designed specifically for this task.  Here are some links for
more information:

CGI Samples: http://www.cobolscript.com/samples.htm
WML: http://www.cobolscript.com/wml.htm
HDML: http://www.cobolscript.com/hdml.htm
ODBC: http://www.cobolscript.com/prodwin.htm
unixODBC: http://www.cobolscript.com/produnix.htm
FAQ: http://www.cobolscript.com/csfaq.htm
Functionality FAQ: http://www.cobolscript.com/csfaq3.htm



Also, our site at http://www.cobolscript.com was written in CS.  If you
have any questions send me an email directly and I will help you out.

Best Regards,

Matt Dean
dean@deskware.com

Horsupport wrote:

> Any way to use COBOL to write CGI scripts in developing websites?
```

I have Fujitsu COBOL  Version 3.0 and 4.0.  When installing 4.0 on Windows
2000 Pro I get the message "You have to install Windows NT service Pack 3",
and then the installation exits.

My questions are:
1.  Is there a way to install version 4.0 on Windows 2000?
2. Will version 3.0 work on Windows 2000?

Regards

Lyndon

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>

"gnrs" <gnrs@mybeat.co.uk> wrote in message
news:p5Vj5.9576$9A1.127914@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com...
> I have Fujitsu COBOL  Version 3.0 and 4.0.  When installing 4.0 on
Windows
> 2000 Pro I get the message "You have to install Windows NT service
Pack 3",
> and then the installation exits.
>
> My questions are:
> 1.  Is there a way to install version 4.0 on Windows 2000?

No. From their website

http://www.adtools.com/support/index.htm

Fujitsu COBOL V4 and Windows 2000:

Fujitsu COBOL V4 will not install on the Microsoft Windows 2000
operating system. If you attempt to do this you will receive the
following message:

"NT Service Pack 3 must be installed before Fujitsu can be installed"

(but you already knew that)


> 2. Will version 3.0 work on Windows 2000?

Probably not.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Older Fujitsu Versions on Windows 2000?

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3991B588.54E13485@netins.net>`
- **References:** `<p5Vj5.9576$9A1.127914@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com> <GN_j5.59$W03.27928@nnrp1.sbc.net> <39918c15.52668422@207.126.101.100>`

```
Thane:

Are you sure?  I installed v3 under Win 2000 - I was able to edit,
compile, and link, with only minimal adjustments to the standard
installation.  But when I tried to execute I received an error message
indicating that a dll (F3B1SQL.DLL) could not be found.  I tried copying
the requested dll over to the directory containing the executable, but
without success.  The program I was working with was relatively simple:

     000010 @OPTIONS MAIN
     000020 identification division.
     000021 program-id.    HelloWorld.
     000030
     000040 environment division.
     000050
     000060 data division.
     000070
     000080 procedure division.
     000090 Hello-Start.
     000100    display "this is a test".
     000101    stop run.
     000110

I completed a standard installation - though I had to be sure that the
standard file names did not contain any spaces (as is typical when using
Fujitsu COBOL).  I know that you have indicated in the past that v3
would work with Win 2000, my recent experience would seem to question
this earlier advice.

Can anybody else verify that v3 is working under Win 2000?  Any other
suggestions?

Thanks for any available feedback.

Floyd Johnson



==========================================================================

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Older Fujitsu Versions on Windows 2000?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399201b6.138119@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<p5Vj5.9576$9A1.127914@news2-win.server.ntlworld.com> <GN_j5.59$W03.27928@nnrp1.sbc.net> <39918c15.52668422@207.126.101.100> <3991B588.54E13485@netins.net>`

```
I'm sure.  The problem you experienced is because the install does not
force a reboot, but one is necessary to get the "path" to take.  Once
you reboot it will work fine.  There is a small problem if you install
OVER or after unistalling a later Fujitsu version.  I'm not sure where
it's keeping track, but some PSTAFF settings are kept track of
somewhere, and error listings are not correctly displayed, and
sometimes the EDIT function does not work.

However, a virgin install works just fine.  I have a test bed Win2K
machine now that I try things like this out on, and I tried it on the
production version with Fix Pack 1 installed.

## Thread: memory bounds error and weird compilation messages — 6 msgs

### memory bounds error and weird compilation messages

- **From:** copthoog@gfg-group.com (Chris Opthoog)
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3990999a.67842302@gfgXserver>`

```
Hi

I am trying to write a program that calls a function in a C module.
The call statement in the Cobol is;

CALL "get_pid" USING MY-PID.

where MY-PID is

77 MY-PID		PIC 9(9) COMP-5.


When I compile and run it, I get;

Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 10).

This happens if I try to reference the contents of the variable AT
ALL. Even using printf inside the C module crashes it.

When I compile the program, I get the following messages (not all of
them, because there are heaps!);

cob -x -C nowarning nofmainm.cbl tcpnof.o noferror.o noftimer.o -lxnet
warning: Text relocation remains                referenced

    against symbol                  offset      in file
_
_mFa603X                            0x2d4
/u01/cobol/coblib/libasmcrtn.a(\
routines.o)
_
_mFa603X                            0x2d0
/u01/cobol/coblib/libasmcrtn.a(\
routines.o)
_
_mF8301X                            0x274
/u01/cobol/coblib/libasmcrtn.a(\
routines.o)
_
_mF8301X                            0x270
/u01/cobol/coblib/libasmcrtn.a(\
routines.o)
-

Any ideas or help would be greatly appreciated.

Ta.
```

#### ↳ Re: memory bounds error and weird compilation messages

Can you show us how the C function is expecting to "receive" the passed
parameter?  Any chance that it is expecting it to be "on the stack" (or in
COBOL "BY VALUE")?
```

int get_pid(int *pid)

    *pid=getpid();

}

The idea is that the cobol app will get its process id, using the c
functions.

```
Chris Opthoog wrote:

> Ok, the C function is this;
>
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Why use 'C' when you can use it straight from COBOL...

 working-storage section.
 *> NB: mypid size may change from OS 2 OS..check pid_t size..
 01 mypid pic s9(9) comp-5.
 procedure division.
  call "getpid" returning mypid
  display "MYPID : " mypid

PS: You assume a "process id" is the same size as a integer, this may not be true for
some OS's... try to use "pid_t" instead of int in the 'C' and change the size of mypid
in the COBOL if required.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: memory bounds error and weird compilation messages

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399117B6.7226AF95@dial.pipex.com>`
- **References:** `<3990999a.67842302@gfgXserver> <8mq760$vb5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3990a19e.399945@gfgXserver>`

```
Chris,

This *might* be an alignment problem (where the 'int' you have declared
in COBOL is not on a valid boundary for the indirection (*pid) to work).
In general, if you're passing 01 or 77 level items, this shouldn't be
the case, but there are directives which affect this, so I'll mention it
anyway.

Try doing a byte-for-byte copy:

int get_pid(int *pid)
{
    int p;
    p = getpid();
    memcpy((void *)pid, (void *)&p, sizeof(int));
    return 0;
}

If that works (and therefore alignment is the problem), then the
declaration 'int *pid' is misleading and should be changed (for your
sanity at some point in the future when maintaining the code!).

If your version of MF COBOL have them, check out files called
'ugetput.h' (or 'getput.h') and 'mf_types.h' somewhere under $COBDIR
($COBDIR/src ??). These files have type and macro declarations that
allow you to write this code portably and still get best performance
(eg, on a chip which is tolerant of misalignment, the macro *will* just
do a pointer indirection).

.. of course, you *could* always code directly to getpid():

CALL "getpid" RETURNING MY-PID

Cheers, Kev.

Chris Opthoog wrote:
> 
> Ok, the C function is this;
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cobol 4.1
```

## Thread: Help- Visual Basic — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help- Visual Basic

- **From:** "Raimundo Carvalho" <ray@mail.telepac.pt>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mpkjn$oi6$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
I am calling from Visual Basic a program linked with a dll extension and
with an entry point like says the book "Microfocus Cobol- Colbol users
guide". But i have a return error 458 yhat does know thr entry point .
Can abyone hepls me.
I build de program ddl with netexpress.
Thanks
Raimundo Carvalho


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. PRA00W.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           CALL-CONVENTION 3 IS WINAPI.
       input-output section.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       working-storage section.
           copy "dssysinf.cpy".
           copy "ds-cntrl.mf".
           copy "pRA00w.cpb".
       01 STR PIC X(10).
       01 dsgrun pic x(6) value "DSGRUN".
       01 action-code                  pic 999.
           78 accao-fim                      value 1.
       01 CORRENTE-DATA.
          05 CORRENTE-DATA-AMD             pic X(08) VALUE SPACES.
          05 FILLER                        pic X(13) VALUE SPACES.

       01 end-of-file-flag              pic 9.
           88 end-of-file                      value 1.

       01 end-of-actions-flag           pic 9.
           88 end-of-actions                   value 1.
****************************
       COPY FSTLINK.CBL.
*************************************
       procedure division WINAPI.
*************************************
           ENTRY "cobstring" USING STR.
       R-APRES-1.
           MOVE STR TO PRA00W-STRING.
********************** comeca aqui ****************************
           initialize ds-control-block
           initialize PRA00W-data-block
           move "pra00w" to ds-set-name
           move ds-new-set to ds-control
           move PRA00W-data-block-version-no to
                 ds-data-block-version-no
           move PRA00W-version-no to ds-version-no
           move SPACES TO COM-PGM
           move zero to end-of-actions-flag.
************************
       R-CALL.
           MOVE SPACES TO PRA00W-TESTE
           call DSGRUN using ds-control-block,
                              PRA00W-data-block.
           move ds-continue      to ds-control
********************************
           IF   PRA00W-TESTE    = "sa"
                GO TO R-EXIT.
           GO TO R-CALL.
******************************************************************
       R-EXIT.
           move PRA00W-data-block-version-no to
                 ds-data-block-version-no
           move PRA00W-version-no to ds-version-no
           move "pst00w" to ds-set-name
           move ds-quit-set      to ds-control
           call DSGRUN using ds-control-block,
                              PRA00W-data-block.
                EXIT PROGRAM.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help- Visual Basic

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mrbcg$c6$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8mpkjn$oi6$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
Raimundo,

The DLLs that 32 bit Visual BASIC creates are OLE servers and not
standard Windows DLLs.  You have to access these DLLs via OLE
and Object COBOL i.e. a CALL can no longer be used.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

Raimundo Carvalho <ray@mail.telepac.pt> wrote in message
news:8mpkjn$oi6$1@venus.telepac.pt...
> I am calling from Visual Basic a program linked with a dll extension and
> with an entry point like says the book "Microfocus Cobol- Colbol users
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-edf5489089"></a>
## Thread: binding DBMS-database — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### binding DBMS-database

- **From:** "Turgon" <lupker@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uXdH4vWAAHA.225@net003s>`

```
Hi everybody,

I've been programming in COBOL for some time, but last week I ran into a
problem I cannot seem to solve.

Since last week our team is able to use an Alpha with Open VMS (7.2), DBMS
(7.0) and COBOL (2.6). If this doesn't impress you, let me tell you: it's
quite an improvement since we have been working on one of the most worn-out
VAX-machines ever with Open VMS 6.something. However, my problem....

Because we now have the Alpha available we started compiling our sources on
the Alpha. Besides the usual alignment problems and such, we didn't find
anything strange... Until!! we started using the COBOL to bind our
DBMS-database, freshly transported from the VAX to the Alpha. We already ran
a
DBO/CONVERT and a DBO/CONVERT/CDD. So both CDD and database were converted.

But lo and behold!! our COBOL could not bind the database, although we
didn't
change anything in the code (under Data Division we have a DB
DEFAULT_SUBSCHEMA etc.... line, which always worked on the VAX).

Has anybody else ran into this problem when migrating from VAX to Alpha? And
if so, how can this problem be solved?

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Cheers,

Bart Lupker.
gondolin@freeler.nl
```

---

<a id="t-21f20cfcf6"></a>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Batch on unix / CICS transaction on MVS interface

- **From:** Patrick Van Nieuwenhuyze <vannieu.p@fr.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39924F96.AA71A17C@fr.ibm.com>`

What you need is a way to talk to your MVS mainframe from your Solaris
and have a CICS transaction run on the mainframe and return the data
to you on the Solaris.  I would suggest you look at IBM's MQ series as
a way to do this.

okay, 

then you can use the ONC/RPC featrure on CICS, which makes CICS application programs
appear to Uniy like an RPC server

AFAIK the CICS universal client is NOT available on Solaris.  But if a Solaris process can
access a (let's say) windows process, then you couls also use the CICS universal Client
from Windows to access CICS mainframe

The third possibility is to spin the WEB around CICS - access the CICS data using the CICS
Web support (with or without 3279 bridge)

Just make up your mind

## Thread: Palm and WorkPad with COBOL — 2 msgs

### Palm and WorkPad with COBOL

- **From:** Alain Chappuis <chappuis@cmu.unige.ch>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3990FE54.254D0E81@cmu.unige.ch>`

```
Hello,

I dont know if this question have see the day on this forum!

But it's possible to make a program with any compiler COBOL
for the Palm serie?

Thank in advance!

Alain.
```

#### ↳ Re: Palm and WorkPad with COBOL

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4Vjl5.8837$T73.437589@nntp1.onemain.com>`
- **References:** `<3990FE54.254D0E81@cmu.unige.ch>`

```
I know a little bit about Palm development, and even less about COBOL
compilers, so take this with a 1/2 of salt...

PalmPilot software development kits exist for many operating systems that
allow you to access conduit functions (an ultimately the Palm's data.)
Given that you can call DLL exports from your COBOL environment, it seems
certain that it's possible to write a PalmPilot-aware COBOL program.
However, if it's practical is a question for those suffering the task.

If you're looking to do this, O'Reilly makes an excellent book on the topic:
http://www.oreilly.com/catalog/palmprog/

Other links of interest:
http://astro.uchicago.edu/home/web/valdes/pilot/resources.html

- **From:** "lbr" <lbr@ntlworld.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
What is COMP-X?

I'm converting a program that was written for the UNIX platform to Fujitsu
PC COBOL.
Already tested the code in MicroFocus PC COBOL, and it works.

The Code:
01  ws-group.
  05  ws-field1 pic xx comp-x value 1980.

I get compile errors like this:
-COMP-X CLAUSE IS ALREADY SPECIFIED
-SYNTAX OF USAGE CLAUSE IS INVALID

Any Ideas?

I work from home when I can and that's why I'm trying to use my free copy of
Fujitsu at home.
The live programs gets compiled with MicroFocus. Any other pc compiles out
there that I can use?

Thanks

Lyndon
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-X ?

- **From:** Patrick Van Nieuwenhuyze <vannieu.p@fr.ibm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39918326.82E8F123@fr.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
Microfocus Cobol Documentation :
The PICTURE character-string of a COMPUTATIONAL-X item must consist either of
all "9"s or all "X"s. Either gives an item whose category is numeric.
Compatibility : MF (are specific to MICRO FOCUS COBOL)

lbr wrote:

> What is COMP-X?
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lyndon
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-X ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ms1ac$859$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <39918326.82E8F123@fr.ibm.com>`

```
Just so it is clear what the MF documentation is TRYING to say - is that when
you specify

   PIC XX COMP-X

it means that you have a NUMERIC field of two bytes long.  This could be
handled in compilers without COMP-X by code such as:

  Pic S9(04) Binary

     or (depending upon big-/little-endian) on systems that have COMP-5

  Pic S9(04) Comp-5
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-X ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mv9i7$27t$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <39918326.82E8F123@fr.ibm.com> <8ms1ac$859$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <Otik5.5737$bV6.868159@news2.mia> <B0EAD7FB5B0AC9CE.CEF75EE95BC1D931.5CFBAB8AEC20497D@lp.airnews.net>`

```
PIC XX COMP-X is NOT equivalent to PIC 99 COMP-X (yuck !!!)

PIC XX COMP-X says to allocate two bytes and fill it with as large a
(non-truncated) binary field that will fit in two bytes.

Pic 99 Comp-X says - figure out how many bytes it takes to store the value 99
in binary.  Then allocate that much storage (probably one byte) for the
numeric field and allow ANY non-truncated numeric value in that field.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-X ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6IAk5.5967$3J3.2617567@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <39918326.82E8F123@fr.ibm.com> <8ms1ac$859$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <Otik5.5737$bV6.868159@news2.mia> <B0EAD7FB5B0AC9CE.CEF75EE95BC1D931.5CFBAB8AEC20497D@lp.airnews.net>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:
>
> I'm not familiar with either compiler, but I was wondering could the
> PIC  XX  COMP-X on MF be the same as PIC 99 on Fujitsu or does COMP-X
> imply some kind of data compression and/or format?

It is not necessary to use PIC X with COMP-X, but you can do so.
Below is from a working MF COBOL program (actually, a subroutine
that is used in many of my programs):

       01  KEYBOARD-INPUT-AREA.
           03  KIA-CHAR.
               05  KIA-DIGIT           PIC  9(02)  COMP-X.
           03  KIA-KBD-STATUS          PIC  9(02)  COMP-X VALUE 0.

The equivalent using PIC X would be:

       01  KEYBOARD-INPUT-AREA.
           03  KIA-CHAR.
               05  KIA-DIGIT           PIC  X(01)  COMP-X.
           03  KIA-KBD-STATUS          PIC  X(01)  COMP-X VALUE 0.

I have a visceral dislike for using 'X' for numeric fields. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP-X ?

- **From:** "Greg Condon" <zgreg_zcondon@telus.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oJmk5.141356$8u4.1319497@news1.rdc1.bc.home.com>`
- **References:** `<1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com> <39918326.82E8F123@fr.ibm.com> <8ms1ac$859$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Another thing to watch with MF COMP-5 and COMP-X...

COMP-5 is stored in the processor's native byte ordering (as far as I
understand).
COMP-X is stored in a processor-independent byte ordering.

If you are passing data between processors/different machines (such as on a
client/server application betwen Intel and IBM) don't use COMP-5.  If you
are saving data to a file and possibly using different processors to read
the data don't use COMP-5.

I only use COMP-5 for temporary storage.



Regards,

Greg




"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:8ms1ac$859$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net...
> Just so it is clear what the MF documentation is TRYING to say - is that
when
> you specify
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> > The PICTURE character-string of a COMPUTATIONAL-X item must consist
either
> of
> > all "9"s or all "X"s. Either gives an item whose category is numeric.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > I work from home when I can and that's why I'm trying to use my free
copy
> of
> > > Fujitsu at home.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

## Thread: New to Cobol and Having Problwm w/Pgm — 4 msgs

### New to Cobol and Having Problwm w/Pgm

- **From:** wilsont1967@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mrvl7$s45$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hello,


Maybe some out here can help me.  I'm new to COBOL and not getting much
help from the "experts" here at my company.  I've written a COBOL
program that reads 4 records and writes those record to a single line
for a report.  The two problems I'm having are:

1) It creates two extra blank lines at the after the report headings.

2) It counts the lines on the report properly but appears to overwrite
the last line (UPC 663360211020) with the record count.

Any specific recommendations on how to fix these two issues would be
appreciated.

Thank you.

Regards,

Tom Wilson

Below is a sample of the last few record of the input file:
LIN.807.VC.A8019.UP.663360210986.CM.532.SM.50475.
DTM.043.20000712.
PID.F.08...CANDICE KID.
PID.F.73...LILAC/PURP.
PID.F.74...090M.
G55.UP.663360210986.
LIN.808.VC.A8019.UP.663360210993.CM.532.SM.50495.
DTM.043.20000712.
PID.F.08...CANDICE KID.
PID.F.73...LILAC/PURP.

000800********************* PROGRAM ABSTRACT
***************************00080000
000900*
*00090000
001000*
*00100000
001100*  WHICH HAS BEEN UPLOADED TO MAINFRAME FROM EXCEL.  IF RECORD
*00110000
001200*  PASSES EDIT CHECKS, THREE VSAM FILES ARE UPDATED.  IF RECORD
*00120000
001300*  DOES NOT PASS, THE RECORD ERROR IS WRITTEN TO AN ERROR REPORT
*00130000
001310*
*00131000

*****************************00140000
001500*
*00150000

00172000

#### ↳ Re: New to Cobol and Having Problwm w/Pgm

- **References:** `<8mrvl7$s45$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
One possible problem is the length of your report record.  It should
be 133 characters, not 132.  When using the ADVANCING option, the
compiler will generate instructions to insert the appropriate control
character as the first character of a record.

Regards,

## Thread: short step towards re-engineering — 6 msgs

### short step towards re-engineering

- **From:** "kiran" <slsup@bom6.vsnl.net.in>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mvf85$jhn$1@news.vsnl.net.in>`

```
Hello!
         As we know that there is no fixed file format for the COBOL
Databases. Now, what I am doing is, I am analyzing the source code, scan the
data division for the record structure of the file. I am preparing a *Data
Dictionary * that will contain the basic structure of the records such as
Table name corresponding to the Record Name; Fields and its different
attributes such as type, size etc. Now, a query arrives and demands some
data which is in COBOL format.
        My question is how to *map* this data dictionary with the actual
data files which are in COBOL format. Any suggestions???
```

#### ↳ Re: short step towards re-engineering

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZgCk5.393$FD4.50752@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8mvf85$jhn$1@news.vsnl.net.in>`

```
Let me suggest some materials for you to use in your research.

Courtesy of Flexus, there are some materials on their website for download:

at www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html you can get file COBDATA.ZIP, a text
and graphics tutorial on COBOL datatypes, and COBFD.ZIP, a record analyzer
which works the same as your proposed scanner.

at www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html

you can read about data conversion issues, both COBOL to IEEE datatypes and
EBCDIC - ASCII issues.

As far as constructing a "query tool" which would work by mapping the
data-division record types against full records... I have a 32-bit Windows
DLL and some BASIC-language source code which shows how to do that. For
those you'll have to write me.

(No, I don't have it in COBOL.)

-
Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
(Author of the stuff on the Flexus web site).

putting the length of the variable out and then taking the max of all
of those values.  Then use that for the fixed length for each given
field.  Maybe sql can do that.

Or
If you just ran the sql on the fields you want it would make a report
wide enough for the widest element.  If you do it in a fixed length
font you can determine the length.  Make the database do the work.
Imagine them asking why you need all the cycle time, Hee Hee.

Or you could just make a utility program to determine the length of all
the fields as you read each one and store the maximum and print it out
at the end then hard code the field length.  Tallying would be good for
text and just the highest number could be used to determing the numeric
length.  Boy would that be a pain!

#### ↳ Re: short step towards re-engineering

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399336A9.B7FA1E4E@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<8mvf85$jhn$1@news.vsnl.net.in>`

```
kiran wrote:
> 
> Hello!
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> data files which are in COBOL format. Any suggestions???

The easiest way is to license our DataAccess library.
It will take file and record description (FDD and RDD files)
and it will be able to read Cobol data records in several
formats (MF, FSC, RM, ACU) and present them in a way
understood by C++ programs.

More details on it at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

If you ask me: is it cheaper to write such library myself,
then the answer would be No. We are doing this datafile 
reading stuff for the 3rd year now and we still have bugs 
to fix in the new data formats.

The problem with Cobol data files formats is that 
they are not really documented anywhere and 
there's a lot of them (we are aware of at least 20).
So a lot of investigative work is required when 
trying to read (shall we say, unencrypt -) a Cobol data file.

Look for our new release 3.8.1 (to come out in 2-3 days),
it will add 3 new data file formats. But probably you can
read your format with the existing library.

We have Evaluation Package available.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems
```

<a id="t-57260218cd"></a>
## Thread: decrementing count when searching — 6 msgs

### decrementing count when searching

- **From:** rei@jazzandjava.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mtkgd$24a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
For this example taken from my Cobol book:

MOVE some-value TO count
SET index TO start
SEARCH table VARYING count
   AT END imperative-statements
   WHEN condition imperative-statements
   ...
   WHEN condition imperative-statements
END-SEARCH.

Can you have an index that's greater than 1 and decrement the count?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: decrementing count when searching

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3992B711.C1BADF92@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<8mtkgd$24a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
First of all you must use the index in the VARYING clause of the SEARCH
verb.  Inside the WHEN clause you can do whatever you want to count.

Joe

rei@jazzandjava.com wrote:
> 
> For this example taken from my Cobol book:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: decrementing count when searching

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7C058172608088C2.21E73426B14CFBFA.D3B0CF03BF39B75C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<8mtkgd$24a$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3992B711.C1BADF92@yahoo.com>`

```
On Thu, 10 Aug 2000 10:07:13 -0400, Joseph Kohler
<joe_kohler@yahoo.com> enlightened us:

>First of all you must use the index in the VARYING clause of the SEARCH
>verb.  Inside the WHEN clause you can do whatever you want to count.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Before you buy.

First, what I'm telling you applies to mainframe IBM Cobol.  Don't
know about the PC flavors.

What you want to do is not possible with the SEARCH/VARYING because
you cannot use FROM or BY like with other verbs.  This assumes that
count is defined as an index name of your table.

However, it is very easy to set up your own serach.  You could do
something like:

PERFORM
   VARYING WS-INDEX FROM WS-MAX-SIZE BY -1
   UNTIL WS-INDEX EQUAL 0 OR (whatever your WHEN condition is)
END-PERFORM

Here's a better example using real fields and data:

In WORKING-STORAGE:

01  WS-FIELDS.
      03  WS-INDEX             PIC S9(04)  COMP  VALUE +0.
      03  WS-MAX-SIZE       PIC S9(04)  COMP   VALUE +100.
      03  WS-ITEM-PRICE   PIC S9(5)V99  COMP-3  VALUE +0.

01  WS-TABLE.
      03  WS-PRICE-TABLE  OCCURS 100 TIMES.
            05  WS-PT-ITEM    PIC X(10).
            05  WS-PT-PRICE  PIC S9(5)V99  COMP-3.

In PROCEDURE DIVISION:

PERFORM
    VARYING WS-INDEX FROM WS-MAX-SIZE BY -1
    UNTIL WS-INDEX EQUAL 0 OR
                WS-PT-ITEM (WS-INDEX) EQUAL 'CONDOMS'
END-PERFORM

IF WS-INDEX EQUAL 0
   DISPLAY NOT-FOUND 
ELSE
    MOVE WS-PT-PRICE (WS-INDEX) TO WS-ITEM-PRICE
END-IF.

Hope that helps.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I have six locks on my door all in a row. When I go out, I lock every
other one. I figure no matter how long somebody stands there picking the
locks, they are always locking three.






Boycott Mitsubishi Industries
For more info, please see:  
http://www.ran.org/ran/ran_campaigns/mitsubishi/background.html

Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: decrementing count when searching

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_gCk5.394$FD4.50531@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8mtkgd$24a$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3992B711.C1BADF92@yahoo.com> <7C058172608088C2.21E73426B14CFBFA.D3B0CF03BF39B75C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Not true that the VARYING object must be the table index!  That's what I
thought, too, but instead of stating it as fact, I dug out my manual.

In

SEARCH identifier-1 [VARYING   {identifier-2|index-name-1}]
    [AT END imperative-statement-1]
    {WHEN  condition-1 {impoerative statement-2|NEXT SENTENCE}} ...
[END-SEARCH]

" identifier-2, when used, must be described as USAGE IS INDEX or as a
numeric elementary item without any positions to the right of the assumed
decimal point...

" .. If the VARYING index-name-1 phrase is specified, and if index-name-1
appears in the INDEXED BY phrase of identifier-1, that index-name is used
for this search. If this is not the case, or if the VARYING identifier-2
phrase is is specified, the first (or only) index-name given in the INDEXED
BY phrase of identifier-1 is used for the search. In addition, the following
operations will occur:
...
"b. If the VARYING identifier-2 phrase is specified, and identifier-2 is an
index data item, then the data item referenced by identifier-2 is
incremented by the same amount as, and at the same time as, the index
associated with identifier-1 is incremented. If identfier-2 is not an index
data item, the data item referenced by identifier-2 is incremented by the
value (1) at the same time as the index referenced by the index-name
associated with identifier-1 is incremented."

I have never tried this, but it sure reads as though Identifier-2 can be a
non-index dataname counter, effectively creating a numeric subscript
corresponding to the index of an indexed table .

Source: Microfocus COBOL Language Reference, Issue 14, April 1994.
In above, no items are flagged as MF-extensions, and only END-SEARCH is
hilited as "ANSI 85"

MCM




SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote in message
news:7C058172608088C2.21E73426B14CFBFA.D3B0CF03BF39B75C@lp.airnews.net...
> On Thu, 10 Aug 2000 10:07:13 -0400, Joseph Kohler
> <joe_kohler@yahoo.com> enlightened us:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: decrementing count when searching

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3992F938.D5797BBB@yahoo.com>`

```
Michael,
  You have pointed out that another identifier can be used, but as I
stated it must be AN index item.  You are correct, as stated in the
manual, that it doesn't necessarily have to be THE table's index item. 
My point was that the original poster wanted to use a numeric counter as
an index which was not correct.  I merely pointed him in the right
direction.

Joe

…[47 more quoted lines elided]…

### Sorting without an output file

- **From:** "Camenwent" <camslixz@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sp70vn36n4t111@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I've been searching the net for examples of just how to do this. All the
examples that I've found require an Input File...a Sort File....and an
Output File ie(sorted file).

I don't want to create a sorted file...I simply want to use the sort (work)
file to create a report.


For those brainiacs that have nothing better to do than kick everyone else
down....yes I have done my homework....I'm using "COBOL From Micro to

#### ↳ Re: Sorting without an output file

Depending on the capabilities of your compiler
SORT SORT-FILE
  ON ASCENDING ....
   INPUT PROCEDURE IS paragraph-name 
   OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS paragraph-name

-output procedure is where you employ the
RETURN verb to get your records back from
sort to use in your report...

#### ↳ Re: Sorting without an output file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8n02g4$1fo$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<sp70vn36n4t111@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Well, there is a relatively easy way to do this.  In your SORT statement have
a "using" statement pointing to the input file; do the sort (as usual) on the
SD description - BUT then also use an OUTPUT procedure instead of a GIVING
file.

My personal suggestion would be to use Report Writer in your OUTPUT
PROCEDURE - but if you want to use the "manual" method, you can do that too.

Now, you haven't told us which compiler and operating system you are using.
If this is *not* a class assignment *and* you are running under OS/390 (IBM
mainframe), then you could also look into "ICETOOL" (a part of DFSort).  This
has some "simple" reporting features that might give you all you want.
```
