```
David,

you are very lucky I found an old email from beginning of 1999.
I'm not sure Bill Tulllis still live there and can be reach under the
given phone/email-address. However I hope it helps in your research.
I send this mail to wmklein@ix.netcom.com because he could update his
Cobol FAQ

Good luck

Roland

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
--------------------
Hi Bill,

it's time to make public my research for COBOL650.

I start this research because I use this Compiler on  my DOS
machine very often. Unfortunally (!!!) I switch to OS/2 and
use the IBM VA Cobol for a given time and would lovely see
a free compiler on this platform too.

This is a short cut of my research:
The original autor was Robert E. Noweck and he developed this
program as long as he was able to do. Version 650 and 701 was
his product. 701 was his latest version and was build during the time
he was very sick.

Robert (Bob) died in 1992 because of AIDS and during his last days
he was working to keep his Cobol-Compiler for free. He choose to
put the source on his most common mailbox called BBS AV-Sync.

Unfortunally the sysop Bill Tullis close his BBS later and the source
code of COBOL650 or COBOL701 is now longer available.

For further questions you can reach Bill Tullis at Btullis497@aol.com
or 001-404-321-1600

RIP Bob E. Noweck

Roland
----------------------------------------------------------------------

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compiling COBOL650

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** epc2@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-20T02:56:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nnhd6$98j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8nb22n$fg$2@nslave2.tin.it> <399b07fe.3417062@news.asacomp.com> <8ng2pp$sjc$1@nslave1.tin.it> <8ngckd$7e5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <8nh5n7$mom$1@nslave1.tin.it> <8nhk9a$23l5$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de> <399E63F4.8AA64BE6@arvotek.net> <8nmt2m$15gb$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de>`

```
In article <8nmt2m$15gb$1@wrath.news.nacamar.de>,
  "Roland Schiradin" <schiradi@tap.de> wrote:
> David,
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> ----------------------------------------------------------------------
Hi folks. I've done a bit of snooping inside the 650 compiler package
and I really have doubts about where it comes from. If you look at the
first program that runs, say using DOS debug, you'll find that it's
written in a different language from the rest of the package, probably
Turbo C. The sole function of this program is to do an EXEC call which
loads the first overlay into memory and passes it command line
parameters which come from the "Author"s screens. In fact, it is
possible to rename the first OVL which runs to a .COM file and invoke it
just as you would early Microsoft/IBM/MicroFocus products:

cobol source,object,list,map (I think).

Indeed the overlays and libraries are remarkably similar to such early
products, except that the two library files usually found have been
concatenated into one library. In addition, cobol 650 has the same bugs
reported in IBM cobol version 1.00:

- All of this nonsense about needing to include a program called DPATH
or using DOS subst commands arises because the program uses MS-DOS 1.0
type file control block (FCB) system calls. Replacing the appropriate
drive specifier byte in the FCB which is 1 (drive A) with 0, current
drive, permanently fixes the spurious requests for files in drive A.

In addition, library files expected by the linker can be renamed.
Usually a linker from DOS 2.0 and up is smart enough to look for or ask
for an alternate library file.

Lastly, there is a nasty but well documented bug involving printing out
a COMP number 32768 which arises because the binary to decimal prinout
routine makes an unwarranted assumption about what sign extending does
on the 8088. (It does *not* clear the high part of a register if the
number involved is *negative*). Anyway, this very bug is present in IBM
PC Cobol 1.00, and at the same location in its LIB file!

Going one step beyond and comparing files from Cobol 650 with files from
a copy of IBM PC Cobol 1.00, there are files in which the only
differences between the two sets are text strings such as error
messsages and such.

One substantial way that this program does differ from the old PC
product is that it does implement SORT and MERGE, so there may well be
original work here, but if you look at the whole package, the simplest
explanation is that someone wrote a loader in another language and used
a hex program editor to change key text strings. If this is original
work, it is hard to see how it was compiled byte for byte so close to a
commercial product!

If you are determined to use this, Microsoft's 16 bit linker is freely
available on the net. If you really are desparate enough to use this
program, please e-mail me and I'll send you my patch files.

Good luck




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8nedoh$1bu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8nb32r$sh5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
What Paddy fails to note here, is that while the dual reviews in
Inforworld of PERCOBOL and NetExpress are well written and reasonable,
they are written behind a COBOL bashing backdrop.  Here's the root
article:

http://www.infoworld.com/articles/mt/xml/00/08/14/000814mtcobol.xml

The artwork in the magazine shows a COBOL worm becoming a JAVA
butterfly.

Please read the above bashing and send your letters to the editor --
showing just how wrong they are.


In article <8nb32r$sh5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>,
  "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com> wrote:
> Folks,
>
> I thought you might find the following independent review of
interest...
>
>
http://www.infoworld.com/articles/mt/xml/00/08/14/000814mtnetexpress.xml
>
> All the best.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting review...

- **From:** g_t_g@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nhclm$gec$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8nb32r$sh5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <8nedoh$1bu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Yes, this article basically a COBOL bash, with
the reviews sort of thrown in to show there is
some hope of "fixing" COBOL.

Statements like "the language isn't scalable
enough for true online business processing" can't
be more wrong.

What irks me about these "COBOL on the internet"
and general COBOL articles is they keep confusing
the language with the interface. COBOL as a CGI
program on the internet is VERY powerful. There
are several ways now to create and connect
browser based HTML and JAVA programs to COBOL
CGI. Most COBOL compilers also now support
connectivity to RDBM's. Scalability and multi-
tier are now COBOL's middle name.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-3ce9dc31c7"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus COBOL vs. Mainframe COBOL II — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### MicroFocus COBOL vs. Mainframe COBOL II

- **From:** "Lincoln Duncan" <NotOnSite*NOSPAM*@Yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spjbspsvkn9106@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Before I post an example...

Does anyone have an example of  identical COBOL code yielding different
results when compiled and run on a mainframe computer (COBOL II) as opposed
to running on a PC in MicroFocus COBOL ?

LD
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL vs. Mainframe COBOL II

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ncffo$2c8$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<spjbspsvkn9106@corp.supernews.com>`

```
If you are looking for such things, you will need to include a COMPLETE list
of what directives you are using (or "promise" that you are using all the
directives suggested in the MERANT Mainframe Programmer's Guide).  It is
*easy* to show different results if you don't use the directives that MERANT
recommends.  Also, if you are looking for such examples, it will (probably)
depend upon whether or not your are emulating SUPPORTED behavior on the IBM
Mainframe.  There are lots of things that the VS COBOL II compiler
does/allows that are explicitly "unsupported" by IBM.

Having said all of this, if your desire is for the most compatible compiler
(not necessarily run-time results), there is no question that IBM's own
VisualAge COBOL product is "closer" to the mainframe compiler - because it is
the SAME "front-end" as the current/strategic IBM product "IBM COBOL for
OS/390 & VM" (which is a LOT more recent than VS COBOL II).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL vs. Mainframe COBOL II

- **From:** "Lincoln Duncan" <NotOnSite*NOSPAM*@Yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spjnd6ngr5j67@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<spjbspsvkn9106@corp.supernews.com> <8ncffo$2c8$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Well, there is much truth to what you say.  No, I am not looking for a
compiler. Yes, I realize that there are many versions/levels of compilers
running in MF shops in the industry.  According to what I've read (and once
tested), the issue had less to do with compiler options (yes, I recognize
that sounds "inexperienced") and more to do with how compilers manage
storage on dissimilar platforms.

So, what exactly was my purpose for the post?  It appears that this forum is
not overly active. So, considering the number of posts that were generated
from my earlier post (yesterday), "Interesting Date Routine", I thought I
would try to generate some more chatter... get a few more ideas (juices)
flowing.

As for the example I made reference to in my original post, I have it in a
paper file somewhere (pre-dates the paper-less office) and I plan to dig it
up soon.  My recollection is that it was from an article in a trade
publication.... and I'll be darned if I can recall which one.... but I will.

Thanks for you thoughtful response....
LD

```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL vs. Mainframe COBOL II [Part 1]

- **From:** "Lincoln Duncan" <NotOnSite*NOSPAM*@Yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spm7k6ssr5j114@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<spjbspsvkn9106@corp.supernews.com>`

```
The following text was extracted from a publication, "Enterprise Systems
Journal", dated September, 1993, and titled, "COBOL COMPUTE Can Bite You" by
Jim Janossy of DePaul University.  I have reproduced only the first column
of the first page to see if it is of interest to anyone.  I will take the
time to post more if interest exists.

*****     Beginning of Text     ******

A chemistry professor, without a word, entered a lecture hall one Monday
morning, filled a large balloon with hydrogen and let it hover above the
podium on a string. He then held a lighted Bunsen burner under it. The
resulting explosion reverberated throughout the hall and shook loose some
ceiling tiles (and sleeping hulks). It was his way of demonstrating that
some chemical reactions proceed rapidly. Obviously, it caught the audience's
attention, starting the lecture with a bang.

AMOCO Corp. is in the chemicals business and runs one of the larger IS
organizations in the United States. A course was built for its training
division to acclimate programmers to the world of VS COBOL 11 and capture
audience interest with a bang. The [partial] program in Example #1, an
innocent looking routine that divides 248 by 1000 and computes a percentage
accomplished the course's goals.

Example #1:

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  WS-RECORDS-WITH-ERROR           PIC 9(4)   VALUE 248.
01  WS-TOTAL-RECORDS                       PIC 9(4)   VALUE 1000.
01  WS-HIGH-PRECISION-RESULT        PIC 9(3)V9999.
01  WS-ERROR-PERCENTAGE                PIC  9(3)V9.
01  OUTPUT-LINE.
    05  OL-RECORDS-WITH-ERROR        PIC 9999.
    05  FILLER                                              PIC X(7) VALUE
SPACES.
    05  OL-TOTAL-RECORDS                    PIC 9999.
    05  FILLER                                              PIC X(7) VALUE
SPACES.
    05  OL-ERROR-PERCENTAGE             PIC ZZ9.99.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAINLINE.
         DISPLAY 'PROGRAM G2MATH STARTING'
         DISPLAY 'ERR TOTAL  Err/Total %'
         COMPUTE WS-HIGH-PRECISION-RESULT =
                  (WS-RECORDS-WITH-ERROR / WS-TOTAL-RECORDS) * 100
         COMPUTE ERROR-PERCENTAGE  ROUNDED =
                   WS-HIGH-PRECISION-RESULT
         MOVE WS-RECORDS-WITH-ERROR TO OL-RECORDS-WITH-ERROR
         MOVE WS-TOTAL-RECORDS  TO OL-TOTAL-RECORDS
         MOVE WS-ERROR-PERCENTAGE TO OL-ERROR-PERCENTAGE
         DISPLAY OUTPUT-LINE.
         STOP RUN.

The bang occurs when you run this program under VS COBOL or VS COBOL II as
the following results show. Instead of 24.8 being the answer, it is 20.0.
With adequate unit testing, you could detect this problem and eventually
determine why it occurs: IBM's COBOL compilers are stingy with the size of
intermediate fields.

The result, when compiled and run under VS COBOL II is not accurate:
        PROGRAM G2MATH STARTING
        ERR      TOTAL      Err/Total %
        0248      1000          20.00

The result, when compiled and run under Micro Focus COBOL, gives the correct
result:
        PROGRAM G2MATH STARTING
        ERR      TOTAL      Err/Total %
        0248      1000          24.80

The result, when compiled and run under Ryan McFarland RM/COBOL-85, gives
the correct result:
        PROGRAM G2MATH STARTING
        ERR      TOTAL      Err/Total %
        0248      1000          24.80


*****     End of Text     ******



Lincoln Duncan <NotOnSite*NOSPAM*@Yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:spjbspsvkn9106@corp.supernews.com...
> Before I post an example...
>
> Does anyone have an example of  identical COBOL code yielding different
> results when compiled and run on a mainframe computer (COBOL II) as
opposed
> to running on a PC in MicroFocus COBOL ?
>
> LD
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL vs. Mainframe COBOL II [Part 1]

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nfbh3$d6q$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<spjbspsvkn9106@corp.supernews.com> <spm7k6ssr5j114@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Gee,
  That is EXACTLY why Micro Focus (MERANT) offers the "ARITHMETIC" directive.
The "default" is to provide the more "accurate" answer - however, with
    ARITHMETIC(IBM)
specified, you will get the same results as on the mainframe.

This "issue" is fully discussed in the MERANT "Mainframe Programmer's Guide"

(I know that, because I wrote it - when I still worked for Micro Focus).

The CLASSIC (easier to see) example is:

 05 Num7  Pic 9  Value 7.
 05 Num4  Pic 9  Value 4.
 05 NumResult  Pic 9 Value Zero.
         ...
  Compute NumResult = (Num7 / Num4) * Num4
  Evaluate NumResult
     When Num7
          Display "What you expect and MERANT gives by default"
      When Num4
          Display "You have GOT to be kidding, IBM"
       When Other
          Display "some other vendor's COMPUTE value"
   End-Evaluate

It is NOT true that IBM is "stingy" with intermediate results.  There are
ALSO examples where they give a MORE precise value that MERANT.  However,
their rules are a little "strange" in some cases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL vs. Mainframe COBOL II [Part 1]

- **From:** "Lincoln Duncan" <NotOnSite*NOSPAM*@Yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spndo2lkr5j117@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<spjbspsvkn9106@corp.supernews.com> <spm7k6ssr5j114@corp.supernews.com> <8nfbh3$d6q$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
So, I'll take that as a NO....   as in, there is *NO* interest in any
further text from the article.

LD

PS.  How can I get a copy of the MERANT "Mainframe Programmer's Guide"?

Hum, er, ....
  You can get the manual by checking your current licensed program "manual
set"???

If you don't have a set of MERANT manuals, you can try contacting MERANT to
see if you can purchase this manual "separately".  (It is PROBABLY a part of
the Mainframe Express product these days.)  I know that they have moved more
and more to "online documentation" - so I don't know how easy it will be to
get this specific manual.

FYI,
  There is an entire chapter on things like the "compute example" where you
can get different compile or run-time results - if you don't use the correct
directives and/or run-time switches.

### TinyCOBOL release (0.1.5.3)

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39995F54.271E@arvotek.net>`

```
A new version (0.1.5.3) of the Tiny COBOL compiler is available for
download at the TinyCOBOL home page (1) and at Metalab (2). 

In this release, along with some bug fixes, the INITIALIZE, CURRENCY,
PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE/AFTER option statements have been added to the
TC COBOL grammar.

Please foward any comments and/or suggestions to the TC mailing list.


David

1) TinyCOBOL home page
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/ 
2) Metalab
   http://metalab.unc.edu/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/
```

<a id="t-d96a4cecab"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Data Readers ver 3-8-1: Announce — 1 msgs

### Cobol Data Readers ver 3-8-1: Announce

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadim-cbl@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39996F01.FED21CF2@siber.com>`

```
Siber Systems Inc is pleased to announce 
release 3-8-1 of our Cobol Data Reader technology. 
Using this technology, you or your C++/C application 
can read Cobol data files directly, without writing 
a Cobol program to do it or using expensive ODBC drivers.

For more info look at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

In this release:
* Added readers for Acu Cobol Vision 3 and Vision 4 file systems.
* Added reader for compressed Micro Focus Cobol data files.
  Fixed a number of bugs in Micro Focus data file reader.
* Improve Crystal Reports DLL conflict resolution,
  that prevented Data2Cr from working with certain CR versions.
* Considerably improved intellect of Data-Guess program.


Overview of Data Reader technology and its tools:

* DataAccess library is at the core.

  It reads index and relative Cobol data files from 
  these vendors: Micro Focus, Fujitsu, Acu and Ryan-McFarland.
  We can implement data reader for any Cobol data file format.
  Also DataAccess library reads any sequential Cobol data file.

  Then DataAccess converts data to standard formats used in
  C/C++/Java applications. It also provides extensive browsing 
  capabilities both at the record fields and at the file 
  records level.

  DataAcess library takes file description from File Descriptor 
  (FDD) and Record Descriptor (RDD) text files. These files 
  can be generated by Cbl2Fdd program, Data-Guess service or 
  you can build them manually.


* Cbl2Fdd utility generates FDD and RDD files for all data files
  found in a given Cobol program.


* Data-Guess service allows you to build FDD and RDD files 
  for a data file even if you do NOT have the source Cobol 
  program that can read or write this data file.

  Just imagine: you have an important 3rd party business application 
  written in Cobol and you need to export some data generated by 
  this application into a new application that you are writing.
  Or you simply need to rewrite this application in a modern
  language. Or it is your own application but you lost its source.

  How can you extract the data from cryptic Cobol data files?
  Until recently the answer was simply: you cannot.

  But now you can use Data-Guess service by Siber Systems.
  We would build FDD and RDD files for your Cobol data file
  even if you do not have the Cobol program that wrote these files.
  And we will find a best way to integrate the legacy Cobol data
  with you new applications.


* Data Converters: 
  Data2Flat converts Cobol data files to flat CSV text format.
  Data2Dbf converts Cobol data files to dBase-IV DBF format.


* Crystal Reports Data Reader:
  This is a small DLL that allows you to use Cobol data files
  directly in Crystal Reports (the most popular report generator).


Best Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

---

<a id="t-a86ab5239e"></a>
## Thread: FYI (of possible interest to those following COBOL Standards) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### FYI (of possible interest to those following COBOL Standards)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-15T01:11:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8namum$oda$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I thought that some of you who "follow" COBOL Standards might be interested
in the "Convenor's Report" for the upcoming SC22 meeting.  If you have any
"concerns" that you think should be dealt with related to this report, the
ONLY way to get them conveyed is via your "National Standards Body".

To find out "who is" and "how to contact" your individual "National Standards
Body", see

  http://www.iso.ch/addresse/membodies.html


Full text follows:

"Business Plan and Convener's Report
JTC1/SC22/WG4 - Programming Language COBOL

August 1, 2000

Period Covered:
September 1999 - August 2000

Submitted by:
Ann Bennett
Convenor, ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 22/WG 4
 <snip>

1.    Management Summary
1.1    JTC1/SC22/WG4 Statement of Scope
Development and maintenance of ISO/IEC standards related to programming
language COBOL.

1.2    Project Report
1.2.1    Completed Projects
None in this period.

1.2.2    Projects Underway
22.01.07 Revision of ISO 1989:1985 --
The revision provides major new features, including: cultural adaptability,
large character set support, object orientation, condition handling, new
data-types (bit, floating point, native binary), portable arithmetic,
conditional compilation, user-defined functions, file sharing/record locking,
and improved interoperability with other programming languages.

Combined CD registration and CD ballots N2260 closed in January 1997. The
summary of voting is contained in N2382. WG4 responded to comments in N2616.
Development of changes in response to specific technical comments was
completed in 1998. Three national bodies requested that the document be
improved in quality. In response, WG4 held an internal quality review in
1999, which resulted in substantial rework and the need for further internal
review. That review completed in May 2000 and associated rework is is
progress. WG4 intends to forward a document for Final CD ballot in February
2001.

1.2.3     Cancelled Projects
None

1.3    Cooperation and Competition
WG4 cooperates closely with NCITS COBOL Technical Committee J4, to whom SC22
has delegated the technical development and maintenance of COBOL.

WG4 has liaisons with the following groups:

    SC22/WG20 - Ann Bennett
    SC22/WG11 - Don Nelson

2.    Period Review
2.1     Market Requirements
COBOL continues to be widely used for development and for enhancement and
re-engineering of existing applications. Many factors drive the market for
COBOL standardization:

Technology advances and the resulting spread of computers to end users makes
it mandatory that computer systems adapt to the languages of users. This
gives rise to a need in COBOL for support of large character sets and
cultural adaptability. The current draft includes substantial support for
large character sets and cultural adaptability.

The trend in the industry is to web-enable COBOL applications, with COBOL
running on a server interacting with a non-COBOL user interface. This gives
rise to the need for enhanced interoperability with other programming
languages and system services. A variety of new datatypes, user-defined
functions, and call enhancements are provided in the current draft to support
interoperability.

Market pressure for new technology led COBOL vendors to cooperate on
object-oriented design through the standardization process. Early
implementations of the object-oriented features in the draft are now
available and users are designing them into new applications.

Deployment of applications across workstations and distributed environments
and the growth of COBOL in Unix environments generated requirements for new
features in the language. These needs were met by implementor extensions to
the language, in different ways by different implementors, leading to a need
for post-implementation standardization. Many of these extensions are
included in the draft.

Growth of COBOL in the Unix market led X/Open (now Open Group) to develop a
Common Application Environment (CAE) providing a portable definition of
features essential in a Unix environment, but lacking in standard COBOL. The
need for portability with non-Unix platforms has resulted the inclusion of
some of these features in the draft.

The impact of year 2000 date handling generated a requirement for portable
year-2000 features; these are included in the draft and have been
implemented. Their utility extends beyond the year-2000 conversion
requirements.

The current draft addresses many of the market requirements for COBOL, but
not all of them. Continued evolution of the international standard for COBOL
is essential to provide the benefits of new technologies and new environments
to COBOL users worldwide.

2.2     Achievements
WG4 completed technical work to address CD comments and comments in a
subsequent internal quality review. Corrections resulting from that review
are in progress.

2.3     Resources
WG4 meets as needed, usually once a year, and works by electronic
correspondence between meetings. Five countries are participating in
meetings: Germany, Japan, The Netherlands, the UK, and the USA.

Detailed technical development is delegated to NCITS J4. J4 has 13 members
participating in detailed technical development - - 6 COBOL vendor
organizations and 7 user organizations.

3.     Focus Next Work Period
WG4 will focus on quality and preparing the draft for Final CD ballot.

3.1     Deliverables
WG4 anticipates delivering a document for FCD ballot in the first quarter of
2001.

3.2     Strategies
Focus on progression of the draft to FCD ballot.

3.2.1     Risks
In order to support extended identifiers, WG4 is dependent on a normative
specification of character attributes, particularly upper and lower case
equivalents. WG4 has been planning to reference WG20's TR 14652. Certain
issues with respect to case folding need to be resolved to avoid divergence
of approaches across programming languages.

3.2.2     Opportunities
None

3.3     Work Program Priorities
WG4's highest priority is preparation of the draft for Final CD ballot. The
next priorities are development of a technical report for a finalizer
feature, development of a technical report for an object-oriented class
library, and processing of defect reports - in that order.

4.     Other Items
This section includes items that are part of the Converner's report, but not
part of the Business Plan.

4.1     Action Requests at the Forthcoming Plenary
WG4 requests that SC22 authorize development of a technical report for a
finalizer method and associated object-oriented language.

A quality review comment noted a deficiency in object-oriented design because
there was no feature for finalizing an object before garbage collection.
Efforts to design a finalizer method failed for lack of design consensus and
concerns that the technology is not yet stable. WG4 therefore proposes that a
finalizer method and any associated O-O language features be developed in a
Technical Report. WG4 requests that SC22 approve a subdivision of the COBOL
work item for development of that TR.

The following draft resolution is proposed:

ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 22
authorizes a subdivision of the COBOL work item for development of a
technical report for a finalizer method and associated object-oriented
language elements, and delegates the technical development to NCITS J4.


The WG4 convener requests that SC22 allocate time for a working session to
identify issues and anomalies in case folding and determine a consistent
approach across program languages, where appropriate.

4.2     Schedule
The schedule for project 22.01.07 is as follows:

    Final CD start  February 2001
    DIS start June 2002
    Forward IS to ITTF December 2002

4.3     WG4 Meetings
4.3.1     Recent Meetings
May 2000, Newbury, UK     (co-located with an NCITS J4 meeting)

4.3.2     Future Meetings
November 2001,     tentatively USA (to be co-located with an NCITS J4
meeting)

"
```

## Thread: COBOL vs Java — 38 this month / 39 total

### COBOL vs Java

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T01:09:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

Our principal competitor uses Java as a development language.  We are using
Merant NetExpress and Flexus Sp2.  Their sales people are saying that Java is a
64bit product while we are 32bit.  I am confused becuase they are running under
Win 9x just as we are and I thought Win9x was a 32bit O/S.  We both use the
same hardware (300 mhz 32MB RAM) vendor and the hardware vendor claims they
have a 64bit processor and bus.  I know less about hardware then I do about
software which says how little I know :).  Could someone shed some light on the
32bit vs 64bit area ?  Also, is Java still an interpretive language ?  I am
hoping that they are so that I can play up the native vs interpretive angle.  I
believe our product is the better one, but the competition plays on hot buttons
like the 32bit vs 64bit and the perception that anything written in Java is
cutting edge.  

Any help would be most appreciated
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dpIm5.437$bG.70435@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
You're pretty much screwed. I'm sorry I can't answer your specific
questions inasmuch as we have no truck with Java. But we have been
subject to the same kinds of competitive misinformations. Here are
some arguments we've promoted (although not specifically Java vs
COBOL) with results varying from poor to pitiful.

1. If that's the best they can say about their product, then they
can't really be serious about problem solving.
2. Assume metric bolts and nuts are superior. A Ferrari is built with
metric bolts and nuts, this proves the superiority of metric bolts and
nuts, doesn't it?  But a Yugo is built with metric bolts and nuts. Let
me prove it. I can write you a Java program by this afternoon. It
won't actually DO anything useful, but it WILL be a Java program.
3. So what if their product is Java or 64-bit? Nobody wants Java or
64-bits; what they want is fast and accurate processing. We are using
a technology of demonstrated, proven stability and dependability
rather than the fad of the week.

But if you want to exaggerate as much as the competition:

I'm sure they have a good product, but what with the litigation
between the inventors of Java and Microsoft, it is conceivable that
Java will not run on Microsoft operating systems in the future. You'll
then have to buy Sun workstations - at $20,000 a copy - to run their
software.

Every use of Java entails a royalty payment to Sun Microsystems and a
small portion of that royalty payment goes to support Democrats.

Oh well.

Best of Luck.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Bob,

I agree with Mike.  I thought Java was both interpreted (JVM) or compiled
to machine code.  This equates to MERANT INT and GNT/OBJ code.  In
fact MERANT has used the concept of a virtual machine for COBOL for
years now.  That is how we port INT code between platforms.

On Windows 95/98/NT Java is going to be 32 bit - I can't see how it
can be anything else (note I am not a Java programmer and I am happy to
be corrected <g>).  Java may be 64 bit on some of the UNIX platforms
for example.  Mind you, MERANT can supply 64 bit COBOL products
here as well.

To be blunt this all seems pointless from the Customer's point of view.  If
I am purchasing a payroll package I couldn't care less if it is 8/16/32/64
or 128 bit!  I would be interested in the features it provides, what
platforms
it runs on, performance, support, future-proofing and so on.

I know this doesn't help you in your sales situation but really people need
to get smarter and rise above the hype.

All the best.

P.S. I am not anti-Java, just in case the above is taken that way <g>.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DfZm5.114231$i5.1657270@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8nh3r5$jdo$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
"Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com> wrote
> I agree with Mike.  I thought Java was both interpreted (JVM) or compiled
> to machine code.  This equates to MERANT INT and GNT/OBJ code.  In
> fact MERANT has used the concept of a virtual machine for COBOL for
> years now.  That is how we port INT code between platforms.

there's a number of different options for compiling Java. briefly (and with
limited knowledge ;-):

* INTERMEDIATE compilation of source->byte-code where the byte-code
represents a virtual (or abstract) machine. this code requires an
interpreter (an instantiation of the virtual machine) to execute it. the
byte-code is very portable -- just requires a resident interpreter, but
generally doesn't run as fast as native code. [MERANT COBOL supports this
model].

* DYNAMIC compilation of byte-code->native-code (more commonly known as a
Just-In-Time [JIT] compiler) -- as the application executes, code ahead of
the execution queue is analyzed and selectively compiled into
machine-instructions. since this compilation is a "feature" of the
interpreter the byte-code remains portable but has the potential to run
faster. there is however, an overhead to executing apps this way, so it
doesn't scale very well. [MERANT COBOL does NOT support this model].

* STATIC compilation of byte-code->native-code (more commonly known as an
Ahead-Of-Time [AOT] compiler) -- code is compiled to machine-instructions
ahead of execution. generally, the fastest and most scaleable way of
deploying an application. obviously, native-code is generally not portable
between different platforms and processors, but for execution most Java and
COBOL applications simply need a recompilation of the application source
code on the new target platform. [MERANT COBOL supports this model].

* ADAPTIVE compilation of byte-code->machine instructions (a la Sun's
HotSpot Java RT/compiler). somewhat similar to JIT, except that the end-goal
of an adaptive compiler is to execute native-code. it does this by
iteratively compiling the executing application. the idea being that over
multiple executions the application will run faster and faster until the
application is n% saturated as native-code. a more aggressive approach than
JIT and difficult to manage in an environment that needs to scale. [MERANT
COBOL does NOT support this model].
```

Although I GENERALLY agree with this and the similar posts, I did want to
raise one "red flag".  I may be totally MISTAKEN on this, but doesn't 32-bit
vs 64-bit determine the size of the "files" that can be handled within an
operating system? At least it seems to me that the "over 2G" file limitation
went away with the 64-bit Unix systems.

Having said that, it seems to me that MOST (not all) "Workstation" systems
with files that large are PROBABLY using a SQL database for file handling.
"Flat files" of this type of size were common on Mainframes, but I don't
think that this has been the major inhibitor to moving Mainframe COBOL
applications to 16 or 32-bit operating systems.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399d32f9.32139379@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8nh3r5$jdo$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Just to correct a few misconceptions.

Java is 64 Bit.

The JVM on any platform can take JAVA Byte code (64 bit) and run it.
It is not interpreted, it's run natively in the JVM.

It really *IS* 64 bit.  This is VERY similar to what used to happen
with Windows 3.1 running WIn32s stuff.  The JVM handles the thunking
(address translation) and there is a performance hit for that as well
as the hit because the JVM is a process running under the OS.


On Thu, 17 Aug 2000 15:58:29 +0100, "Paddy Coleman"
<paddy.coleman@merant.com> wrote:

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)

>Java is 64 Bit.
>
>The JVM on any platform can take JAVA Byte code (64 bit) and run it.
>It is not interpreted, it's run natively in the JVM.

[snip]

But come on, Thane, the JVM is an interpreter: it interprets byte code into the
machine instructions of the hardware platform it runs on. Maybe "emulator" is
more accurate than "interpreter" but it is NOT native (which means using the
hardware's own instruction set).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8njsu1$uej$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<399d32f9.32139379@207.126.101.100> <20000818114248.29069.00000199@ng-cs1.aol.com>`

```
Steve,

Right time for me to jump in and support Thane here.  The JVM does not
"interpret" the code Java Byte Code (not in the way most people would
think).  This is a multi-layer process (and is VERY similar to the way
Micro Focus implemented its own INT code and virtual machine many,
many years ago) and I will attempt to show the layers.

A programmer will write a program and store it in a source file.  This
could be Java source, COBOL, C etc.

The compiler then converts the source (syntax checking etc.) in to
Java Byte Code - yes, you could have a COBOL compiler that
generated Java Byte Code - there's an idea <g>.

The JVM should then be thought of as a "translator".  At runtime it
translates the Java Byte Code in native code (e.g. Intel) and this is
executed with the help of runtime support modules.

I hope this makes sense - I have tried to simplify what is a very
complex topic.

For more information see:

http://java.sun.com/docs/books/vmspec/2nd-edition/html/VMSpecTOC.doc.html

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

is

the
> hardware's own instruction set).
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000818184222.11963.00000229@ng-bh1.aol.com>`

Paddy Coleman writes ...

>Steve,

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>



>The JVM should then be thought of as a "translator".  At runtime it
>translates the Java Byte Code in native code (e.g. Intel) and this is
>executed with the help of runtime support modules.
>
========================================================
Well, there you have it. You just said the JVM "translates the Java Byte Code
in native code". That is, the JVM is running the native instructions (it has
to)
but the Java Byte Code is being interpreted. In other words, the Java Byte
Code is _not_ in the native instructions of the platform. It can't be. I think
this has to mean the Java Byte Code is being interpreted: not in the usual
sense like, say, REXX, but still it is not running native; that is the meaning
of the word "interpret", in the broad sense.
========================================================
>I hope this makes sense - I have tried to simplify what is a very
>complex topic.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@cegi.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-18T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<01bffee0$2a808b60$010aa8c0@voyager>`

I've been following this with great enjoyment. :) 

The Java Virtual Machine is just that, a virtual machine that executes Java
Code. 
The JVM does mate JVM instructions (Java Byte Code) to the underlying
processor;
it does it in the same way that a 390 or AS/400 maps IBM mainframe
instructions to the 
underlying physcial processor. In short, it can be very efficient... 

It is precisely like the way AcuCOBOL runs.PerCOBOL takes it even
further - it translates relatively standard COBOL into Java, then uses 
any standard JDK to compile and run it. 

Very nice indeed. 

-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praul@cegi.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c00a4c$d3dfddb0$010aa8c0@voyager>`
- **References:** `<8njsu1$uej$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <20000818184222.11963.00000229@ng-bh1.aol.com> <01bffee0$2a808b60$010aa8c0@voyager> <8nmtvp$9j8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Yep, that's pretty much what you guys are doing right now right? 
There are a couple advantages to at least having an option to compile
it to a .java file first. 

(1) The poor guy who has to debug the beastie can look at the .java source
and
      see what it thinks it is trying to do. Especially true if you toss in
some nice
      comments, sorta like what the CICS and DB/2 co-processors do.

(2)  You can more easily include inline Java source in your cobol Sometimes
       it's a good think to just call that stinking method directly...

(3)   It winds up in java classes that are at least named something close
to 
        sane, not just PRG00001, PRG0002, etc. This is important from 
        the Java side, as managing Java packages and such is a non-trivial 
        repository task.

I'm sure there are a few other good reasons of course, but in practice it
won't make
that much difference if the compiler is a decent compiler,  and you
automatically get
the benefit of the better JVM's as they come out. 

I understand that IBM is coming our with one *hot* JVM for OS/390 soon,
and I;'m watching that with my eyes out on stalks. The goal is to get near
COBOL
performance levels, which is kinda difficult since COBOL might as well be
an
assmebler language for the 390 instruction set. :) 

-Paul

Paul Raulerson wrote:
> 
> Yep, that's pretty much what you guys are doing right now right?
> There are a couple advantages to at least having an option to compile
> it to a .java file first.

There was an article written that basically killed the theory of
language conversion.  Byte compiles are one thing, language conversion
is entirely another.

For example mapping  PIC9-4 + PIC9-4 where the values are 9999 in both
and it is stored in the same field.  The truncation is a major concern
and does not cleanly map to Java.  There are several other issues but
this one will do for a start.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nmtr3$9j4$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8njsu1$uej$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <20000818184222.11963.00000229@ng-bh1.aol.com>`

```
Steve,

It is one of those "academic" type things which some people get very#
worked up about.  Whether you want to think of the JVM as an
interpreter is not really that important (in my opinion).

However, the purists will always argue that it is not an interpreter in
the purest sense.  When the source code of a language is executed
directly then this is normally called an interpreter (like BASIC from
the early days <g>).  The JVM however (and MERANT's INT code
support) is really a translator of machine independent byte code in
to native machine code.

Splitting hairs really, but as I said, important to some <g>.

Have a great weekend.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

has

> but the Java Byte Code is being interpreted. In other words, the Java Byte
> Code is _not_ in the native instructions of the platform. It can't be. I
think
> this has to mean the Java Byte Code is being interpreted: not in the usual

meaning
> of the word "interpret", in the broad sense.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spu12fga87v65@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8njsu1$uej$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <20000818184222.11963.00000229@ng-bh1.aol.com> <8nmtr3$9j4$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```

Paddy Coleman <paddy.coleman@merant.com> wrote in message
news:8nmtr3$9j4$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk...
> Steve,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Splitting hairs really, but as I said, important to some <g>.

Almost no "interpreters" interpret the raw source text; that's just too
slow.

Most of them at least tokenize the source text into language atoms.
Better ones "compile" the source text to some kind of "virtural" stack
machine
instruction set (this is the JVM model), "interpret" (in the literal
sense) this instruction set, and can reproduce source text from
the instruction set (not the JVM model).   Both these techniques
appear to be interpreting text, but they clearly are not.

Really good interpreter do "on-the-fly" compilation to the underlying
native machine; and perhaps even recompile the same code
at runtime as more details are discovered, such as SELF.
The JVMs are generally approaching this technology.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8njmi2$tld$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8nh3r5$jdo$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <399d32f9.32139379@207.126.101.100>`

```
Thane,

Normally I would not have responded to this but you sent it to
me directly so I feel I should.

I have checked your posting with our Java developers and they
do not agree with you.  Even Sun's own information on their
web site does not state that Java is 64 bit - indeed it does not
say it is anything apart from the fact that it is implementation
independent.

Java does support 64 bit primitive data types but then so does
COBOL - this does not make it a 64 bit language.

Java byte code then runs on top of the JVM.  It is up to the JVM
to implement the Java language features.  The JVM itself may be
64 bit although this will not be true on Windows 3.1, 95/98/NT
or any other 32 bit operating system.  Certain JVMs may be
written to use 64 bit features but these are down to the JVM
implementor and have nothing to do with Java itself.

If you read the specification of the JVM on Sun's web site you
will see in the limitations chapter that a number of the restrictions
are caused by 16 bit "counters".

MERANT has not yet come across a 64 bit JVM, even Sun's JVM
reports that it is 32 bit!  We are not saying that there isn't one, just
that we have not come across one yet - and we cover a lot of
platforms in MERANT <g>.

As I said before, all this 32 versus 64 bit is not important in most
cases.  It may be important when writing low level software that will
interface with hardware etc. but for most business applications 32
bit will be enough.

Anyway, I hope the above is helpful.

P.S. Having read a great deal of Sun's documentation I must say that
I think Java is "groovy baby" - so is COBOL though <g>.

Have a great weekend.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-19T03:34:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399dff8e.34650324@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com> <8nh3r5$jdo$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <399d32f9.32139379@207.126.101.100> <8njmi2$tld$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <399dae15.13789719@207.126.101.100>`

```
One further note, especially for Bob H, who is fighting the
propaganda.

Check this out:

http://java.sun.com/docs/books/vmspec/html/Overview.doc.html

Go down a bit to where it talks about how big a "word" is and what
memory addressing is used.  This is where "bitness" comes in.

The bottom line -

JAVA does use 64 bit arithmatic where it can.  (Where I got the idea
it was 64 bit)

INT's are 32 bit.

LONG's are 64 bit.

Memory addressing and WORD size --- depend on the platform that the
JVM is executing on.  That means that anyone who says that their JAVA
is 64 bit on an Intel 32 bit platform is...... well either (like I
was) misinformed or.... well ... lying.

couple of advantages of java. it does run on multiple platforms well,
has usage display data types, and doesn't require interpereting. the
other advantage is you can read cobol very well, where java throws in
alot of punctuation.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399CA016.865DF7A1@perfectcircle.com>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Dear Bob:
    I can't speak well to the Java angle, but in my experience, Win 9x is 16 bit.
I've never seen the kernel code, so I don't know for sure, but with the same
program compiled with Net Express 3.0 (32 bit) and Workbench 3.2 (16 bit), the 16
bit program run faster, by a lot.  On NT, the reverse is true, with the 32 bit
program running faster.

    I believe win9x Thunks 32 bit code into a 16 bit kernel, and on NT, 16 bit code
is WOW'd onto a 32 bit kernel.  The performance differences are significant on both
platforms, so much so that we still ship both versions.

    Kinda makes me wonder why Merant Sundown'd the 16 bit product line with so many
windows 9x platforms in use.

    Good Luck

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-08-20T03:14:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399f4ba5.41253863@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<399CA016.865DF7A1@perfectcircle.com> <20000819211846.05713.00000708@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
On 20 Aug 2000 01:18:46 GMT, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Good Afternoon,

>Thane,
>  you mentioned that Java supports 64bit math functions
>  does COBOL also do this ?  

Well -- Not exactly.  COBOL doesn't care about the Bits.  However in
JAVA a "Long Integer" is 64 Bits.  What does that mean?  Well it means
it can handle a binary number this big: 
1111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111111

I think this equals:
18,446,744,073,709,551,616

Which is bigger than COBOL can handle (18 digits) -- however the next
standard allows for 30 digits, which is much larger than this number.
Now, obviously floating point numbers can be bigger, but we are
talking about accurate integer values here.  Your compiler
(NetExpress) has the larger numbers already supported, so you can
handle MUCH bigger numbers - Integers - with accuracy.
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

- **References:** `<+76fOXbu8XTxVam5t07beoMWIx03@4ax.com> <20000820195303.08458.00000491@ng-me1.aol.com> <8nq4vp$bln$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On the point of compiled java - Towerj have  a post-compilation solution.
Check out their site for more info.

"TowerJ converts standard Java bytecode into compact self-contained
optimized binary native executables using post-compilation. These "trusted"
executables can be safely deployed and dynamically extended via native
libraries and/or bytecode. TowerJ provides an extra layer of safety and
system manageability for business critical applications by allowing
organizations to specify which portions of a system can be dynamically
modified (opened) and which portions can not (closed)."

http://www.towerj.com/products/towerjsystem.shtml

Mike Sheehan

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-08-21T03:13:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-sM6jCcdvd02d@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<+76fOXbu8XTxVam5t07beoMWIx03@4ax.com> <20000820195303.08458.00000491@ng-me1.aol.com>`

```
On Sun, 20 Aug 2000 23:53:03, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

> Good Afternoon,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>             999,999,999,999,999,999

This is only true for ANSI 85 COBOL. Several COBOL compilers
available allow for larger integers than PIC 9(18).

There is a possible restriction on the use of the larger integer
values with either COBOL or Java if the data store used does
not support the larger values. For instance, IBMs DB2 only allows
for the 18 digit decimal datatype. This size limit is common to
a number of SQL database engines. 

Your examples below are a little "Windows Centric". There
are a lot of other operating systems that support both COBOL
and Java. Both COBOL and Java are real multi-platform 
development tools. A list of ust a few of the operating systems
would be Solaris, AIX, LINUX various UNIX flavours, 
MVS (IBM mainframe OS), and OS/2.

> 
> Statement Number 2:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Intel Chip Set


Properly speaking this should read

	Java Executable
	Java Virtual Machine
	Operating system supported by the JVM
	underlying OS specific hardware 

or perhaps

	Java Executable
	JavaOS
	underlying OS specific hardware 

> 
>     In COBOL the system would look like
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>         Intel Chip Set

And this one should read

	COBOL Executable
	Operating System supported by the COBOL compiler
	underlying OS specific hardware

or sometimes

	COBOL Executable
	COBOL Virtual Machine (runtime)
	Operating System supported by the COBOL VM
	underlying OS specific hardware
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>

- **References:** `<399CA016.865DF7A1@perfectcircle.com> <20000819211846.05713.00000708@ng-fq1.aol.com> <399f4ba5.41253863@207.126.101.100>`

```
thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>Which is bigger than COBOL can handle (18 digits) -- however the next
>standard allows for 30 digits, which is much larger than this number.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>handle MUCH bigger numbers - Integers - with accuracy.

yea, i was under the impression that the compilers generally do handle
30 digits (there was some article awhile back about going to 31 or
32). in any case, i couldn't see a platform which uses 33 bits (or up
to 64). that's about 8 gigs of memory (which to the normal user would
be about $8000(at $1 per meg)). i don't even want to speculate on the
cost of the motherboard (which would hold 32 256meg drams or you could
just buy 8 standard motherboards each holding 4 256meg drams and
client/server software)). the difference between digits and bits is
basically one is used for calculations, the other for pointers.

and even if you don't have 64 native support, you can do something
called thunking (you send two sets of 32 bits) for devices which
support it.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "mike sheehan" <sheehan_mj@iolfree.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<neOm5.1529$sa4.4863@news.iol.ie>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
You'd probably do better not to play down Java rather than to highlight the
benefits of COBOL... after all Merant seem to have put alot of Java/COBOL
interoperability into NetExpress!

Java is both a compiled and interpreted language!
It is compiled into "byte code" by a Java compiler. A Java interpreter is
used to run it on a specific platform e.g. a Wintel PC giving it  "Write
once, run anywhere" portability. You could play on the interpreted is slower
argument...

I've read somewhere that Microfocus provided "cross-platform" COBOL
compilers in similiar fashion!
[sidebar: it is possible to compile Java in the traditional sense - but
portability is lost]

The supposed 64bit Java selling point may sound good, but, I do not think it
will be of any practical benefit as long as 32bit is the norm on the
desktop.

Why not investigate the benefits of using Java and XML with your COBOL,"we
have the technology", thereby diluting your competitors vapour leading
edge - it all boils down to what kind of applications do your clients want
and can your approach fulfill that?

Mike Sheehan
Bob7536 wrote in message <20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>...
>Good Afternoon,
>
>Our principal competitor uses Java as a development language.  We are using
>Merant NetExpress and Flexus Sp2.  Their sales people are saying that Java
is a
>64bit product while we are 32bit.  I am confused becuase they are running
under
>Win 9x just as we are and I thought Win9x was a 32bit O/S.  We both use the
>same hardware (300 mhz 32MB RAM) vendor and the hardware vendor claims they
>have a 64bit processor and bus.  I know less about hardware then I do about
>software which says how little I know :).  Could someone shed some light on
the
>32bit vs 64bit area ?  Also, is Java still an interpretive language ?  I am
>hoping that they are so that I can play up the native vs interpretive
angle.  I
>believe our product is the better one, but the competition plays on hot
buttons
>like the 32bit vs 64bit and the perception that anything written in Java is
>cutting edge.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ngj9l$h2f$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>,
  bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:
> Good Afternoon,
>
> Our principal competitor uses Java as a development language.  We are
using
> Merant NetExpress and Flexus Sp2.  Their sales people are saying that
Java is a
> 64bit product while we are 32bit

Hi:

It is a sad thing if sales people are driving technical people.  Tell
them to mind
their own business and sell what you know to be best.  It is even more
sad
that people evaluate software based on whether it is 16 or 32 bit.

Software should be evaluated on things like maintainability,
dependability,
portability, ease-of-use, supportability, efficiency,  functionality,
etc., rather than whether it is written in the flavor of the month.

I stick with the same 16-bit compiler I have been using for years and I
doubt
VERY MUCH if anyone could see the difference between run times with
16 or 32.  It is extra sad that buzzword things like Java make a
difference
in sales.  It just goes to show that the sales people don't know
anything
and the people being sold don't  know anything.  Ask the sales people to
determine the cost of changing your compiler thingies and ask them how
much it will cost to support your existing customers who use the old
stuff.
Or, do you update the old customers to the new stuff and who pays for
that?  In addition, making any kind of conversion is going to lead to
beaucoup headaches and aggro. Get the sales people to PROPERLY
evaluate the cost to the company before they start shoving Java down
your throats.

It is interesting how things like 32-bit influence people who don't know
anything.  Computer 'dealers' who want to sell my software occasionally
ask if it is 16- or 32-bit and there can be a noticeable silence at the
other
end when I say 16. I doubt if they really know the difference anyway.
Needless
to say, they don't get to be dealers.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T03:57:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yoJm5.112225$i5.1628481@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
"Bob7536" <bob7536@aol.com> wrote:
> Our principal competitor uses Java as a development language.  We are
using
> Merant NetExpress and Flexus Sp2.  Their sales people are saying that Java
is a
> 64bit product while we are 32bit.  I am confused becuase they are running
under
> Win 9x just as we are and I thought Win9x was a 32bit O/S.  We both use
the
> same hardware (300 mhz 32MB RAM) vendor and the hardware vendor claims
they
> have a 64bit processor and bus.  I know less about hardware then I do
about
> software which says how little I know :).  Could someone shed some light
on the
> 32bit vs 64bit area ?  Also, is Java still an interpretive language ?  I
am
> hoping that they are so that I can play up the native vs interpretive
angle.  I
> believe our product is the better one, but the competition plays on hot
buttons
> like the 32bit vs 64bit and the perception that anything written in Java
is
> cutting edge.

the actual executable app created under Win9x can NOT be a 64-bit app (how
hardware transports data around is irrelevant). Java may (or may not -- i'm
not sure) support 64-bit "features" such as a pointer datatype that will
allow you to address a piece of RAM beyond >4 GIG, but i very much doubt
that Win9x can support any of these so i'm guessing that the salesperson is
selling the fact that Java may (or again, may not) be "64-bit ready" so if
64-bit becomes important later, then as an application developer you are
left with the perception that you have a "head start".

MERANT already has 64-bit compilers (Server Express) available for UNIX
today (because all leading UNIX's are 64-bit operating systems sat on top of
64-bit processors today) -- when 64-bit Windows is a reality (atop of
Intel's 64-bit Itanium processor), i'm sure you will find a 64-bit COBOL
compiler there too.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs Java

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39A1B07B.2A3E155C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000816210926.18268.00000215@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```


Bob7536 wrote:
> 
> Good Afternoon,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Bob,

Hey I like your honesty - "I know less about hardware then I do about
software which says how little I know :)". You and me both <G>

You have had all sorts of techie answers which no doubt are useful. But
if you are trying to push your product then your salesman(en) should be
promoting what appeals to the consumer. If I'm a retailer selling booze,
I could care less about 1, 16, 32 or 64 bit. I want a product that I
know works.

If your competitor is playing dirty pool, bamboozling the customer with
techie jargon - hit him where it hurts at the business level. Take his 
product, what does yours do better ( and I don't know which aspect of
retail you are talking about, some sort of mix I'm sure) :-

- Your POS screens are easier to use - you have layaways they don't; you
can handle Visa, Master Card, Amex - they can't etc. In fact, your whole
system is considerably easier to use than theirs. Easy cash register
reconciliations; slips produced to help with banking etc.

- Your system ensures data integrity - you can recover easier from file
crashes - and we know it is going to happen with POS systems.

- You can rapidly produce selective re-order reports, you do sales
forecasting, Just-in-Time inventory etc. I know from here in Alberta
many small booze retailers go grab some more cases from the local
wholesaler just as the shelves empty (cash flow !). Do you have on-line
re-ordering.

- If fashion - you can give a quick breakdown of sales by colour and
size.

I could go on an on - but you get the idea. Have a brainstorming session
with a white board, (systems, programming and salesmen) and write down
those things which make your system really zing. Of course, second
issue, write down the weak points, (or the things you haven't yet
covered), - and have the answers ready to refute these before the
potential customer is prompted, (by your competitor), to ask the awkward
questions.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-8d0d0f33a3"></a>
## Thread: "Whither" IBM's COBOLSet product(s) — 4 msgs

### "Whither" IBM's COBOLSet product(s)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nf19h$bkb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I am trying to track down exactly what this means, but I thought I would pass
along the following "Press Release" made today.  (NOTE WELL: It does seem to
be talking ONLY about AIX and *not* USS - but one has to "wonder" about the
future for COBOL under USS as well)

"MERANT Works With IBM to Provide Customers With High-Performance Development
Platform; MERANT Products Now Available for IBM's AIX 5L On IBM Power and
Intel IA-64 Architectures

August 16, 2000 08:26 AM Eastern Time

ROCKVILLE, Md.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Aug. 16, 2000--MERANT , a leading global
e-business software solutions company, today announced a technology
partnership with IBM, whereby MERANT's Server Express, a product in MERANT's
Egility solutions suite, will be ported to IBM's AIX 5L UNIX operating system
to support both the IBM Power and the Intel IA-64 bit architectures. Under
this agreement, MERANT's Egility solutions will enable customers to utilize
this powerful new development platform for enterprise and e-business
applications.

"MERANT's Server Express is an important development tool for many new and
existing applications," said Tilak Agerwala, vice president of UNIX marketing
and product management for IBM. "Our customers will be delighted with the
increased flexibility as they will have to place their applications on new,
cost-effective server platforms when Server Express becomes available for AIX
5L on both the IBM Power and the Intel IA-64 platforms."

"This partnership, which includes funding from IBM, demonstrates MERANT's
leadership and commitment to supporting key customer platforms in this
rapidly emerging e-business environment," said Tony Hill, senior vice
president and general manager of MERANT. "IBM's AIX 5L Operating System is a
cutting edge environment, and by leveraging our solutions for this platform,
customers will now have a clear business advantage over the competition in
their market spaces."

MERANT's Traditional Development, Legacy Extension and Legacy Integration
solutions are all supported through this agreement. These solutions enable
enterprises to extend their legacy assets to the Web, integrate their newly
developed Web presence with their existing systems, and provide a highly
productive and robust enterprise application development environment for
mission-critical applications including e-business.

AIX 5L on IA-64 incorporates technologies from IBM's AIX, DYNIX/ptx and SCO
UnixWare. AIX 5L is designed to run on servers built around IBM's Power and
Intel's IA-64 and architectures. IBM, and other manufacturers, will offer
customers a choice of servers capable of running AIX 5L, allowing them to
take advantage of the industry's strongest UNIX operating system on both the
Power and IA-64 platform. With MERANT's Server Express on AIX 5L, customers
will be able to build new applications and migrate existing enterprise
applications to the proven POWER architecture or to the emerging IA-64
platform. "
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Whither" IBM's COBOLSet product(s)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-16T00:00:00

```
(You were probably joking, but just to clarify for others)

Simple,
  If you want TCP/IP from an OS/390 environment, then "THOU SHALT USE USS"
<G>

(Hence any COBOL application that uses TCP/IP or any of the other "growing
number" of OS/390 services requiring USS , *must* use USS).

Support for Linux on IBM mainframes is a "new" and rather limited feature
with more restrictions than facility.
```

actually, no.

> Simple,
>   If you want TCP/IP from an OS/390 environment, then "THOU SHALT USE USS"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> number" of OS/390 services requiring USS , *must* use USS).

my angle was Linux vs. USS -- i wasn't starting from OS/390.

> Support for Linux on IBM mainframes is a "new" and rather limited feature
> with more restrictions than facility.

Linux on S/390 is a "vanilla" implementation of Linux. i.e. as an operating
system, it's as "limited" as any other Linux.

i found this presentation useful:

http://www.vm.ibm.com/devpages/jelliott/pdfs/linux390.pdf
```

I've heard that there is a compiler which is very small. Does anyone
know more about this? I was interested in case they made
nanotechnology, and they needed something as easy to read as cobol,
but quick and small like assembler and C.




reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam

#### ↳ Re: tiny cobol

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

In article <na3kpso81cervtnhjh25t5pemabm74cj28@4ax.com>,
  gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com wrote:
> I've heard that there is a compiler which is very small. Does anyone
> know more about this? I was interested in case they made
> nanotechnology, and they needed something as easy to read as cobol,
> but quick and small like assembler and C.

Hi:

I use a minimalist approach. I use a subset of COBOL (and SP2)
which might be called COBOL 60. So, you can pretend you have tiny COBOL
even if your compiler can do hand-stands.

(One of my largest POS programs is 21,000 lines. The DLL is 219k.)

Thanks

Tony Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Tony:

I believe that Mr. Moore is literally referring to the Tiny COBOL
(a.k.a. GNU COBOL) project.  It is an "open source" project in which
anyone who wants to work on the compiler can obtain the source code
for the compiler (just like Linux).  If it is to be distributed in the
same manner as Linux, it will be a free  user-supported COBOL
compiler.

The Tiny COBOL compiler is currently only available for the Linux
environment.

Here is a link for the home page:

http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tiny cobol

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399D250C.3ABE1091@arvotek.net>`
- **References:** `<na3kpso81cervtnhjh25t5pemabm74cj28@4ax.com> <8ngk2g$hvm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <399c1869.676976@news.epix.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe <flexus@epix.net> wrote:
>  
> I believe that Mr. Moore is literally referring to the Tiny COBOL
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> environment.


The Tiny COBOL project (1) is not part of, and never was part of the GNU
COBOL project (2), also know as the COBOL2C project (3).

FYI, the GNU COBOL project is inactive, with any future development is
unlikely. 

However there is project called COBOL for GCC (4). It's objective is to
develop an ANSI/ISO COBOL 85 (20xx ?) compliant COBOL compiler, which
will be integrated with the GNU Compiler Collection (GCC).



David


1) The TinyCOBOL project 
   http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/
2) GNU COBOL Project
   http://www.gnu.org/software/cobol/
3) COBOL2C project
   http://www.lusars.net/gnu-cobol/
4) COBOL for GCC project
   http://CobolForGCC.sourceforge.net/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL DATA EDITING

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nfre8$aao$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8nf1gh$5l4$1@barcode.tesco.net> <399b58c3.953307@207.126.101.100>`

```
I hate to "disagree" with Thane, BUT ...

WFL is not what you want (for file editing).  What you want is (or at least
used to be) called "DFED" (Data File Editor).

WFL is used for "file conversions" (from one format to another) - not for
file editing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL DATA EDITING

- **From:** Albatross <mark_warren@uk2.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8nghfu$f4v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8nf1gh$5l4$1@barcode.tesco.net> <399b58c3.953307@207.126.101.100> <8nfre8$aao$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
And I hate (even more) to disagree with Bill ... WFL can be a useful
data creation/editing tool. Create a set of test data using your
favourite text editor (the source editor provided in Workbench, Net
Express and Mainframe Express would be a good choice), and then use WFL
to convert it to the required format (ISAM, relative or whatever). The
source editor makes it easy to create many records of similar data by
using cut/copy/paste. In Net Express and Mainframe Express, WFL is
replaced by the cunningly named "Data File Convertor" for doing
conversions on data files.

DFED of course is an excellent tool and GUI versions are provided with
Workbench, Net Ex and Mainframe Express (the latter products having the
nice Windows native look-and-feel. Mainframe Express V2.0 (with
AddPack) and the next release (look out for it soon) include a version
specifically for editing IMS databases - something you won't find
anywhere else.

```
Believe it or not, the following was a REAL question in the IBM-MAIN list.

to this NG for humor.

I have cut out the poster's name, but other than that this really was ALL the

```

#### ↳ Re: OT: (Humor) (was: $ cost for an I-O

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399c1cab.1767206@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<8ngn7v$ljm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8nguue$1rp$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Believe it or not, the following was a REAL question in the IBM-MAIN list.
>So far the only reply has been "polite" - but I thought I HAD to pass it on
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Bill:

Are they looking for the cost of both an "I" and an "O" or just one or
the other?  I believe that most "I-O shops" sell them as a package,
which means that you pay a premium when purchasing them separately.

You also have to take into consideration the condition of the "I-O."
For example, do they want to purchase new "I-O's" or are they willing
to accept used ones.  New ones generally cost more, but then they also
come with a warranty.  Used ones are cheaper unless you purchase
antique "I-O's" which can be very expensive (providing of course they
are in very good condition).

I prefer to purchase my "I-O's" at "I-O" auctions.  There is nothing
quite as exciting as a flurry of competitive bidding on a highly
desirable "I" or "O."

Be careful when buying "I-O's" outside of your home country and then
attempting to carry them onto a commercial flight.  If you attempt to
carry more than 10,000 I-O's through customs, they can be confiscated
in some countries.

I know that the VAT for I-O's in France are quite high because of the
high price/high quality of I-O's from the Bordeaux region.

Sorry.....I lost it again!

;-)


Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: (Humor) (was: $ cost for an I-O

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<399C236B.C5DDE659@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<8ngn7v$ljm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8nguue$1rp$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <399c1cab.1767206@news.epix.net>`

```
I find that I have fewer bugs when I create Cobol programs without I-O.  At least
fewer bugs that the users complain about.  But if the cost of I-O drops below 30
lira per G-I or 25 pesos per G-O, I will consider adding an I or an O,
respectively to a test program and then test to see if my budget has been blown.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: (Humor) (was: $ cost for an I-O

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>

<a id="t-a4abfdc458"></a>
## Thread: CEGPID and other LE V2R10 info — 3 msgs

### CEGPID and other LE V2R10 info

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-08-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ni21f$gf5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I just thought that given previous posts that referenced the CEEGPID LE
user-interface, that I would post something that I just noticed in the LE
V2R10 Migration Guide.  It states,

"Users migrating to V2R10 from a previous release of Language Environment
must be aware of the new Version_ID interface for CEEGPID. In previous
releases of Language Environment, the CEE_Version_ID of CEEGPID was a three
digit number, VRM, with one digit each for Version, Release, and Modification
level. In Release 10, the CEE_Version_ID for CEEGPID is a six digit number,
with two digits each for Version, Release, and Modification level. "

The other V2R10 issue that I thought MIGHT interest several readers is that
the "Vendor Interface" is now an UNLICENSED manual and is available on-line
at:

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEV1000/CCONTENTS
```
